question_html: In Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, find the percentage change in the radius of its orbit when an electron makes a transition from {tex}{n}=3{/tex} state to {tex}{n}=2{/tex} state.
question_text: In Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, find the percentage change in the radius of its orbit when an electron makes a transition from {tex}{n}=3{/tex} state to {tex}{n}=2{/tex} state.
explanation: {tex} {r} \alpha {n}^2 {/tex} {tex} \frac{{r}_2}{{r}_3}=\frac{4}{9} {/tex} Percentage change when electron makes transition from {tex} n=3 \text { to } n=2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\left|r_2-r_3\right|}{r_3} \times 100 {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{|4-9|}{9}\right) \times 100 {/tex} {tex} =55.55 \% {/tex}
solution: {tex} {r} \alpha {n}^2 {/tex} {tex} \frac{{r}_2}{{r}_3}=\frac{4}{9} {/tex} Percentage change when electron makes transition from {tex} n=3 \text { to } n=2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\left|r_2-r_3\right|}{r_3} \times 100 {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{|4-9|}{9}\right) \times 100 {/tex} {tex} =55.55 \% {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} {r} \alpha {n}^2 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{{r}_2}{{r}_3}=\frac{4}{9} {/tex}
Percentage change when electron makes transition from {tex} n=3 \text { to } n=2 {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\left|r_2-r_3\right|}{r_3} \times 100 {/tex}
{tex} =\left(\frac{|4-9|}{9}\right) \times 100 {/tex}
{tex} =55.55 \% {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Write the mathematical forms of three postulates of Bohr's theory of the hydrogen atom. Using them prove that, for an electron revolving in the {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit,

  1. the radius of the orbit is proportional to {tex}{n}^{2}{/tex}, and
  2. the total energy of the atom is proportional to {tex}\left(\frac{1}{{n}^{2}}\right){/tex}.
question_text: Write the mathematical forms of three postulates of Bohr's theory of the hydrogen atom. Using them prove that, for an electron revolving in the {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit, the radius of the orbit is proportional to {tex}{n}^{2}{/tex} , and the total energy of the atom is proportional to {tex}\left(\frac{1}{{n}^{2}}\right){/tex} .
explanation: Mathematical form of postulates of Bohr's Theory {tex} \text { } E_n=\frac{-13.6}{n^2} {eV} {/tex} {tex}{L}={mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}{h} v={E}_{{f}}-{E}_{{i}}{/tex} (a) {tex}\frac{{mv}^2}{{r}}=\frac{{Ze}^2}{{r}^2}{/tex} ...(1) {tex} {mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi} {/tex} ...(2) Solving (1) & (2) {tex} {r}={n}^2\left(\frac{{~h}}{2 \pi}\right)^2 \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{{me}^2} {/tex} ...(3) Since energy in the orbit {tex}E_n=\frac{-e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} Using eq (3) {tex}E_n=\frac{-m e^4}{8 n^2 \varepsilon_0{ }^2 h^2}{/tex} or {tex}{E}_{{n}} \propto \frac{1}{{n}^2}{/tex}
solution: Mathematical form of postulates of Bohr's Theory {tex} \text { } E_n=\frac{-13.6}{n^2} {eV} {/tex} {tex}{L}={mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}{h} v={E}_{{f}}-{E}_{{i}}{/tex} (a) {tex}\frac{{mv}^2}{{r}}=\frac{{Ze}^2}{{r}^2}{/tex} ...(1) {tex} {mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi} {/tex} ...(2) Solving (1) & (2) {tex} {r}={n}^2\left(\frac{{~h}}{2 \pi}\right)^2 \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{{me}^2} {/tex} ...(3) Since energy in the orbit {tex}E_n=\frac{-e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} Using eq (3) {tex}E_n=\frac{-m e^4}{8 n^2 \varepsilon_0{ }^2 h^2}{/tex} or {tex}{E}_{{n}} \propto \frac{1}{{n}^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Mathematical form of postulates of Bohr's Theory

  1. {tex} \text { } E_n=\frac{-13.6}{n^2} {eV} {/tex}
  2. {tex}{L}={mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex}
  3. {tex}{h} v={E}_{{f}}-{E}_{{i}}{/tex}

(a) {tex}\frac{{mv}^2}{{r}}=\frac{{Ze}^2}{{r}^2}{/tex} ...(1)
{tex} {mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi} {/tex} ...(2)
Solving (1) & (2)
{tex} {r}={n}^2\left(\frac{{~h}}{2 \pi}\right)^2 \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{{me}^2} {/tex} ...(3)
Since energy in the orbit {tex}E_n=\frac{-e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex}
Using eq (3) {tex}E_n=\frac{-m e^4}{8 n^2 \varepsilon_0{ }^2 h^2}{/tex}
or {tex}{E}_{{n}} \propto \frac{1}{{n}^2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: How is the necessary force provided to an electron to keep it moving in a circular orbit according to Bohr model of hydrogen atom? Derive an expression for the total energy of an electron moving in an orbit of radius r in hydrogen atom. Give the significance of negative sign in this expression.
question_text: How is the necessary force provided to an electron to keep it moving in a circular orbit according to Bohr model of hydrogen atom? Derive an expression for the total energy of an electron moving in an orbit of radius r in hydrogen atom. Give the significance of negative sign in this expression.
explanation: The electrostatic force of attraction between the electrons and the nucleus provides the necessary centripetal force required to an electron to revolve in the orbit. {tex}\frac{m {v}^{2}}{r}=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}{/tex} ...(1) ( {tex}{Z}=1{/tex} for hydrogen atom) Kinetic energy of the electron {tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m {v}^{2}{/tex} {tex}K=\frac{e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} (fromeq (1)) Potential energy of the electron {tex}U=\frac{-e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} \left(\because U=\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}\right){/tex} Total energy of the electron {tex}{E}={K}+{U}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{-e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} Negative sign signifies that electron is bound to the nucleus OR force is attractive.
solution: The electrostatic force of attraction between the electrons and the nucleus provides the necessary centripetal force required to an electron to revolve in the orbit. {tex}\frac{m {v}^{2}}{r}=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}{/tex} ...(1) ( {tex}{Z}=1{/tex} for hydrogen atom) Kinetic energy of the electron {tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m {v}^{2}{/tex} {tex}K=\frac{e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} (fromeq (1)) Potential energy of the electron {tex}U=\frac{-e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} \left(\because U=\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}\right){/tex} Total energy of the electron {tex}{E}={K}+{U}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{-e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} Negative sign signifies that electron is bound to the nucleus OR force is attractive.
solution_html:
The electrostatic force of attraction between the electrons and the nucleus provides the necessary centripetal force required to an electron to revolve in the orbit.
{tex}\frac{m {v}^{2}}{r}=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}{/tex} ...(1) ( {tex}{Z}=1{/tex} for hydrogen atom)
Kinetic energy of the electron
{tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m {v}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}K=\frac{e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} (fromeq (1))
Potential energy of the electron
{tex}U=\frac{-e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} \left(\because U=\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}\right){/tex}
Total energy of the electron
{tex}{E}={K}+{U}{/tex}
{tex}E=\frac{-e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex}
Negative sign signifies that electron is bound to the nucleus
OR force is attractive.

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the ratio of minimum to maximum wavelength of radiations emitted when electron jumps from higher energy state into ground state of hydrogen atom.
question_text: Find the ratio of minimum to maximum wavelength of radiations emitted when electron jumps from higher energy state into ground state of hydrogen atom.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}={R}\left[\frac{1}{{n}_{{f}}^{2}}-\frac{1}{{n}_{{i}}^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {max }}}={R}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\text {max }}=\frac{4}{3 {R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {min }}}={R}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\text {min }}=\frac{1}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\left\lvert\, \frac{\lambda_{\text {min }}}{\lambda_{\text {max }}}=\frac{3}{4}\right.{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}={R}\left[\frac{1}{{n}_{{f}}^{2}}-\frac{1}{{n}_{{i}}^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {max }}}={R}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\text {max }}=\frac{4}{3 {R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {min }}}={R}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\text {min }}=\frac{1}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\left\lvert\, \frac{\lambda_{\text {min }}}{\lambda_{\text {max }}}=\frac{3}{4}\right.{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}={R}\left[\frac{1}{{n}_{{f}}^{2}}-\frac{1}{{n}_{{i}}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {max }}}={R}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{\text {max }}=\frac{4}{3 {R}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {min }}}={R}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{\text {min }}=\frac{1}{{R}}{/tex}
{tex}\left\lvert\, \frac{\lambda_{\text {min }}}{\lambda_{\text {max }}}=\frac{3}{4}\right.{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: An electron in Bohr model of hydrogen atom makes a transition from energy level -1.51 eV to -3.40 eV. Calculate the change in the radius of its orbit. The radius of orbit of electron in its ground state is {tex}0.53 \overset o A{/tex}.
question_text: An electron in Bohr model of hydrogen atom makes a transition from energy level -1.51 eV to -3.40 eV. Calculate the change in the radius of its orbit. The radius of orbit of electron in its ground state is {tex}0.53 \overset o A{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}E_{n}=\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}} {eV}{/tex} For {tex}E_{n}=-1.51 {eV}{/tex} {tex}-1.51=\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}}{/tex} {tex}{n}=3{/tex} For {tex}E_{n}=-3.40 {eV}{/tex} {tex}-3.40=\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}}{/tex} {tex}{n}=2{/tex} {tex}\because r=0.53 n^{2} \overset o A{/tex} {tex}\therefore \text { change in radius }{/tex} {tex}\Delta {r} =0.53\left[3^{2}-2^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=0.53 \times 5{/tex} {tex}=2.65 \overset oA{/tex}
solution: {tex}E_{n}=\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}} {eV}{/tex} For {tex}E_{n}=-1.51 {eV}{/tex} {tex}-1.51=\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}}{/tex} {tex}{n}=3{/tex} For {tex}E_{n}=-3.40 {eV}{/tex} {tex}-3.40=\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}}{/tex} {tex}{n}=2{/tex} {tex}\because r=0.53 n^{2} \overset o A{/tex} {tex}\therefore \text { change in radius }{/tex} {tex}\Delta {r} =0.53\left[3^{2}-2^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=0.53 \times 5{/tex} {tex}=2.65 \overset oA{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}E_{n}=\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}} {eV}{/tex}
For {tex}E_{n}=-1.51 {eV}{/tex}
{tex}-1.51=\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}{n}=3{/tex}
For {tex}E_{n}=-3.40 {eV}{/tex}
{tex}-3.40=\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}{n}=2{/tex}
{tex}\because r=0.53 n^{2} \overset o A{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \text { change in radius }{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {r} =0.53\left[3^{2}-2^{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=0.53 \times 5{/tex}
{tex}=2.65 \overset oA{/tex}

question_html: The energy of an electron in an orbit of Bohr hydrogen atom is -3.4 eV. Find its angular momentum.
question_text: The energy of an electron in an orbit of Bohr hydrogen atom is -3.4 eV. Find its angular momentum.
explanation: {tex}E_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} {eV}{/tex} {tex}{n}^{2}=\frac{-13.6}{-3.4}=4{/tex} {tex}{n}=2{/tex} Angular momentum {tex}{L}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}{~L}=\frac{h}{\pi}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{3.14}=2.11 \times 10^{-34} {Js}{/tex}
solution: {tex}E_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} {eV}{/tex} {tex}{n}^{2}=\frac{-13.6}{-3.4}=4{/tex} {tex}{n}=2{/tex} Angular momentum {tex}{L}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}{~L}=\frac{h}{\pi}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{3.14}=2.11 \times 10^{-34} {Js}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}E_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} {eV}{/tex}
{tex}{n}^{2}=\frac{-13.6}{-3.4}=4{/tex}
{tex}{n}=2{/tex}
Angular momentum
{tex}{L}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}{~L}=\frac{h}{\pi}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{3.14}=2.11 \times 10^{-34} {Js}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Prove that, in Bohr model of hydrogen atom, the time period of revolution of an electron in {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit is proportional to {tex}{n}^{3}{/tex}.
question_text: Prove that, in Bohr model of hydrogen atom, the time period of revolution of an electron in {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit is proportional to {tex}{n}^{3}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}{T}=\frac{2 \pi {r}}{{v}}{/tex} ...(1) From Bohr's quantization condition {tex}{mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}{v}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi {mr}}{/tex} ...(2) From (1) and (2) {tex}{T} =\frac{2 \pi {r}}{({nh} / 2 \pi {mr})}{/tex} {tex}{T} =\frac{2 \pi {r}(2 \pi {mr})}{{nh}}{/tex} {tex}{~T} =\frac{4 \pi^{2} {mr}^{2}}{{nh}}{/tex} From {tex}r=\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k e^{2}}{/tex} {tex}T=\frac{4 \pi^{2} m}{n h}\left(\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k e^{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}{T}=\frac{{n}^{3} {~h}^{3}}{4 \pi^{2} {mk}^{2} {e}^{4}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {T} \ \alpha \ {n}^{3}{/tex}
solution: {tex}{T}=\frac{2 \pi {r}}{{v}}{/tex} ...(1) From Bohr's quantization condition {tex}{mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}{v}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi {mr}}{/tex} ...(2) From (1) and (2) {tex}{T} =\frac{2 \pi {r}}{({nh} / 2 \pi {mr})}{/tex} {tex}{T} =\frac{2 \pi {r}(2 \pi {mr})}{{nh}}{/tex} {tex}{~T} =\frac{4 \pi^{2} {mr}^{2}}{{nh}}{/tex} From {tex}r=\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k e^{2}}{/tex} {tex}T=\frac{4 \pi^{2} m}{n h}\left(\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k e^{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}{T}=\frac{{n}^{3} {~h}^{3}}{4 \pi^{2} {mk}^{2} {e}^{4}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {T} \ \alpha \ {n}^{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{T}=\frac{2 \pi {r}}{{v}}{/tex} ...(1)
From Bohr's quantization condition
{tex}{mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}{v}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi {mr}}{/tex} ...(2)
From (1) and (2)
{tex}{T} =\frac{2 \pi {r}}{({nh} / 2 \pi {mr})}{/tex}
{tex}{T} =\frac{2 \pi {r}(2 \pi {mr})}{{nh}}{/tex}
{tex}{~T} =\frac{4 \pi^{2} {mr}^{2}}{{nh}}{/tex}
From {tex}r=\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k e^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}T=\frac{4 \pi^{2} m}{n h}\left(\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k e^{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}{T}=\frac{{n}^{3} {~h}^{3}}{4 \pi^{2} {mk}^{2} {e}^{4}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {T} \ \alpha \ {n}^{3}{/tex}

question_html: Write the three postulates of Bohr model of hydrogen atom. Show that, in this model, the frequency of revolution of an electron in its {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit is proportional to {tex}\left(\frac{1}{{n}^{3}}\right){/tex}.
question_text: Write the three postulates of Bohr model of hydrogen atom. Show that, in this model, the frequency of revolution of an electron in its {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit is proportional to {tex}\left(\frac{1}{{n}^{3}}\right){/tex} .
explanation: Bohr's Postulates An electron in an atom could revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy. Electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is some integral multiple of {tex}h / 2 \pi{/tex} . An electron might make a transition from one of its specified non-radiating orbits to another of lower energy. When it does so, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states. The frequency of the emitted photon is then given by {tex} h v=E_i-E_f {/tex} As frequency of revolution of electron is given by {tex} v=\frac{{v}}{2 \pi r} {/tex} ...(1) In Bohr's model the velocity of the electron in the nth orbit is {tex} v_{{n}}=\frac{e^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 n h} {/tex} {tex} v_{{n}} \propto \frac{1}{n} \ldots (2){/tex} and radius of the electron in the {tex}n{/tex} th orbit is {tex} r_n=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2} {/tex} {tex} r_n \propto n^2 {/tex} ...(3) Using (3) and (2) in (1) {tex} v \alpha \frac{1}{n^3} {/tex}
solution: Bohr's Postulates An electron in an atom could revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy. Electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is some integral multiple of {tex}h / 2 \pi{/tex} . An electron might make a transition from one of its specified non-radiating orbits to another of lower energy. When it does so, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states. The frequency of the emitted photon is then given by {tex} h v=E_i-E_f {/tex} As frequency of revolution of electron is given by {tex} v=\frac{{v}}{2 \pi r} {/tex} ...(1) In Bohr's model the velocity of the electron in the nth orbit is {tex} v_{{n}}=\frac{e^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 n h} {/tex} {tex} v_{{n}} \propto \frac{1}{n} \ldots (2){/tex} and radius of the electron in the {tex}n{/tex} th orbit is {tex} r_n=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2} {/tex} {tex} r_n \propto n^2 {/tex} ...(3) Using (3) and (2) in (1) {tex} v \alpha \frac{1}{n^3} {/tex}
solution_html:
Bohr's Postulates

  1. An electron in an atom could revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy.
  2. Electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is some integral multiple of {tex}h / 2 \pi{/tex}.
  3. An electron might make a transition from one of its specified non-radiating orbits to another of lower energy. When it does so, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states. The frequency of the emitted photon is then given by
    {tex} h v=E_i-E_f {/tex}

As frequency of revolution of electron is given by
{tex} v=\frac{{v}}{2 \pi r} {/tex} ...(1)
In Bohr's model the velocity of the electron in the nth orbit is
{tex} v_{{n}}=\frac{e^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 n h} {/tex}
{tex} v_{{n}} \propto \frac{1}{n} \ldots (2){/tex}
and radius of the electron in the {tex}n{/tex}th orbit is
{tex} r_n=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2} {/tex}
{tex} r_n \propto n^2 {/tex} ...(3)
Using (3) and (2) in (1)
{tex} v \alpha \frac{1}{n^3} {/tex}

question_html:
In Bohr model of hydrogen atom, an electron is revolving in second orbit. Find the value of:

  1. angular momentum of electron,
  2. radius of the orbit, and
  3. kinetic energy of electron.

Take radius of first orbit of hydrogen atom as {tex}0.5 \overset oA{/tex}.
question_text: In Bohr model of hydrogen atom, an electron is revolving in second orbit. Find the value of: angular momentum of electron, radius of the orbit, and kinetic energy of electron. Take radius of first orbit of hydrogen atom as {tex}0.5 \overset oA{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} for {tex}n=2{/tex} {tex}L=\frac{2 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 3.14}{/tex} {tex}=2.11 \times 10^{-34} {kg} \ {m}^{2} {s}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}r_{n}=n^{2} r_{0}{/tex} {tex}r_{2}=4(0.5 \overset oA){/tex} {tex}r_{2}=2 \overset 0A{/tex} Total energy of electron {tex}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} {eV}{/tex} {tex}E=-3.4 {eV} (n=2){/tex} {tex}K=-E{/tex} {tex}K=3.4 {eV}{/tex}
solution: {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} for {tex}n=2{/tex} {tex}L=\frac{2 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 3.14}{/tex} {tex}=2.11 \times 10^{-34} {kg} \ {m}^{2} {s}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}r_{n}=n^{2} r_{0}{/tex} {tex}r_{2}=4(0.5 \overset oA){/tex} {tex}r_{2}=2 \overset 0A{/tex} Total energy of electron {tex}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} {eV}{/tex} {tex}E=-3.4 {eV} (n=2){/tex} {tex}K=-E{/tex} {tex}K=3.4 {eV}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    for {tex}n=2{/tex}
    {tex}L=\frac{2 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 3.14}{/tex}
    {tex}=2.11 \times 10^{-34} {kg} \ {m}^{2} {s}^{-1}{/tex}
  2. {tex}r_{n}=n^{2} r_{0}{/tex}
    {tex}r_{2}=4(0.5 \overset oA){/tex}
    {tex}r_{2}=2 \overset 0A{/tex}
  3. Total energy of electron {tex}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} {eV}{/tex}
    {tex}E=-3.4 {eV} (n=2){/tex}
    {tex}K=-E{/tex}
    {tex}K=3.4 {eV}{/tex}

question_html: An alpha particle and a deuterium ion are accelerated through the same potential difference. These are then directed towards a target nucleus to make head-on collision. It is observed that their distance of closest approach is the same. Justify it theoretically.
question_text: An alpha particle and a deuterium ion are accelerated through the same potential difference. These are then directed towards a target nucleus to make head-on collision. It is observed that their distance of closest approach is the same. Justify it theoretically.
explanation: For a given nucleus, the distance of closest approach for a charged particle depends only on the accelerating potential difference. Since both {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle and a deuterium ion are accelerated through same potential difference, therefore distance of closest approach will be same for both.

question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
A hydrogen atom consists of an electron revolving in a circular orbit of radius {tex}r{/tex} with certain velocity {tex}v{/tex} around a proton located at the nucleus of the atom. The electrostatic force of attraction between the revolving electron and the proton provides the requisite centripetal force to keep it in the orbit. According to Bohr's model, an electron can revolve only in certain stable orbits. The angular momentum of the electron in these orbits is some integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}, where {tex}h{/tex} is the Planck's constant. Further, when an electron makes a transition from one orbit of higher energy to that of lower energy, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the difference between energies of the initial and final states. Assuming the mass and charge of an electron as {tex}m{/tex} and e respectively, answer the following questions.

  1. The expression for the speed of electron v in terms of radius of the orbit ({tex}r{/tex}) and physical constant ({tex}K=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{/tex}) is:
    1. {tex}\frac{{Ke}^2}{{mr}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{{Ke}^2}{{mr}^2}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\sqrt{\frac{{Ke}^2}{{mr}}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\sqrt{\frac{{Ke}^2}{{mr}^2}}{/tex}
  2. The total energy of the atom in terms of r and physical constant K is:
    1. {tex}\frac{{Ke}^2}{{r}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}-\frac{{Ke}^2}{2 {r}}{/tex}
    3. {tex} \frac{{Ke}^2}{2 {r}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\frac{3}{2} \frac{{Ke}^2}{{r}}{/tex}
  3. A photon of wavelength 500 nm is emitted when an electron makes a transition from one state to the other state in an atom. The change in the total energy of the electron and change in its kinetic energy in eV as per Bohr's model, respectively will be:
    1. {tex}2.48,-2.48{/tex}
    2. {tex}-1.24,1.24{/tex}
    3. {tex}-2 \cdot 48,2 \cdot 48{/tex}
    4. {tex}1 \cdot 24,-1 \cdot 24{/tex}
    1. In Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, the frequency of revolution of electron in its {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit is proportional to:
      1. n
      2. {tex}\frac{1}{n}{/tex}
      3. {tex}\frac{1}{{n}^2}{/tex}
      4. {tex}\frac{1}{{n}^3}{/tex}

        OR

    2. An electron makes a transition from -3.4 eV state to the ground state in hydrogen atom. Its radius of orbit changes by: (radius of orbit of electron in ground state {tex}=0.53 \overset o A{/tex})
      1. {tex}0.53 \overset o A{/tex}
      2. {tex}1.06 \overset o A{/tex}
      3. {tex}1.59 \overset o A{/tex}
      4. {tex}2 \cdot 12 \overset o A{/tex}

question_html: Define the 'distance of closest approach'. An {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle of kinetic energy K is bombarded on a thin gold foil. Derive an expression for the 'distance of closest approach'.
question_text: Define the 'distance of closest approach'. An {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle of kinetic energy K is bombarded on a thin gold foil. Derive an expression for the 'distance of closest approach'.
explanation: The distance between the {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle and the target nucleus when whole kinetic energy of an {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle gets converted into potential energy. At distance of closest approach {tex} {K}={U} {/tex} {tex} {~K}=\frac{(2 e)(Z e)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 d} {/tex} {tex} \therefore d=\frac{2 Z e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 {~K}} {/tex}
solution: The distance between the {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle and the target nucleus when whole kinetic energy of an {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle gets converted into potential energy. At distance of closest approach {tex} {K}={U} {/tex} {tex} {~K}=\frac{(2 e)(Z e)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 d} {/tex} {tex} \therefore d=\frac{2 Z e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 {~K}} {/tex}
solution_html:
The distance between the {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle and the target nucleus when whole kinetic energy of an {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle gets converted into potential energy.
At distance of closest approach
{tex} {K}={U} {/tex}
{tex} {~K}=\frac{(2 e)(Z e)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 d} {/tex}
{tex} \therefore d=\frac{2 Z e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 {~K}} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Write the drawbacks of Rutherford's atomic model. How did Bohr remove them? Show that different orbits in Bohr's atom are not equally spaced.
question_text: Write the drawbacks of Rutherford's atomic model. How did Bohr remove them? Show that different orbits in Bohr's atom are not equally spaced.
explanation: Drawbacks of Rutherford's Model: Instability: Electrons should spiral into the nucleus due to radiative energy loss, making the atom unstable. Spectral Lines: It couldn't explain the discrete spectral lines of atoms, predicting a continuous spectrum instead. Electron Stability: It did not account for the observed stability of electrons in specific orbits. Bohr's Improvements: Quantized Orbits: Bohr proposed that electrons occupy stable, quantized orbits without radiating energy, resolving instability. Energy Levels: He introduced discrete energy levels, explaining spectral lines as transitions between these levels. Non-Equidistant Orbits: The radius of the {tex}n{/tex} -th orbit in Bohr's model is {tex}r_n \propto{/tex} {tex}n^2{/tex} , so the orbits are not equally spaced; spacing increases with {tex}n{/tex} .
solution: Drawbacks of Rutherford's Model: Instability: Electrons should spiral into the nucleus due to radiative energy loss, making the atom unstable. Spectral Lines: It couldn't explain the discrete spectral lines of atoms, predicting a continuous spectrum instead. Electron Stability: It did not account for the observed stability of electrons in specific orbits. Bohr's Improvements: Quantized Orbits: Bohr proposed that electrons occupy stable, quantized orbits without radiating energy, resolving instability. Energy Levels: He introduced discrete energy levels, explaining spectral lines as transitions between these levels. Non-Equidistant Orbits: The radius of the {tex}n{/tex} -th orbit in Bohr's model is {tex}r_n \propto{/tex} {tex}n^2{/tex} , so the orbits are not equally spaced; spacing increases with {tex}n{/tex} .
solution_html:
Drawbacks of Rutherford's Model:

  1. Instability: Electrons should spiral into the nucleus due to radiative energy loss, making the atom unstable.
  2. Spectral Lines: It couldn't explain the discrete spectral lines of atoms, predicting a continuous spectrum instead.
  3. Electron Stability: It did not account for the observed stability of electrons in specific orbits.

Bohr's Improvements:

  1. Quantized Orbits: Bohr proposed that electrons occupy stable, quantized orbits without radiating energy, resolving instability.
  2. Energy Levels: He introduced discrete energy levels, explaining spectral lines as transitions between these levels.
  3. Non-Equidistant Orbits: The radius of the {tex}n{/tex}-th orbit in Bohr's model is {tex}r_n \propto{/tex} {tex}n^2{/tex}, so the orbits are not equally spaced; spacing increases with {tex}n{/tex}.

question_html: Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the radius of the nth orbit of an electron in a hydrogen atom. Also find the numerical value of Bohr's radius a0.
question_text: Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the radius of the n th orbit of an electron in a hydrogen atom. Also find the numerical value of Bohr's radius a 0 .
explanation: From Bohr's second postulate {tex}m {v} r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(1) Also {tex}\frac{m {v}^2}{r}=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2} ({z}=1){/tex} {tex} {v}=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m r}}{/tex} Substituting in (1) and simplifying {tex} r=\frac{n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{\pi m e^2}{/tex} For {tex}{n}=1 {r}={a}_{{o}} {/tex} (Bohr's radius) {tex} a_o =\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34}\right)^2 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{3.14 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2} {/tex} {tex} =5.29 \times 10^{-11} {~m} {/tex} {tex}\overset{\mathrm o}{\mathrm A}{/tex}
solution: From Bohr's second postulate {tex}m {v} r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(1) Also {tex}\frac{m {v}^2}{r}=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2} ({z}=1){/tex} {tex} {v}=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m r}}{/tex} Substituting in (1) and simplifying {tex} r=\frac{n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{\pi m e^2}{/tex} For {tex}{n}=1 {r}={a}_{{o}} {/tex} (Bohr's radius) {tex} a_o =\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34}\right)^2 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{3.14 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2} {/tex} {tex} =5.29 \times 10^{-11} {~m} {/tex} {tex}\overset{\mathrm o}{\mathrm A}{/tex}
solution_html:
From Bohr's second postulate
{tex}m {v} r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(1)
Also {tex}\frac{m {v}^2}{r}=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2} ({z}=1){/tex}
{tex} {v}=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m r}}{/tex}
Substituting in (1) and simplifying
{tex} r=\frac{n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{\pi m e^2}{/tex}
For {tex}{n}=1 {r}={a}_{{o}} {/tex} (Bohr's radius)
{tex} a_o =\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34}\right)^2 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{3.14 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2} {/tex}
{tex} =5.29 \times 10^{-11} {~m} {/tex}
{tex}\overset{\mathrm o}{\mathrm A}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
State the three postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom.
A hydrogen atom de-excites from level {tex}n{/tex} to level ( {tex}{n}-1{/tex} ). Show that, according to Bohr's theory, the frequency of radiation emitted {tex}v \cong \frac{\alpha}{{n}^3}{/tex}, for large values of {tex}n{/tex}, where {tex}\alpha{/tex} is a constant. This result exactly agrees with that obtained from classical physics - one of the successes of Bohr's theory.
question_text: State the three postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom. A hydrogen atom de-excites from level {tex}n{/tex} to level ( {tex}{n}-1{/tex} ). Show that, according to Bohr's theory, the frequency of radiation emitted {tex}v \cong \frac{\alpha}{{n}^3}{/tex} , for large values of {tex}n{/tex} , where {tex}\alpha{/tex} is a constant. This result exactly agrees with that obtained from classical physics - one of the successes of Bohr's theory.
explanation: Bohr’s postulates:- An electron in an atom could revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy. The electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is some integral multiple of h/2 {tex}\pi{/tex} An electron might make a transition from one of its specified nonradiating orbits to another of lower energy. When it does so, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states. {tex} {h} \nu={E}_{{i}}-{E}_{{f}} {/tex} ...(i) {tex} {E}_{{n}} \propto \frac{1}{n^2} {/tex} {tex} E_n-E_{n-1}=k\left[\frac{1}{(n-1)^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right] {/tex} {tex} = k \frac{n^2-\left[n^2+1-2 n\right]}{n^4-2 n^3+n^2} {/tex} {tex} =k \frac{2 n-1}{n^4-2 n^3+n^2} {/tex} For large {tex}{n}, 2 {n}-1 \cong 2 {n}{/tex} {tex} {n}^2\left[{n}^2-2 {n}+1\right] \cong {n}^4{/tex} {tex} E_n-E_{n-1} \cong k \frac{2 n}{n^4} \cong \frac{2 k}{n^3}{/tex} ...(ii) From equation (i) and (ii) {tex} v \cong \frac{\alpha}{n^3}{/tex}
solution: Bohr’s postulates:- An electron in an atom could revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy. The electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is some integral multiple of h/2 {tex}\pi{/tex} An electron might make a transition from one of its specified nonradiating orbits to another of lower energy. When it does so, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states. {tex} {h} \nu={E}_{{i}}-{E}_{{f}} {/tex} ...(i) {tex} {E}_{{n}} \propto \frac{1}{n^2} {/tex} {tex} E_n-E_{n-1}=k\left[\frac{1}{(n-1)^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right] {/tex} {tex} = k \frac{n^2-\left[n^2+1-2 n\right]}{n^4-2 n^3+n^2} {/tex} {tex} =k \frac{2 n-1}{n^4-2 n^3+n^2} {/tex} For large {tex}{n}, 2 {n}-1 \cong 2 {n}{/tex} {tex} {n}^2\left[{n}^2-2 {n}+1\right] \cong {n}^4{/tex} {tex} E_n-E_{n-1} \cong k \frac{2 n}{n^4} \cong \frac{2 k}{n^3}{/tex} ...(ii) From equation (i) and (ii) {tex} v \cong \frac{\alpha}{n^3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Bohr’s postulates:-

  1. An electron in an atom could revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy.
  2. The electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is some integral multiple of h/2{tex}\pi{/tex}
  3. An electron might make a transition from one of its specified nonradiating orbits to another of lower energy. When it does so, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states.
    {tex} {h} \nu={E}_{{i}}-{E}_{{f}} {/tex} ...(i)
    {tex} {E}_{{n}} \propto \frac{1}{n^2} {/tex}
    {tex} E_n-E_{n-1}=k\left[\frac{1}{(n-1)^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right] {/tex}
    {tex} = k \frac{n^2-\left[n^2+1-2 n\right]}{n^4-2 n^3+n^2} {/tex}
    {tex} =k \frac{2 n-1}{n^4-2 n^3+n^2} {/tex}
    For large {tex}{n}, 2 {n}-1 \cong 2 {n}{/tex}
    {tex} {n}^2\left[{n}^2-2 {n}+1\right] \cong {n}^4{/tex}
    {tex} E_n-E_{n-1} \cong k \frac{2 n}{n^4} \cong \frac{2 k}{n^3}{/tex} ...(ii)
    From equation (i) and (ii)
    {tex} v \cong \frac{\alpha}{n^3}{/tex}

question_html: State Bohr's first and second postulates. Use them to derive an expression for the radius of the nth orbit in a hydrogen atom.
question_text: State Bohr's first and second postulates. Use them to derive an expression for the radius of the nth orbit in a hydrogen atom.
explanation: - Bohr's first postulate An electron in an atom revolves in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy. - Bohr's second postulate Electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} . Electrostatic force between revolving electron and nucleus provides requisite centripetal force {tex}\frac{m v_n^2}{r_n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r_n^2}{/tex} {tex} v_n=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m r_n}} {/tex} {tex} m v_n r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} {/tex} using equations (i) and (ii) {tex}r_n=\left(\frac{n^2}{m}\right)\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi}\right)^2 \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{e^2}{/tex}
solution: - Bohr's first postulate An electron in an atom revolves in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy. - Bohr's second postulate Electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} . Electrostatic force between revolving electron and nucleus provides requisite centripetal force {tex}\frac{m v_n^2}{r_n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r_n^2}{/tex} {tex} v_n=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m r_n}} {/tex} {tex} m v_n r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} {/tex} using equations (i) and (ii) {tex}r_n=\left(\frac{n^2}{m}\right)\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi}\right)^2 \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{e^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
- Bohr's first postulate
An electron in an atom revolves in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy.
- Bohr's second postulate Electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}.
Electrostatic force between revolving electron and nucleus provides requisite centripetal force
{tex}\frac{m v_n^2}{r_n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r_n^2}{/tex}
{tex} v_n=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m r_n}} {/tex}
{tex} m v_n r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} {/tex}
using equations (i) and (ii)
{tex}r_n=\left(\frac{n^2}{m}\right)\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi}\right)^2 \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{e^2}{/tex}

question_html:
1. State Bohr's postulates and derive an expression for the energy of electron in {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit in Bohr's model of hydrogen atom.
  2. Calculate binding energy per nucleon (in MeV) of {tex}{ }_{6}^{12} {C}{/tex}.
    Given:
    {tex}{m}\left({ }_{6}^{12} {C}\right)=12.000000 {u}{/tex}
    {tex}{~m}\binom{1}{0^{{n}}}=1.008665 {u}{/tex}
    {tex}{~m}\left({ }_{1}^{1} {H}\right)=1.007825 {u}{/tex}
question_text: State Bohr's postulates and derive an expression for the energy of electron in {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit in Bohr's model of hydrogen atom. Calculate binding energy per nucleon (in MeV) of {tex}{ }_{6}^{12} {C}{/tex} . Given: {tex}{m}\left({ }_{6}^{12} {C}\right)=12.000000 {u}{/tex} {tex}{~m}\binom{1}{0^{{n}}}=1.008665 {u}{/tex} {tex}{~m}\left({ }_{1}^{1} {H}\right)=1.007825 {u}{/tex}
explanation: Bohr's Postulates Bohr's first postulate was that an electron in an atom could revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy, Bohr's second postulate states that the electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is some integral multiple of {tex}{h} / 2 \pi{/tex} where h is the Planck's constant. Bohr's third postulate states that an electron might make a transition from one of its specified non-radiating orbits to another of lower energy. When it does so, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states. Derivation Total energy of electron in the stationary state of hydrogen atoms is {tex}{E}=-\frac{{e}^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}_{{n}}}{/tex} ...(1) Substituting eq (2) in eq (1) {tex}{E}_{{n}}=-\frac{{me}^{4}}{8 {n}^{2} \varepsilon_{0}^{2} {~h}^{2}}{/tex} ...(2) Where {tex}{r}_{{n}}{/tex} is radius of {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit {tex}{r}_{{n}}=\frac{{n}^{2} {~h}^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}{\pi {me}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\text { Mass defect, }{/tex} {tex}\Delta {m} =\left[6 {~m}\left({ }_{0}^{1} {n}\right)+6 {~m}\left({ }_{1}^{1} {H}\right)\right]-{m}\left({ }_{6}^{12} {C}\right){/tex} {tex}\Delta {m} =(6 \times 1.008665+6 \times 1.007825){/tex} {tex}- 12.000000{/tex} {tex}\Delta {~m} =0.09894 {u}{/tex} {tex}\text { B.E. } =\Delta {m} \times 931.5 {MeV}{/tex} {tex}=92.16 {MeV}{/tex} Binding energy per nucleon, {tex}E_{b n}=\frac{E_{b}}{A}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{92.16}{12}{/tex} = 7.68MeV
solution: Bohr's Postulates Bohr's first postulate was that an electron in an atom could revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy, Bohr's second postulate states that the electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is some integral multiple of {tex}{h} / 2 \pi{/tex} where h is the Planck's constant. Bohr's third postulate states that an electron might make a transition from one of its specified non-radiating orbits to another of lower energy. When it does so, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states. Derivation Total energy of electron in the stationary state of hydrogen atoms is {tex}{E}=-\frac{{e}^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}_{{n}}}{/tex} ...(1) Substituting eq (2) in eq (1) {tex}{E}_{{n}}=-\frac{{me}^{4}}{8 {n}^{2} \varepsilon_{0}^{2} {~h}^{2}}{/tex} ...(2) Where {tex}{r}_{{n}}{/tex} is radius of {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit {tex}{r}_{{n}}=\frac{{n}^{2} {~h}^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}{\pi {me}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\text { Mass defect, }{/tex} {tex}\Delta {m} =\left[6 {~m}\left({ }_{0}^{1} {n}\right)+6 {~m}\left({ }_{1}^{1} {H}\right)\right]-{m}\left({ }_{6}^{12} {C}\right){/tex} {tex}\Delta {m} =(6 \times 1.008665+6 \times 1.007825){/tex} {tex}- 12.000000{/tex} {tex}\Delta {~m} =0.09894 {u}{/tex} {tex}\text { B.E. } =\Delta {m} \times 931.5 {MeV}{/tex} {tex}=92.16 {MeV}{/tex} Binding energy per nucleon, {tex}E_{b n}=\frac{E_{b}}{A}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{92.16}{12}{/tex} = 7.68MeV
solution_html:
1. Bohr's Postulates
    1. Bohr's first postulate was that an electron in an atom could revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy,
    2. Bohr's second postulate states that the electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is some integral multiple of {tex}{h} / 2 \pi{/tex} where h is the Planck's constant.
    3. Bohr's third postulate states that an electron might make a transition from one of its specified non-radiating orbits to another of lower energy. When it does so, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states.
      Derivation
      Total energy of electron in the stationary state of hydrogen atoms is
      {tex}{E}=-\frac{{e}^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} {r}_{{n}}}{/tex} ...(1)
      Substituting eq (2) in eq (1)
      {tex}{E}_{{n}}=-\frac{{me}^{4}}{8 {n}^{2} \varepsilon_{0}^{2} {~h}^{2}}{/tex} ...(2)
      Where {tex}{r}_{{n}}{/tex} is radius of {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit
      {tex}{r}_{{n}}=\frac{{n}^{2} {~h}^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}{\pi {me}^{2}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\text { Mass defect, }{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta {m} =\left[6 {~m}\left({ }_{0}^{1} {n}\right)+6 {~m}\left({ }_{1}^{1} {H}\right)\right]-{m}\left({ }_{6}^{12} {C}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Delta {m} =(6 \times 1.008665+6 \times 1.007825){/tex} {tex}- 12.000000{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta {~m} =0.09894 {u}{/tex}
    {tex}\text { B.E. } =\Delta {m} \times 931.5 {MeV}{/tex}
    {tex}=92.16 {MeV}{/tex}
    Binding energy per nucleon, {tex}E_{b n}=\frac{E_{b}}{A}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{92.16}{12}{/tex} = 7.68MeV

question_html: Briefly explain Geiger-Marsden experiment. Show the variation of the number of particles scattered (N) with scattering angle ({tex}\theta{/tex}) in this experiment. What is the main conclusion that can be inferred from this plot?
question_text: Briefly explain Geiger-Marsden experiment. Show the variation of the number of particles scattered (N) with scattering angle ( {tex}\theta{/tex} ) in this experiment. What is the main conclusion that can be inferred from this plot?
explanation: The alpha-particles emitted by a radioactive source were allowed to fall on a thin foil of gold. The scattered alpha-particles were observed through a rotatable detector consisting of zinc sulphide screen and a microscope. The scattered alpha-particles on striking the screen produced brief light flashes. Conclusion: The existence of positively charged nucleus inside an atom and provide an upper limit to the size of the nucleus.

Conclusion: The existence of positively charged nucleus inside an atom and provide an upper limit to the size of the nucleus.

  2. The wavelength of the second line of the Balmer series in the hydrogen spectrum is 4861 {tex}\overset o{A}{/tex}. Calculate the wavelength of the first line of the same series.
question_text: Write the limitations of Rutherford's model of atom. The wavelength of the second line of the Balmer series in the hydrogen spectrum is 4861 {tex}\overset o{A}{/tex} . Calculate the wavelength of the first line of the same series.
explanation: The stability of atom was not explain by Rutherford Model. Rutherford Model could not explain the existence of the line spectrum. {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_i^2}-\frac{1}{n_f^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{16}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\frac{5 \mathrm{R}}{36}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{36}{5 R} \frac{3 R}{16}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_1=\frac{36 \times 3}{5 \times 16} \times{/tex} 4861 {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = 6562.35 {tex}\overset o{A}{/tex}
solution: The stability of atom was not explain by Rutherford Model. Rutherford Model could not explain the existence of the line spectrum. {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_i^2}-\frac{1}{n_f^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{16}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\frac{5 \mathrm{R}}{36}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{36}{5 R} \frac{3 R}{16}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_1=\frac{36 \times 3}{5 \times 16} \times{/tex} 4861 {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = 6562.35 {tex}\overset o{A}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The stability of atom was not explain by Rutherford Model.
    Rutherford Model could not explain the existence of the line spectrum.
  2. {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_i^2}-\frac{1}{n_f^2}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{16}{/tex} ...(i)
    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\frac{5 \mathrm{R}}{36}{/tex} ...(ii)
    {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{36}{5 R} \frac{3 R}{16}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda_1=\frac{36 \times 3}{5 \times 16} \times{/tex} 4861
    {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = 6562.35 {tex}\overset o{A}{/tex}

question_html: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. What is the potential energy and kinetic energy of an electron in the third excited state?
question_text: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. What is the potential energy and kinetic energy of an electron in the third excited state?
explanation: E n = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{n^2}{/tex} eV = Total energy For third excited state n = 4 E 4 = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{4^2}=\frac{-13.6}{16}{/tex} = -0.85 eV Potential Energy = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} Total Energy = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} E 4 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (-0.85) eV = -1.70 eV Kinetic energy = -(Total Energy) = -E 4 = 0.85 eV
solution: E n = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{n^2}{/tex} eV = Total energy For third excited state n = 4 E 4 = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{4^2}=\frac{-13.6}{16}{/tex} = -0.85 eV Potential Energy = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} Total Energy = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} E 4 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (-0.85) eV = -1.70 eV Kinetic energy = -(Total Energy) = -E 4 = 0.85 eV
solution_html:
En = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{n^2}{/tex} eV = Total energy
For third excited state n = 4
E4 = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{4^2}=\frac{-13.6}{16}{/tex} = -0.85 eV
Potential Energy = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} Total Energy = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} E4
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (-0.85) eV
= -1.70 eV
Kinetic energy = -(Total Energy) = -E4
= 0.85 eV

question_text: Calculate the wavelength of the second line of Lyman series in a spectrum of hydrogen atom. (Take Rydberg constant, R = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -1 )
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} For second line of Lyman series n 1 = 1, n 2 = 3 {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.1 \times 10^7\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 {tex}\left[1-\frac{1}{9}\right]{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{8}{9}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8.8}{9}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -1 {tex}\lambda=\frac{9}{8.8}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m =1.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m = 1023 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} For second line of Lyman series n 1 = 1, n 2 = 3 {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.1 \times 10^7\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 {tex}\left[1-\frac{1}{9}\right]{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{8}{9}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8.8}{9}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -1 {tex}\lambda=\frac{9}{8.8}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m =1.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m = 1023 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex}
For second line of Lyman series
n1 = 1, n2 = 3
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.1 \times 10^7\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]{/tex}
= 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 {tex}\left[1-\frac{1}{9}\right]{/tex}
= 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{8}{9}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{8.8}{9}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 107 m-1
{tex}\lambda=\frac{9}{8.8}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m
=1.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m
= 1023 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}

question_html:
1. Differentiate between distance of closest approach and impact parameter.
  2. Determine the distance of closest approach when an alpha particle of kinetic energy 3.95 MeV approaches a nucleus of Z = 79, stops and reverses its directions.
question_text: Differentiate between distance of closest approach and impact parameter . Determine the distance of closest approach when an alpha particle of kinetic energy 3.95 MeV approaches a nucleus of Z = 79, stops and reverses its directions.
explanation: Distance of closest approach: It is the minimum distance of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle from the centre of nucleus at which its total kinetic energy gets converted into electrostatic potential energy. {tex}r_o=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \frac{2 Z e^2}{E_K}{/tex} Impact parameter: Perpendicular distance of the initial velocity vector of the α- particle from the centre of the nucleus. {tex}r_o=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \frac{2 Z e^2}{E_K}{/tex} {tex}r_o=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 79 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2}{3.95 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex} = 57.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m hence,the distance of closest approach will be 57.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m
solution: Distance of closest approach: It is the minimum distance of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle from the centre of nucleus at which its total kinetic energy gets converted into electrostatic potential energy. {tex}r_o=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \frac{2 Z e^2}{E_K}{/tex} Impact parameter: Perpendicular distance of the initial velocity vector of the α- particle from the centre of the nucleus. {tex}r_o=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \frac{2 Z e^2}{E_K}{/tex} {tex}r_o=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 79 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2}{3.95 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex} = 57.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m hence,the distance of closest approach will be 57.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m
solution_html:
1. Distance of closest approach: It is the minimum distance of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle from the centre of nucleus at which its total kinetic energy gets converted into electrostatic potential energy.
    {tex}r_o=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o} \frac{2 Z e^2}{E_K}{/tex}
    Impact parameter: Perpendicular distance of the initial velocity vector of the α- particle from the centre of the nucleus.
  2. {tex}r_o=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_o} \frac{2 Z e^2}{E_K}{/tex}
    {tex}r_o=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 79 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2}{3.95 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex}
    = 57.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 m
    hence,the distance of closest approach will be 57.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 m

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State three postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom.
  2. Find the angular momentum of an electron revolving in the second orbit in Bohr's hydrogen atom.
question_text: State three postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom. Find the angular momentum of an electron revolving in the second orbit in Bohr's hydrogen atom.
explanation: An electron in an atom revolves in certain stable orbit without the emission of radiant energy. The electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} where h is the Planck’s constant. When an electron makes a transition from one of its specified non-radiating orbits to another of lower energy orbit a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states. Angular momentum = {tex}\frac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex} For n = 2 Angular momentum = {tex}\frac{2h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\pi}{/tex} = 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 kg m 2 s -1 Hence, the required angular momentum will be 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 kg m 2 s -1 .
solution: An electron in an atom revolves in certain stable orbit without the emission of radiant energy. The electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} where h is the Planck’s constant. When an electron makes a transition from one of its specified non-radiating orbits to another of lower energy orbit a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states. Angular momentum = {tex}\frac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex} For n = 2 Angular momentum = {tex}\frac{2h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\pi}{/tex} = 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 kg m 2 s -1 Hence, the required angular momentum will be 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 kg m 2 s -1 .
solution_html:
1. An electron in an atom revolves in certain stable orbit without the emission of radiant energy.
    2. The electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} where h is the Planck’s constant.
    3. When an electron makes a transition from one of its specified non-radiating orbits to another of lower energy orbit a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states.
  2. Angular momentum = {tex}\frac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    For n = 2
    Angular momentum = {tex}\frac{2h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\pi}{/tex}
    = 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 kg m2 s-1
    Hence, the required angular momentum will be 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 kg m2 s-1.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Increase in the intensity of the radiation causing photo-electric emission from a surface, does not affect the maximum K.E. of the photo electrons. Explain.
  2. The photon emitted during the de-excitation from the first excited level to the ground state of hydrogen atom is used to irradiate a photo cathode in which stopping potential is 5 V. Calculate the work function of the cathode used.
question_text: Increase in the intensity of the radiation causing photo-electric emission from a surface, does not affect the maximum K.E. of the photo electrons. Explain. The photon emitted during the de-excitation from the first excited level to the ground state of hydrogen atom is used to irradiate a photo cathode in which stopping potential is 5 V. Calculate the work function of the cathode used.
explanation: {tex}h v=\phi_o+K_{\max }{/tex} It is evident from the above equation that the maximum kinetic energy depends on the frequency of incident radiation and is independent of the intensity of the radiations. Energy carried by the photon E = E i - E f {tex}E=-\frac{13.6}{2^2}-\left(\frac{-13.6}{1^2}\right){/tex} = 10.2 eV {tex}\phi_o=E-e V_o{/tex} = 10.2 eV - 5 eV = 5.2 eV Hence,the work function of the cathode used is 5.2 eV.
solution: {tex}h v=\phi_o+K_{\max }{/tex} It is evident from the above equation that the maximum kinetic energy depends on the frequency of incident radiation and is independent of the intensity of the radiations. Energy carried by the photon E = E i - E f {tex}E=-\frac{13.6}{2^2}-\left(\frac{-13.6}{1^2}\right){/tex} = 10.2 eV {tex}\phi_o=E-e V_o{/tex} = 10.2 eV - 5 eV = 5.2 eV Hence,the work function of the cathode used is 5.2 eV.
solution_html:
1. {tex}h v=\phi_o+K_{\max }{/tex}
    It is evident from the above equation that the maximum kinetic energy depends on the frequency of incident radiation and is independent of the intensity of the radiations.
  2. Energy carried by the photon
    E = Ei - Ef
    {tex}E=-\frac{13.6}{2^2}-\left(\frac{-13.6}{1^2}\right){/tex} = 10.2 eV
    {tex}\phi_o=E-e V_o{/tex}
    = 10.2 eV - 5 eV
    = 5.2 eV
    Hence,the work function of the cathode used is 5.2 eV.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Differentiate between impact parameter and distance of closest approach in alpha particle scattering experiment. Identify, situations when impact parameter is (i) minimum, and (ii) very large.
  2. An electron cannot revolve around the nucleus in a hydrogen atom at an arbitrary distance. Explain.
question_text: Differentiate between impact parameter and distance of closest approach in alpha particle scattering experiment. Identify, situations when impact parameter is (i) minimum, and (ii) very large. An electron cannot revolve around the nucleus in a hydrogen atom at an arbitrary distance. Explain.
explanation: Impact parameter is the perpendicular distance of the initial velocity vector of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} – particle from the centre of the nucleus. Distance of closest approach: It is the minimum distance between the projected {tex}\alpha{/tex} – particle and the nucleus of target atom at which the kinetic energy of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} – particle becomes equal to potential energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle in the field of nucleus. Minimum: When {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle rebounds back {tex}(\theta=\pi){/tex} Very large: when {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle goes nearly undeviated and has a small deflection {tex}(\theta=0^o){/tex} According to Bohr's second postulate electron revolves only in those orbit for which angular momentum is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex} .
solution: Impact parameter is the perpendicular distance of the initial velocity vector of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} – particle from the centre of the nucleus. Distance of closest approach: It is the minimum distance between the projected {tex}\alpha{/tex} – particle and the nucleus of target atom at which the kinetic energy of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} – particle becomes equal to potential energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle in the field of nucleus. Minimum: When {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle rebounds back {tex}(\theta=\pi){/tex} Very large: when {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle goes nearly undeviated and has a small deflection {tex}(\theta=0^o){/tex} According to Bohr's second postulate electron revolves only in those orbit for which angular momentum is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. Impact parameter is the perpendicular distance of the initial velocity vector of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} – particle from the centre of the nucleus.
    Distance of closest approach: It is the minimum distance between the projected {tex}\alpha{/tex} – particle and the nucleus of target atom at which the kinetic energy of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} – particle becomes equal to potential energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle in the field of nucleus.
    Minimum: When {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle rebounds back {tex}(\theta=\pi){/tex}
    Very large: when {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle goes nearly undeviated and has a small deflection {tex}(\theta=0^o){/tex}
  2. According to Bohr's second postulate electron revolves only in those orbit for which angular momentum is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex}.

question_html:

question_text: An electron in a hydrogen atom jumps from second excited state to the first excited state. Name the spectral series in the spectrum of hydrogen atom to which the emitted radiation belongs. Find the ratio of the wavelengths of the most energetic spectral lines in the Balmer series to that in Paschen series of Hydrogen spectrum.
explanation: The spectral series in the spectrum of hydrogen atom to which the emitted radiation belongs to Balmer series. {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_B}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_B}=\frac{R}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_p}=R\left[\frac{1}{3^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda p}=\frac{R}{9}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_B}{\lambda p}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
solution: The spectral series in the spectrum of hydrogen atom to which the emitted radiation belongs to Balmer series. {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_B}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_B}=\frac{R}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_p}=R\left[\frac{1}{3^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda p}=\frac{R}{9}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_B}{\lambda p}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The spectral series in the spectrum of hydrogen atom to which the emitted radiation belongs to Balmer series.
  2. {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_B}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex}

    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_p}=R\left[\frac{1}{3^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex}

    {tex}\frac{\lambda_B}{\lambda p}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. An {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle having kinetic energy K approaches a nucleus of atomic number Z. It gets close to the nucleus and then approaches a distance (d) and reverses its direction. Obtain an expression for the distance of closest approach (d) in terms of kinetic energy of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle.
  2. A proton and an alpha particle approach a target nucleus in head-on position, with equal velocities. Find the ratio of their distances of closest approach to the target nucleus.
question_text: An {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle having kinetic energy K approaches a nucleus of atomic number Z. It gets close to the nucleus and then approaches a distance (d) and reverses its direction. Obtain an expression for the distance of closest approach (d) in terms of kinetic energy of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. A proton and an alpha particle approach a target nucleus in head-on position, with equal velocities. Find the ratio of their distances of closest approach to the target nucleus.
explanation: At the distance, d, the K.E, K, gets converted into P.E. of the system. P.E. at distance d = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{(2 e)\left(Z_e\right)}{d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 Z e^2}{d}{/tex} = K {tex}\therefore{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 Z e^2}{\mathrm{~K}}{/tex} Charge: Proton (+1e), Alpha Particle (+2e) Mass: Proton (m p ), Alpha Particle (4m p ) {tex}\left(F \propto \frac{q_1 \times q_2}{r^2}\right){/tex} . - Proton: {tex}r_p \propto \frac{k_e \cdot Z^2 \cdot e^2}{m_p \cdot v}{/tex} ...1 - Alpha Particle: {tex}r_\alpha \propto \frac{k_e \cdot Z^2 \cdot(2 e)^2}{m_\alpha \cdot v}{/tex} ...2 comparing (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{r_p}{r_\alpha}=\frac{m_\alpha}{m_p}{/tex} = 4. The ratio of distances of closest approach for a proton and an alpha particle is 4:1. T
solution: At the distance, d, the K.E, K, gets converted into P.E. of the system. P.E. at distance d = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{(2 e)\left(Z_e\right)}{d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 Z e^2}{d}{/tex} = K {tex}\therefore{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 Z e^2}{\mathrm{~K}}{/tex} Charge: Proton (+1e), Alpha Particle (+2e) Mass: Proton (m p ), Alpha Particle (4m p ) {tex}\left(F \propto \frac{q_1 \times q_2}{r^2}\right){/tex} . - Proton: {tex}r_p \propto \frac{k_e \cdot Z^2 \cdot e^2}{m_p \cdot v}{/tex} ...1 - Alpha Particle: {tex}r_\alpha \propto \frac{k_e \cdot Z^2 \cdot(2 e)^2}{m_\alpha \cdot v}{/tex} ...2 comparing (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{r_p}{r_\alpha}=\frac{m_\alpha}{m_p}{/tex} = 4. The ratio of distances of closest approach for a proton and an alpha particle is 4:1. T
solution_html:
1. At the distance, d, the K.E, K, gets converted into P.E. of the system.
    P.E. at distance d = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{(2 e)\left(Z_e\right)}{d}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 Z e^2}{d}{/tex} = K
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 Z e^2}{\mathrm{~K}}{/tex}

  2. Charge: Proton (+1e), Alpha Particle (+2e)
    Mass: Proton (mp), Alpha Particle (4mp)
    {tex}\left(F \propto \frac{q_1 \times q_2}{r^2}\right){/tex}.
    - Proton: {tex}r_p \propto \frac{k_e \cdot Z^2 \cdot e^2}{m_p \cdot v}{/tex} ...1
    - Alpha Particle: {tex}r_\alpha \propto \frac{k_e \cdot Z^2 \cdot(2 e)^2}{m_\alpha \cdot v}{/tex} ...2
    comparing (1) and (2)
    {tex}\frac{r_p}{r_\alpha}=\frac{m_\alpha}{m_p}{/tex} = 4.
    The ratio of distances of closest approach for a proton and an alpha particle is 4:1. T

explanation: He mentioned that if we use the wavelength associated with the electron, and assume that an integral number of wavelengths must fit in the circumference of an orbit, we get same Bohr second postulate for quantization of angular momentum . {tex}2 \pi r =n \lambda{/tex} r is radius of orbit {tex} =\frac{n h}{p}{/tex} n is integral number & p is momentum of electron {tex}2 \pi r =\frac{n h}{m v}{/tex} . {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
solution: He mentioned that if we use the wavelength associated with the electron, and assume that an integral number of wavelengths must fit in the circumference of an orbit, we get same Bohr second postulate for quantization of angular momentum . {tex}2 \pi r =n \lambda{/tex} r is radius of orbit {tex} =\frac{n h}{p}{/tex} n is integral number & p is momentum of electron {tex}2 \pi r =\frac{n h}{m v}{/tex} . {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
solution_html:
He mentioned that if we use the wavelength associated with the electron, and assume that an integral number of wavelengths must fit in the circumference of an orbit, we get same Bohr second postulate for quantization of angular momentum .
{tex}2 \pi r =n \lambda{/tex} r is radius of orbit
{tex} =\frac{n h}{p}{/tex} n is integral number & p is momentum of electron
{tex}2 \pi r =\frac{n h}{m v}{/tex}.
{tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}

explanation: CONCLUSIONS FROM GRAPH: Decreasing slope shows that most of the particles passes straight through the gold foil without deflection. So most of the space within the atom must be empty. A few particles, about 1 in 800 got deflected through 90 degree or more, depending on the impact parameter.
solution: CONCLUSIONS FROM GRAPH: Decreasing slope shows that most of the particles passes straight through the gold foil without deflection. So most of the space within the atom must be empty. A few particles, about 1 in 800 got deflected through 90 degree or more, depending on the impact parameter.
solution_html:
CONCLUSIONS FROM GRAPH:

  1. Decreasing slope shows that most of the particles passes straight through the gold foil without deflection. So most of the space within the atom must be empty.
  2. A few particles, about 1 in 800 got deflected through 90 degree or more, depending on the impact parameter.

question_text: Define the term - Distance of closest approach. How will it be affected, for an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle, if kinetic energy of the particle is doubled?
explanation: Distance of closest approach: It is the distance of the alpha particle from the nucleus when the kinetic energy of alpha particle becomes equal to electrical potential energy between nucleus and alpha particle. KE {tex}\propto \frac{1}{r}{/tex} if KE is doubled r decreases by half {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
solution: Distance of closest approach: It is the distance of the alpha particle from the nucleus when the kinetic energy of alpha particle becomes equal to electrical potential energy between nucleus and alpha particle. KE {tex}\propto \frac{1}{r}{/tex} if KE is doubled r decreases by half {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Distance of closest approach: It is the distance of the alpha particle from the nucleus when the kinetic energy of alpha particle becomes equal to electrical potential energy between nucleus and alpha particle.
KE {tex}\propto \frac{1}{r}{/tex}
if KE is doubled r decreases by half {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}

question_text: Sketch the energy level diagram for hydrogen atom. Find the ratio of the longest and the shortest wavelength in Lyman series in hydrogen atom.
explanation: {tex}E=-\frac{13 \cdot 6}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{n_2{ }^2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\min }}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{\infty^2}\right)=R{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {max }}}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right)=\frac{3 R}{4} {/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\max }}{\lambda_{\min }}=\frac{4}{3 R} \times R=\frac4 3{/tex}
solution: {tex}E=-\frac{13 \cdot 6}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{n_2{ }^2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\min }}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{\infty^2}\right)=R{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {max }}}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right)=\frac{3 R}{4} {/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\max }}{\lambda_{\min }}=\frac{4}{3 R} \times R=\frac4 3{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}E=-\frac{13 \cdot 6}{n^2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{n_2{ }^2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\min }}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{\infty^2}\right)=R{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {max }}}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right)=\frac{3 R}{4} {/tex}
{tex}\frac{\lambda_{\max }}{\lambda_{\min }}=\frac{4}{3 R} \times R=\frac4 3{/tex}

question_html:
The energy of a hydrogen atom in the first excited state is -3.4 eV. Find:

  1. the radius of this orbit. (Take Bohr radius = 0.53 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex})
  2. the angular momentum of the electron in the orbit.
  3. the kinetic and potential energy of the electron in the orbit.
question_text: The energy of a hydrogen atom in the first excited state is -3.4 eV. Find: the radius of this orbit. (Take Bohr radius = 0.53 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} ) the angular momentum of the electron in the orbit. the kinetic and potential energy of the electron in the orbit.
explanation: Energy E = -3.4eV {tex}r=\frac{k e^2}{2 E}{/tex} as PE = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} E {tex}r=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2}{2 \times 3.4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} r = 2.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}E=-\frac{13.6}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}-3.4=\frac{-13.6}{n^2}{/tex} n = 2 {tex}L=\frac{2 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times \pi}{/tex} L = 2.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 js KE = -E = 3.4 eV PE = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} E = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.4 = -6.8 eV
solution: Energy E = -3.4eV {tex}r=\frac{k e^2}{2 E}{/tex} as PE = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} E {tex}r=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2}{2 \times 3.4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} r = 2.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}E=-\frac{13.6}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}-3.4=\frac{-13.6}{n^2}{/tex} n = 2 {tex}L=\frac{2 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times \pi}{/tex} L = 2.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 js KE = -E = 3.4 eV PE = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} E = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.4 = -6.8 eV
solution_html:
Energy E = -3.4eV

  1. {tex}r=\frac{k e^2}{2 E}{/tex} as PE = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} E
    {tex}r=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2}{2 \times 3.4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
    r = 2.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m
  2. {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    {tex}E=-\frac{13.6}{n^2}{/tex}
    {tex}-3.4=\frac{-13.6}{n^2}{/tex}
    n = 2
    {tex}L=\frac{2 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times \pi}{/tex}
    L = 2.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 js
  3. KE = -E = 3.4 eV
    PE = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} E = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.4
    = -6.8 eV

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State Bohr's quantization condition for defining stationary orbits.
  2. Use the energy level diagram shown below to obtain the relation between three wavelengths {tex}\lambda_1, \lambda_2{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_3{/tex} emitted due to the transition of electron from the energy states C and B.
question_text: State Bohr's quantization condition for defining stationary orbits. Use the energy level diagram shown below to obtain the relation between three wavelengths {tex}\lambda_1, \lambda_2{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_3{/tex} emitted due to the transition of electron from the energy states C and B.
explanation: According to Bohr's quantization condition for defining stationary orbits is only those orbits around the nucleus are allowed and stable for electron to revolve for which the angular momentum of the electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} where h is Planck's constant {tex}\text { mvr }=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_B^2}-\frac{1}{n_C^2}\right){/tex} ...(i) {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_A^2}-\frac{1}{n_B^2}\right){/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_3}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_A^2}-\frac{1}{n_C^2}\right){/tex} ...(iii) From (i) {tex}\frac{R}{n_B^2}=\frac{1}{\lambda_1}+\frac{R}{n_C^2}{/tex} Substituting in (ii) {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{R}{n_A^2}-\frac{1}{\lambda_1}-\frac{R}{n_C^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}+\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{A^2}}-\frac{1}{n_{C^2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}+\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\frac{1}{\lambda_3}{/tex}
solution: According to Bohr's quantization condition for defining stationary orbits is only those orbits around the nucleus are allowed and stable for electron to revolve for which the angular momentum of the electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} where h is Planck's constant {tex}\text { mvr }=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_B^2}-\frac{1}{n_C^2}\right){/tex} ...(i) {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_A^2}-\frac{1}{n_B^2}\right){/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_3}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_A^2}-\frac{1}{n_C^2}\right){/tex} ...(iii) From (i) {tex}\frac{R}{n_B^2}=\frac{1}{\lambda_1}+\frac{R}{n_C^2}{/tex} Substituting in (ii) {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{R}{n_A^2}-\frac{1}{\lambda_1}-\frac{R}{n_C^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}+\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{A^2}}-\frac{1}{n_{C^2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}+\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\frac{1}{\lambda_3}{/tex}
solution_html:
According to Bohr's quantization condition for defining stationary orbits is only those orbits around the nucleus are allowed and stable for electron to revolve for which the angular momentum of the electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} where h is Planck's constant
{tex}\text { mvr }=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_B^2}-\frac{1}{n_C^2}\right){/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_A^2}-\frac{1}{n_B^2}\right){/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_3}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_A^2}-\frac{1}{n_C^2}\right){/tex} ...(iii)
From (i)
{tex}\frac{R}{n_B^2}=\frac{1}{\lambda_1}+\frac{R}{n_C^2}{/tex}
Substituting in (ii)
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{R}{n_A^2}-\frac{1}{\lambda_1}-\frac{R}{n_C^2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}+\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{A^2}}-\frac{1}{n_{C^2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}+\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\frac{1}{\lambda_3}{/tex}

question_text: An electron is in the third excited state in a hydrogen atom. It undergoes transitions to the lower energy states. What is the maximum number of spectral lines that can be emitted? Calculate the minimum wavelength of the spectral lines emitted.

solution_html:
as {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\min }}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)=\frac{15 R}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{\min }=\frac{16}{15 R}=\frac{16}{15 \times 1.09 \times 10^7}{/tex}
= 0.978 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m
= 97.8 nm
