question_html: State Bohr's postulate to explain stable orbits in a hydrogen atom. Prove that the speed with which the electron revolves in nth orbit is proportional to (1/n)
question_text: State Bohr's postulate to explain stable orbits in a hydrogen atom. Prove that the speed with which the electron revolves in n th orbit is proportional to (1/n)
explanation: According to Bohr's second postulate only those orbits around the nucleus are allowed for electron to revolve for which the angular momentum of the electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} where h is Planck's constant So the electron revolues as Centripetal force = Coulombs force between proton electrons {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=\frac{k e^2}{r^2}{/tex} {tex}v^2=\frac{k e^2}{m r}{/tex} also for hydrogen atom {tex}r=\frac{\varepsilon_0 h^2 n^2}{e^2 \pi m}{/tex} {tex}v^2=\frac{k e^2 \times e^2 \pi m}{m \times \varepsilon_0 h^2 n^2}=\frac{k \pi e 4}{\varepsilon_0 h^2 n^2}{/tex} {tex}v^2 \propto \frac{1}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}v \propto \frac1 n{/tex}
solution: According to Bohr's second postulate only those orbits around the nucleus are allowed for electron to revolve for which the angular momentum of the electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} where h is Planck's constant So the electron revolues as Centripetal force = Coulombs force between proton electrons {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=\frac{k e^2}{r^2}{/tex} {tex}v^2=\frac{k e^2}{m r}{/tex} also for hydrogen atom {tex}r=\frac{\varepsilon_0 h^2 n^2}{e^2 \pi m}{/tex} {tex}v^2=\frac{k e^2 \times e^2 \pi m}{m \times \varepsilon_0 h^2 n^2}=\frac{k \pi e 4}{\varepsilon_0 h^2 n^2}{/tex} {tex}v^2 \propto \frac{1}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}v \propto \frac1 n{/tex}
solution_html:
According to Bohr's second postulate only those orbits around the nucleus are allowed for electron to revolve for which the angular momentum of the electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} where h is Planck's constant
So the electron revolues as
Centripetal force = Coulombs force between proton electrons
{tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=\frac{k e^2}{r^2}{/tex}
{tex}v^2=\frac{k e^2}{m r}{/tex}
also for hydrogen atom
{tex}r=\frac{\varepsilon_0 h^2 n^2}{e^2 \pi m}{/tex}
{tex}v^2=\frac{k e^2 \times e^2 \pi m}{m \times \varepsilon_0 h^2 n^2}=\frac{k \pi e 4}{\varepsilon_0 h^2 n^2}{/tex}
{tex}v^2 \propto \frac{1}{n^2}{/tex}
{tex}v \propto \frac1 n{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A narrow beam of protons, each having 4.1 MeV energy is approaching a sheet of lead (Z = 82). Calculate:

  1. the speed of a proton in the beam, and
  2. the distance of its closest approach
question_text: A narrow beam of protons, each having 4.1 MeV energy is approaching a sheet of lead (Z = 82). Calculate: the speed of a proton in the beam, and the distance of its closest approach
explanation: KE of proton = 4.1 Mev = 4.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 J Mass of proton = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -21 kg {tex}K E=\frac{1}{2} m v^2{/tex} {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K E}{m}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6.56 \times 10^{-13}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}}}{/tex} = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 J For the distance of closest approach KE of proton = PE of system 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 = {tex}\frac{k e \times 82 e}{\gamma}{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 82 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2}{6.56 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex} = 288 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 = 2.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 m
solution: KE of proton = 4.1 Mev = 4.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 J Mass of proton = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -21 kg {tex}K E=\frac{1}{2} m v^2{/tex} {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K E}{m}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6.56 \times 10^{-13}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}}}{/tex} = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 J For the distance of closest approach KE of proton = PE of system 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 = {tex}\frac{k e \times 82 e}{\gamma}{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 82 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2}{6.56 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex} = 288 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 = 2.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 m
solution_html:
1. KE of proton = 4.1 Mev = 4.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
    = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 J
    Mass of proton = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-21 kg
    {tex}K E=\frac{1}{2} m v^2{/tex}
    {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K E}{m}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6.56 \times 10^{-13}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}}}{/tex}
    = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 J
  2. For the distance of closest approach
    KE of proton = PE of system
    6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 = {tex}\frac{k e \times 82 e}{\gamma}{/tex}
    {tex}r=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 82 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2}{6.56 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex}
    = 288 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16
    = 2.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 m

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. The energy of a hydrogen atom in an orbit is -1.51 eV. What are the kinetic and potential energies of the electron in this orbit?
  2. The electron in a hydrogen atom is typically found at a distance of about 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 m from the nucleus which has a diameter of about 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 m. Assuming the hydrogen atom to be a sphere of radius 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 m, what fraction of its volume is occupied by the nucleus?
question_text: The energy of a hydrogen atom in an orbit is -1.51 eV. What are the kinetic and potential energies of the electron in this orbit? The electron in a hydrogen atom is typically found at a distance of about 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 m from the nucleus which has a diameter of about 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m. Assuming the hydrogen atom to be a sphere of radius 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 m, what fraction of its volume is occupied by the nucleus?
explanation: Total energy of hydrogen atom = -1.51 ev KE = -Total energy = -(-1.51) ev KE = 1.51 ev PE = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} total energy = -3.02 eV diameter of nucleus = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m radius (r) = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m distance of electron from nucleus = 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 m = R fraction of volume occupied = {tex}\frac{\text { Volume of nucleus }}{\text { Volume of atom }}{/tex} by nucleus = {tex}\frac{(\frac 43) \pi r^3}{(\frac 43) \pi R^3}=\frac{r^3}{R^3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(0.5 \times 10^{-15}\right)^3}{\left(5.3 \times 10^{-11}\right)^3}{/tex} = 8.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16
solution: Total energy of hydrogen atom = -1.51 ev KE = -Total energy = -(-1.51) ev KE = 1.51 ev PE = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} total energy = -3.02 eV diameter of nucleus = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m radius (r) = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m distance of electron from nucleus = 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 m = R fraction of volume occupied = {tex}\frac{\text { Volume of nucleus }}{\text { Volume of atom }}{/tex} by nucleus = {tex}\frac{(\frac 43) \pi r^3}{(\frac 43) \pi R^3}=\frac{r^3}{R^3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(0.5 \times 10^{-15}\right)^3}{\left(5.3 \times 10^{-11}\right)^3}{/tex} = 8.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16
solution_html:
1. Total energy of hydrogen atom = -1.51 ev
    KE = -Total energy
    = -(-1.51) ev
    KE = 1.51 ev
    PE = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} total energy = -3.02 eV
  2. diameter of nucleus = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 m
    radius (r) = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 m
    distance of electron from nucleus = 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 m = R
    fraction of volume occupied = {tex}\frac{\text { Volume of nucleus }}{\text { Volume of atom }}{/tex}
    by nucleus = {tex}\frac{(\frac 43) \pi r^3}{(\frac 43) \pi R^3}=\frac{r^3}{R^3}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\left(0.5 \times 10^{-15}\right)^3}{\left(5.3 \times 10^{-11}\right)^3}{/tex}
    = 8.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16

subject: Physics
question_html: Name the spectral series for a hydrogen atom that lies in the visible region. Find the ratio of the maximum to the minimum wavelengths of this series.
question_text: Name the spectral series for a hydrogen atom that lies in the visible region. Find the ratio of the maximum to the minimum wavelengths of this series.
explanation: Boher Series From Bohr's theory = {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right){/tex} For maximum wavelength transition is from 3 rd {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2 nd = {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 R}{36}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\max }=\frac{36}{5 R}{/tex} For minimum wavelength transition is from {tex}\infty{/tex} to 2 nd {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\min }} =R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{4}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\min } =\frac{4}{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\max }}{\lambda_{\min }}=\frac{36}{5 R} \times \frac{R}{4}=\frac{9}{5}{/tex}
solution: Boher Series From Bohr's theory = {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right){/tex} For maximum wavelength transition is from 3 rd {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2 nd = {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 R}{36}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\max }=\frac{36}{5 R}{/tex} For minimum wavelength transition is from {tex}\infty{/tex} to 2 nd {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\min }} =R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{4}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\min } =\frac{4}{R}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\max }}{\lambda_{\min }}=\frac{36}{5 R} \times \frac{R}{4}=\frac{9}{5}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Boher Series
  2. From Bohr's theory = {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right){/tex}
    For maximum wavelength transition is from 3rd {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2nd = {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{5 R}{36}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda_{\max }=\frac{36}{5 R}{/tex}
    For minimum wavelength transition is from {tex}\infty{/tex} to 2nd {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\min }} =R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{R}{4}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda_{\min } =\frac{4}{R}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\max }}{\lambda_{\min }}=\frac{36}{5 R} \times \frac{R}{4}=\frac{9}{5}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. In Geiger-Marsden experiment, calculate the distance of closest approach for an alpha particle with energy 2.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 J. Consider that the particle approaches gold nucleus (Z = 79) in head-on position.
  2. If the above experiment is repeated with a proton of the same energy, then what will be the value of the distance of closest approach?
question_text: In Geiger-Marsden experiment, calculate the distance of closest approach for an alpha particle with energy 2.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 J. Consider that the particle approaches gold nucleus (Z = 79) in head-on position. If the above experiment is repeated with a proton of the same energy, then what will be the value of the distance of closest approach?
explanation: Charge on gold nucleus = 79e Charge on {tex}\propto{/tex} particle = 2e Energy = 2.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 J Energy {tex}=\frac{k q_1 q_2}{r}{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{k q_1 q_2}{\text { Energy }}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 79 \mathrm{e} \times 2 e}{2.56 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 1422 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 m r = 1.422 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 m Proton charge = e {tex}r=\frac{k q_1 q_2}{\text { energy }}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 79 \mathrm{e} \times e}{2.56 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 0.711 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 m
solution: Charge on gold nucleus = 79e Charge on {tex}\propto{/tex} particle = 2e Energy = 2.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 J Energy {tex}=\frac{k q_1 q_2}{r}{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{k q_1 q_2}{\text { Energy }}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 79 \mathrm{e} \times 2 e}{2.56 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 1422 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 m r = 1.422 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 m Proton charge = e {tex}r=\frac{k q_1 q_2}{\text { energy }}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 79 \mathrm{e} \times e}{2.56 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 0.711 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 m
solution_html:
1. Charge on gold nucleus = 79e
    Charge on {tex}\propto{/tex} particle = 2e
    Energy = 2.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 J
    Energy {tex}=\frac{k q_1 q_2}{r}{/tex}
    {tex}r=\frac{k q_1 q_2}{\text { Energy }}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 79 \mathrm{e} \times 2 e}{2.56 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex}
    = 1422 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 m
    r = 1.422 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 m
  2. Proton charge = e
    {tex}r=\frac{k q_1 q_2}{\text { energy }}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 79 \mathrm{e} \times e}{2.56 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex}
    = 0.711 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 m

explanation: Since alpha particles are highly massive as compared to the proton in the nucleus of hydrogen, the scattering angle would have been lesser. The alpha particles must not have bounced back and retraced same path as they have more charge and mass as compared to proton.
solution: Since alpha particles are highly massive as compared to the proton in the nucleus of hydrogen, the scattering angle would have been lesser. The alpha particles must not have bounced back and retraced same path as they have more charge and mass as compared to proton.

question_html: Draw a graph showing the variation of number of particles scattered (N) with the scattering angle {tex}\theta{/tex} in Geiger-Marsden experiment. Why only a small fraction of the particles are scattered at {tex}\theta{/tex} > 90o? Mention two limitations of Rutherford nuclear model of an atom.
question_text: Draw a graph showing the variation of number of particles scattered (N) with the scattering angle {tex}\theta{/tex} in Geiger-Marsden experiment. Why only a small fraction of the particles are scattered at {tex}\theta{/tex} > 90 o ? Mention two limitations of Rutherford nuclear model of an atom.
explanation: Only a small fraction of the particles are scattered at {tex}\theta{/tex} > 90 o as most of the space within the atom was empty and nucleus was at a very small space, so there were only few particles which were able to come near to the nucleus. As there impact parameter was reduced they undergoes more deflecting angle. b 1 < b 2 So deflection is more for {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} than {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} Limitation of Rutherford model of atom: He concluded that the electrons revolve around the nucleus in circular orbits I. If so, then electron must be accelerating around the nucleus and that should cause the electron to radiate energy in the form of electromagnetic waves and loosing energy which would in result must make electron fall in the nucleus. This was not explained by him. He mentioned electron revolves in any energy orbit around the nucleus. But later it was found that they form discrete atomic spectra. He was not able to answer this too.
solution: Only a small fraction of the particles are scattered at {tex}\theta{/tex} > 90 o as most of the space within the atom was empty and nucleus was at a very small space, so there were only few particles which were able to come near to the nucleus. As there impact parameter was reduced they undergoes more deflecting angle. b 1 < b 2 So deflection is more for {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} than {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} Limitation of Rutherford model of atom: He concluded that the electrons revolve around the nucleus in circular orbits I. If so, then electron must be accelerating around the nucleus and that should cause the electron to radiate energy in the form of electromagnetic waves and loosing energy which would in result must make electron fall in the nucleus. This was not explained by him. He mentioned electron revolves in any energy orbit around the nucleus. But later it was found that they form discrete atomic spectra. He was not able to answer this too.
solution_html:
Only a small fraction of the particles are scattered at {tex}\theta{/tex} > 90o as most of the space within the atom was empty and nucleus was at a very small space, so there were only few particles which were able to come near to the nucleus. As there impact parameter was reduced they undergoes more deflecting angle.

b1 < b2
So deflection is more for {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} than {tex}\lambda_2{/tex}
Limitation of Rutherford model of atom:

  1. He concluded that the electrons revolve around the nucleus in circular orbits I. If so, then electron must be accelerating around the nucleus and that should cause the electron to radiate energy in the form of electromagnetic waves and loosing energy which would in result must make electron fall in the nucleus. This was not explained by him.
  2. He mentioned electron revolves in any energy orbit around the nucleus. But later it was found that they form discrete atomic spectra. He was not able to answer this too.

question_html:
1. Use Bohr's postulate to prove that the radius of nth orbit in a hydrogen atom is proportional to n2.
  2. How will the energy of a hydrogen atom change if n increases from 1 to {tex}\infty{/tex}?
question_text: Use Bohr's postulate to prove that the radius of n th orbit in a hydrogen atom is proportional to n 2 . How will the energy of a hydrogen atom change if n increases from 1 to {tex}\infty{/tex} ?
explanation: according to Bohr's second postulate {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}{/tex} So, if electron rotates in orbit of a hydrogen Centripetal for a = electrostatic force of attraction {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=\frac{k z e \cdot e}{r^2}{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{k z e^2}{m v^2}{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{k z e^2}{m } \times \frac{(2 \pi)^2}{n^2 h^2} m^2r^2{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 k z e^2}{/tex} [r {tex}\propto{/tex} n 2 ] {tex}E=\frac{-13.6}{n^2} \mathrm{ev}^v{/tex} So, if n increases magnitude of energy decreases but due to -ve sign energy increase at {tex}\infty{/tex} E = 0
solution: according to Bohr's second postulate {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}{/tex} So, if electron rotates in orbit of a hydrogen Centripetal for a = electrostatic force of attraction {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=\frac{k z e \cdot e}{r^2}{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{k z e^2}{m v^2}{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{k z e^2}{m } \times \frac{(2 \pi)^2}{n^2 h^2} m^2r^2{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 k z e^2}{/tex} [r {tex}\propto{/tex} n 2 ] {tex}E=\frac{-13.6}{n^2} \mathrm{ev}^v{/tex} So, if n increases magnitude of energy decreases but due to -ve sign energy increase at {tex}\infty{/tex} E = 0
solution_html:
1. according to Bohr's second postulate
    {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    {tex}v=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}{/tex}
    So, if electron rotates in orbit of a hydrogen
    Centripetal for a = electrostatic force of attraction
    {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=\frac{k z e \cdot e}{r^2}{/tex}
    {tex}r=\frac{k z e^2}{m v^2}{/tex}
    {tex}r=\frac{k z e^2}{m } \times \frac{(2 \pi)^2}{n^2 h^2} m^2r^2{/tex}
    {tex}r=\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 k z e^2}{/tex} [r {tex}\propto{/tex} n2]
  2. {tex}E=\frac{-13.6}{n^2} \mathrm{ev}^v{/tex}
    So, if n increases magnitude of energy decreases but due to -ve sign energy increase
    at {tex}\infty{/tex} E = 0

subject: Physics
question_html:
An electron in a hydrogen atom makes transitions from orbits of higher energies to orbits of lower energies.

  1. When will such transitions result in (a) Lyman (b) Balmer series?
  2. Find the ratio of the longest wavelength in Lyman series to the shortest wavelength in Balmer series.
question_text: An electron in a hydrogen atom makes transitions from orbits of higher energies to orbits of lower energies. When will such transitions result in (a) Lyman (b) Balmer series? Find the ratio of the longest wavelength in Lyman series to the shortest wavelength in Balmer series.
explanation: Transition result in Lyman series if electron will jump from a higher energy orbit to n = 1 orbit Transition result in Balmer series if electron will jump from a higher energy orbit to n = 2 orbit Longest wavelength in Lyman Series {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R \cdot\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]=\frac{3 R}{4}{/tex} Shorted wavelength in Balmer Series {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_S}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{R}{4}{/tex} Ratio {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_S}=\frac{4 }{3 R} \times \frac{R}{4}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution: Transition result in Lyman series if electron will jump from a higher energy orbit to n = 1 orbit Transition result in Balmer series if electron will jump from a higher energy orbit to n = 2 orbit Longest wavelength in Lyman Series {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R \cdot\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]=\frac{3 R}{4}{/tex} Shorted wavelength in Balmer Series {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_S}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{R}{4}{/tex} Ratio {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_S}=\frac{4 }{3 R} \times \frac{R}{4}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Transition result in Lyman series if electron will jump from a higher energy orbit to n = 1 orbit
    Transition result in Balmer series if electron will jump from a higher energy orbit to n = 2 orbit
  2. Longest wavelength in Lyman Series
    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R \cdot\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]=\frac{3 R}{4}{/tex}
    Shorted wavelength in Balmer Series
    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_S}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{R}{4}{/tex}
    Ratio {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_S}=\frac{4 }{3 R} \times \frac{R}{4}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

question_html: Identify the transition of electron in Bohr model which gives rise to (i) the maximum, and (ii) the minimum wavelength in Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum.
question_text: Identify the transition of electron in Bohr model which gives rise to (i) the maximum, and (ii) the minimum wavelength in Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum.
explanation: For Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum {tex}\frac {1}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right){/tex} For maximum wavelength: transition of electron is from n = 3 to n = 2 For maximum wavelength: transition of electron is from n = {tex}\infty{/tex} to n = 2
solution: For Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum {tex}\frac {1}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right){/tex} For maximum wavelength: transition of electron is from n = 3 to n = 2 For maximum wavelength: transition of electron is from n = {tex}\infty{/tex} to n = 2
solution_html:
For Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum
{tex}\frac {1}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right){/tex}

  1. For maximum wavelength: transition of electron is from n = 3 to n = 2
  2. For maximum wavelength: transition of electron is from n = {tex}\infty{/tex} to n = 2

question_html: Use Bohr's postulate to prove that the radius of nth orbit in a hydrogen atom is proportional to n2.
question_text: Use Bohr's postulate to prove that the radius of n th orbit in a hydrogen atom is proportional to n 2 .
explanation: The necessary centripetal force for the rotation of electron is supplied by the electrostatic force between the electron and nucleus. {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\right)\left(\frac{e^2}{r^2}\right){/tex} [putting Z = 1] Or, {tex}m v^2=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r} {/tex} ...(i) From Bohr's theory, {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}{/tex} Putting in equation (i) {tex}m\left(\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}\right)^2=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} Or, {tex}r=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2}{/tex} In general, {tex}r_n=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r_n \propto n^2{/tex}
solution: The necessary centripetal force for the rotation of electron is supplied by the electrostatic force between the electron and nucleus. {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\right)\left(\frac{e^2}{r^2}\right){/tex} [putting Z = 1] Or, {tex}m v^2=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r} {/tex} ...(i) From Bohr's theory, {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}{/tex} Putting in equation (i) {tex}m\left(\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}\right)^2=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} Or, {tex}r=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2}{/tex} In general, {tex}r_n=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r_n \propto n^2{/tex}
solution_html:
The necessary centripetal force for the rotation of electron is supplied by the electrostatic force between the electron and nucleus.
{tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\right)\left(\frac{e^2}{r^2}\right){/tex} [putting Z = 1]
Or, {tex}m v^2=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r} {/tex} ...(i)
From Bohr's theory,
{tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}{/tex}
Putting in equation (i)
{tex}m\left(\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}\right)^2=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex}
Or, {tex}r=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2}{/tex}
In general, {tex}r_n=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r_n \propto n^2{/tex}

question_html:
1. In a Geiger-Marsden experiment, find the distance of closest approach to the gold nucleus (mass no. = 79) of a 7.7 Mev {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle before it comes momentarily to rest and reverses its direction. Why is it different from actual radius of gold nucleus?
  2. Plot a graph between number of scattered {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles detected in gold foil experiment and angle of scattering. What is the main assumption in plotting this graph?
question_text: In a Geiger-Marsden experiment, find the distance of closest approach to the gold nucleus (mass no. = 79) of a 7.7 Mev {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle before it comes momentarily to rest and reverses its direction. Why is it different from actual radius of gold nucleus? Plot a graph between number of scattered {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles detected in gold foil experiment and angle of scattering. What is the main assumption in plotting this graph?
explanation: E k = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o}{/tex} {tex}\frac{(2 e)(z e)}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 z e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_o d}{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{2 X\left(9.0 \times 10^9\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2 X 79}{1.2 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 30.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m d = 30 fm = 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m Kinetic energy of α particle is not sufficient to touch the nucleus. Radius of gold nucleus is 6 fm << 30 fm because α- particle does not collide with gold nuclei and therefore distance of closest approach is less than radius of gold nuclei. Atom has a small dense, positively charged nucleus
solution: E k = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o}{/tex} {tex}\frac{(2 e)(z e)}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 z e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_o d}{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{2 X\left(9.0 \times 10^9\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2 X 79}{1.2 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 30.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m d = 30 fm = 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 m Kinetic energy of α particle is not sufficient to touch the nucleus. Radius of gold nucleus is 6 fm << 30 fm because α- particle does not collide with gold nuclei and therefore distance of closest approach is less than radius of gold nuclei. Atom has a small dense, positively charged nucleus
solution_html:
1. Ek = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_o}{/tex} {tex}\frac{(2 e)(z e)}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 z e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_o d}{/tex}
    d = {tex}\frac{2 X\left(9.0 \times 10^9\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2 X 79}{1.2 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 30.4 {tex}\times{/tex}10-15 m
    d = 30 fm = 30 {tex}\times{/tex}10-15 m
  2. Kinetic energy of α particle is not sufficient to touch the nucleus.
    Radius of gold nucleus is 6 fm << 30 fm because α- particle does not collide with gold nuclei and therefore distance of closest approach is less than radius of gold nuclei.

    Atom has a small dense, positively charged nucleus

question_html: Using Bohr’s atomic model, derive the expression for the radius of nth orbit of the revolving electron in a hydrogen atom.
question_text: Using Bohr’s atomic model, derive the expression for the radius of n th orbit of the revolving electron in a hydrogen atom.
explanation: The electrostatic force of attraction between nucleus and the electron is F = {tex}\frac{\text {KZe.e}}{r^{2}}{/tex} F = {tex}\frac{K Z e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} To keep the electron in its orbit, the centripetal force on the electron must be equal to the electrostatic attraction. Therefore, {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{K Z e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{K Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}{/tex} ...(i) m = mass of electron, v = speed of electron According to Bohr's quantisation condition for angular momentum, L = mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii) v = {tex}\frac{2 \pi K Z e^{2}}{n h}{/tex} Putting the value of v in eq (ii), we get r = {tex}\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m K Z e^{2}}{/tex} put Z=1 r = {tex}n^2h^2\over 4\pi^2m Ke^2{/tex} thus r is proportional to n 2
solution: The electrostatic force of attraction between nucleus and the electron is F = {tex}\frac{\text {KZe.e}}{r^{2}}{/tex} F = {tex}\frac{K Z e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} To keep the electron in its orbit, the centripetal force on the electron must be equal to the electrostatic attraction. Therefore, {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{K Z e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{K Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}{/tex} ...(i) m = mass of electron, v = speed of electron According to Bohr's quantisation condition for angular momentum, L = mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii) v = {tex}\frac{2 \pi K Z e^{2}}{n h}{/tex} Putting the value of v in eq (ii), we get r = {tex}\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m K Z e^{2}}{/tex} put Z=1 r = {tex}n^2h^2\over 4\pi^2m Ke^2{/tex} thus r is proportional to n 2
solution_html:
The electrostatic force of attraction between nucleus and the electron is

F = {tex}\frac{\text {KZe.e}}{r^{2}}{/tex} 
F = {tex}\frac{K Z e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} 
To keep the electron in its orbit, the centripetal force on the electron must be equal to the electrostatic attraction. Therefore,
{tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{K Z e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{K Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}{/tex} ...(i)
m = mass of electron, v = speed of electron
According to Bohr's quantisation condition for angular momentum,
L = mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} 
r = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii)
v = {tex}\frac{2 \pi K Z e^{2}}{n h}{/tex} 
Putting the value of v in eq (ii), we get
r = {tex}\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m K Z e^{2}}{/tex}put Z=1 

r = {tex}n^2h^2\over 4\pi^2m Ke^2{/tex}

thus r is proportional to n2

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State the postulates of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom and derive the expression for Bohr radius.
  2. Find the ratio of the longest and the shortest wavelengths amongst the spectral lines of Balmer series in the spectrum of hydrogen atom.
question_text: State the postulates of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom and derive the expression for Bohr radius. Find the ratio of the longest and the shortest wavelengths amongst the spectral lines of Balmer series in the spectrum of hydrogen atom.
explanation: Postulates of Bohr Model of Hydrogen atom: Postulate - I: The electrons revolve in a circular orbit around the nucleus. The electrostatic force of attraction between the positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons provide necessary centripetal force for circular motion. Postulate - II: The electrons can revolve only in certain selected orbits in which angular momentum of electrons is equal to the integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} , where h is Planck's constant. These orbits are known as stationary or permissible orbits. The electrons do not radiate energy while revolving in these orbits. Postulate - III: When an electron jumps from higher energy orbit to lower energy orbit, energy is radiated in the form of a quantum or photon of energy h v, which is equal to the difference of the energies of the electron in the two orbits. Expression for Bohr radius: Let us consider m = Mass of an electron r = Radius of the circular orbit in which the electron is revolving v = Speed of electron -e = Charge of electron From 1 st postulate Centripetal force = Electrostatic force {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r} = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore v^2 = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{m r} {/tex} ...(i) From 2 nd postulate or, mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} or, v = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}{/tex} or, {tex}v^2 = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r^2}{/tex} ...(ii) Comparing eqns (i) and (ii), {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{m r} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r^2}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{m r} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Bohr radius, r = {tex}r=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2}{/tex} Shortest wavelength in Balmer series: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_S} = R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda_S = \frac{4}{R}{/tex} Longest wavelength in Balmer series: {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda_L=\frac{36}{5 R}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{\lambda_L}{\lambda_S}=\frac{\frac{36}{5 R}}{\frac{4}{R}}=\frac{9}{5}{/tex}
solution: Postulates of Bohr Model of Hydrogen atom: Postulate - I: The electrons revolve in a circular orbit around the nucleus. The electrostatic force of attraction between the positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons provide necessary centripetal force for circular motion. Postulate - II: The electrons can revolve only in certain selected orbits in which angular momentum of electrons is equal to the integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} , where h is Planck's constant. These orbits are known as stationary or permissible orbits. The electrons do not radiate energy while revolving in these orbits. Postulate - III: When an electron jumps from higher energy orbit to lower energy orbit, energy is radiated in the form of a quantum or photon of energy h v, which is equal to the difference of the energies of the electron in the two orbits. Expression for Bohr radius: Let us consider m = Mass of an electron r = Radius of the circular orbit in which the electron is revolving v = Speed of electron -e = Charge of electron From 1 st postulate Centripetal force = Electrostatic force {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r} = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore v^2 = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{m r} {/tex} ...(i) From 2 nd postulate or, mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} or, v = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}{/tex} or, {tex}v^2 = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r^2}{/tex} ...(ii) Comparing eqns (i) and (ii), {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{m r} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r^2}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{m r} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Bohr radius, r = {tex}r=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2}{/tex} Shortest wavelength in Balmer series: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_S} = R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda_S = \frac{4}{R}{/tex} Longest wavelength in Balmer series: {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda_L=\frac{36}{5 R}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{\lambda_L}{\lambda_S}=\frac{\frac{36}{5 R}}{\frac{4}{R}}=\frac{9}{5}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Postulates of Bohr Model of Hydrogen atom:
    Postulate - I: The electrons revolve in a circular orbit around the nucleus. The electrostatic force of attraction between the positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons provide necessary centripetal force for circular motion.
    Postulate - II: The electrons can revolve only in certain selected orbits in which angular momentum of electrons is equal to the integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}, where h is Planck's constant. These orbits are known as stationary or permissible orbits. The electrons do not radiate energy while revolving in these orbits.
    Postulate - III: When an electron jumps from higher energy orbit to lower energy orbit, energy is radiated in the form of a quantum or photon of energy h v, which is equal to the difference of the energies of the electron in the two orbits. Expression for Bohr radius:
    Let us consider
    m = Mass of an electron
    r = Radius of the circular orbit in which the electron is revolving
    v = Speed of electron
    -e = Charge of electron
    From 1st postulate
    Centripetal force = Electrostatic force
    {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r} = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r^2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore v^2 = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{m r} {/tex} ...(i)
    From 2nd postulate
    or, mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    or, v = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}{/tex}
    or, {tex}v^2 = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r^2}{/tex} ...(ii)
    Comparing eqns (i) and (ii),
    {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{m r} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r^2}{/tex}
    or, {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{m r} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r^2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Bohr radius, r = {tex}r=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2}{/tex}
  2. Shortest wavelength in Balmer series:
    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_S} = R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \lambda_S = \frac{4}{R}{/tex}
    Longest wavelength in Balmer series:
    {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \lambda_L=\frac{36}{5 R}{/tex}
    So, {tex}\frac{\lambda_L}{\lambda_S}=\frac{\frac{36}{5 R}}{\frac{4}{R}}=\frac{9}{5}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Write the shortcomings of Rutherford atomic model. Explain how these were overcome by the postulates of Bohr's atomic model.
question_text: Write the shortcomings of Rutherford atomic model. Explain how these were overcome by the postulates of Bohr's atomic model.
explanation: Shortcomings of Rutherford atomic model: Rutherford proposed planetary model of an atom in which electrons revolve round the nucleus. An electron revolving round the nucleus has an acceleration directed towards the nucleus. Such accelerated electron must radiate electromagnetic radiation. But, if an revolving electron radiates energy, the total energy of the system must decrease. In such situation, the electron must come closer to the nucleus and hit the nucleus. Also, the radiation spectrum of emitted electromagnetic waves should be continuous. However, this does not happen in an atom. Atom is not unstable and the spectrum is not continuous. Rutherford atomic model cannot explain these two observations. These are the shortcomings of Rutherford Atomic Model. To overcome this discrepancy, Neils Bohr put forward three postulates combining classical Physics and Planck's quantum hypothesis. Bohr's 1st postulate provides stability to the atomic model. Bohr's 2nd postulate provides justification that electrons may revolve in stationary orbit. Bohr's 3rd postulate provides the explanation of line spectrum.
solution: Shortcomings of Rutherford atomic model: Rutherford proposed planetary model of an atom in which electrons revolve round the nucleus. An electron revolving round the nucleus has an acceleration directed towards the nucleus. Such accelerated electron must radiate electromagnetic radiation. But, if an revolving electron radiates energy, the total energy of the system must decrease. In such situation, the electron must come closer to the nucleus and hit the nucleus. Also, the radiation spectrum of emitted electromagnetic waves should be continuous. However, this does not happen in an atom. Atom is not unstable and the spectrum is not continuous. Rutherford atomic model cannot explain these two observations. These are the shortcomings of Rutherford Atomic Model. To overcome this discrepancy, Neils Bohr put forward three postulates combining classical Physics and Planck's quantum hypothesis. Bohr's 1st postulate provides stability to the atomic model. Bohr's 2nd postulate provides justification that electrons may revolve in stationary orbit. Bohr's 3rd postulate provides the explanation of line spectrum.
solution_html:
Shortcomings of Rutherford atomic model:
Rutherford proposed planetary model of an atom in which electrons revolve round the nucleus.
An electron revolving round the nucleus has an acceleration directed towards the nucleus.
Such accelerated electron must radiate electromagnetic radiation.
But, if an revolving electron radiates energy, the total energy of the system must decrease. In such situation, the electron must come closer to the nucleus and hit the nucleus. Also, the radiation spectrum of emitted electromagnetic waves should be continuous.
However, this does not happen in an atom. Atom is not unstable and the spectrum is not continuous. Rutherford atomic model cannot explain these two observations. These are the shortcomings of Rutherford Atomic Model.
To overcome this discrepancy, Neils Bohr put forward three postulates combining classical Physics and Planck's quantum hypothesis.
Bohr's 1st postulate provides stability to the atomic model.
Bohr's 2nd postulate provides justification that electrons may revolve in stationary orbit.
Bohr's 3rd postulate provides the explanation of line spectrum.

subject: Physics
question_html: What do you mean by wave nature of an electron? How was quantisation of angular momentum of the orbiting electron in Bohr’s model of hydrogen atom explained by de Broglie hypothesis?
question_text: What do you mean by wave nature of an electron? How was quantisation of angular momentum of the orbiting electron in Bohr’s model of hydrogen atom explained by de Broglie hypothesis?
explanation: Electrons can exhibit wavelike behavior by showing an interference pattern for electrons travelling through a regular atomic pattern in a crystal. The de Broglie's wavelength {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m V}{/tex} ...(i) For stationary orbit {tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex} ...(ii) Substituting the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} from (i) and (ii) {tex}2 \pi r=\frac{n h}{m V}{/tex} {tex}m V r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} mVr = L (Orbital angular momentum) {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
solution: Electrons can exhibit wavelike behavior by showing an interference pattern for electrons travelling through a regular atomic pattern in a crystal. The de Broglie's wavelength {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m V}{/tex} ...(i) For stationary orbit {tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex} ...(ii) Substituting the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} from (i) and (ii) {tex}2 \pi r=\frac{n h}{m V}{/tex} {tex}m V r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} mVr = L (Orbital angular momentum) {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
solution_html:
Electrons can exhibit wavelike behavior by showing an interference pattern for electrons travelling through a regular atomic pattern in a crystal.
The de Broglie's wavelength
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m V}{/tex} ...(i)
For stationary orbit
{tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex} ...(ii)
Substituting the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} from (i) and (ii)
{tex}2 \pi r=\frac{n h}{m V}{/tex}
{tex}m V r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
mVr = L (Orbital angular momentum)
{tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}

question_html: Using Bohr’s atomic model, derive the expression for the velocity of electron revolving in the nth orbit of hydrogen atom.
question_text: Using Bohr’s atomic model, derive the expression for the velocity of electron revolving in the n th orbit of hydrogen atom.
explanation: Centripetal force is provided by electrostatic force F c = F e {tex}\frac{m_e v_n{ }^2}{r_n}=\frac{k Z e^2}{r_n{ }^2}{/tex} but m e v n r n = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} v n = {tex}\frac{2 \pi k Z e^2}{n h}{/tex} For H atom Z = 1 v n = {tex}\frac{2 \pi k e^2}{n h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^2}{2 \epsilon_o n h}{/tex} hence, the velocity of electron revolving in the n th orbit of hydrogen atom is {tex}\frac{e^2}{2 \epsilon_o n h}{/tex} .
solution: Centripetal force is provided by electrostatic force F c = F e {tex}\frac{m_e v_n{ }^2}{r_n}=\frac{k Z e^2}{r_n{ }^2}{/tex} but m e v n r n = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} v n = {tex}\frac{2 \pi k Z e^2}{n h}{/tex} For H atom Z = 1 v n = {tex}\frac{2 \pi k e^2}{n h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^2}{2 \epsilon_o n h}{/tex} hence, the velocity of electron revolving in the n th orbit of hydrogen atom is {tex}\frac{e^2}{2 \epsilon_o n h}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Centripetal force is provided by electrostatic force
Fc = Fe
{tex}\frac{m_e v_n{ }^2}{r_n}=\frac{k Z e^2}{r_n{ }^2}{/tex}
but
mevnrn = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
vn = {tex}\frac{2 \pi k Z e^2}{n h}{/tex}
For H atom Z = 1
vn = {tex}\frac{2 \pi k e^2}{n h}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{e^2}{2 \epsilon_o n h}{/tex}
hence, the velocity of electron revolving in the nth orbit of hydrogen atom is {tex}\frac{e^2}{2 \epsilon_o n h}{/tex}.

question_text: A hydrogen atom is in its third excited state. How many spectral lines can be emitted by it before coming to the ground state? Show these transitions in the energy level diagram. In which of the above transitions will the spectral line of shortest wavelength be emitted?
explanation: The hydrogen atom is in its third excited state i.e., it is in n = 4 state. Before coming to ground state (n = 1) the total number of spectral lines is n(n - 1) 2 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 32 = 6. The transitions corresponding to these six lines are shown in the energy level diagram here. The spectral line of shortest wavelength in n = 4 to n = 1.

question_html: Use Bohr’s model of hydrogen atom to obtain the relationship between the angular momentum and the magnetic moment of the revolving electron.
question_text: Use Bohr’s model of hydrogen atom to obtain the relationship between the angular momentum and the magnetic moment of the revolving electron.
explanation: According to Bohr’s model of atom L = Angular momentum of electron mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = Magnetic moment of atom = current {tex}\times{/tex} area of the orbit {tex}\mu{/tex} = |e| {tex}\times{/tex} v {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{|e| v r}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{L}{\mu}=\frac{m v r \times 2}{|e| v r}=\frac{2 m}{|e|}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{|e|}{2 m} L{/tex}
solution: According to Bohr’s model of atom L = Angular momentum of electron mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = Magnetic moment of atom = current {tex}\times{/tex} area of the orbit {tex}\mu{/tex} = |e| {tex}\times{/tex} v {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{|e| v r}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{L}{\mu}=\frac{m v r \times 2}{|e| v r}=\frac{2 m}{|e|}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{|e|}{2 m} L{/tex}
solution_html:
According to Bohr’s model of atom
L = Angular momentum of electron mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}\mu{/tex} = Magnetic moment of atom = current {tex}\times{/tex} area of the orbit
{tex}\mu{/tex} = |e| {tex}\times{/tex} v {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex}r2 = {tex}\frac{|e| v r}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{L}{\mu}=\frac{m v r \times 2}{|e| v r}=\frac{2 m}{|e|}{/tex}
{tex}\mu=\frac{|e|}{2 m} L{/tex}

question_html: Calculate the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron revolving in the first excited state of hydrogen atom. The ground state energy of the hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV.
question_text: Calculate the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron revolving in the first excited state of hydrogen atom. The ground state energy of the hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV.
explanation: Energy of the electron in the first excited state E 1 = {tex}-\frac{13.6}{2^2} e V{/tex} = -3.4 eV = -3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = -5.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Associated kinetic energy = Negative of total energy K = 5.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {h} {p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\left(2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 5.44 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{\frac {1} {2}}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{(99.008)^{\frac {1}{2}} \times 10^{-25}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.663 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m = 0.663 nm = 6.63 A o Hence, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron is 6.63 A o
solution: Energy of the electron in the first excited state E 1 = {tex}-\frac{13.6}{2^2} e V{/tex} = -3.4 eV = -3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = -5.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Associated kinetic energy = Negative of total energy K = 5.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {h} {p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\left(2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 5.44 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{\frac {1} {2}}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{(99.008)^{\frac {1}{2}} \times 10^{-25}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.663 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m = 0.663 nm = 6.63 A o Hence, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron is 6.63 A o
solution_html:
Energy of the electron in the first excited state
E1 = {tex}-\frac{13.6}{2^2} e V{/tex} = -3.4 eV
= -3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
= -5.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
Associated kinetic energy = Negative of total energy
K = 5.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {h} {p}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\left(2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 5.44 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{\frac {1} {2}}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{(99.008)^{\frac {1}{2}} \times 10^{-25}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
{tex}\approx{/tex} 0.663 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m = 0.663 nm = 6.63 Ao
Hence, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron is 6.63 Ao

subject: Physics
question_html: Calculate the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the 2nd excited state of hydrogen atom. The ground state energy of the hydrogen atom is 13.6 eV.
question_text: Calculate the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the 2 nd excited state of hydrogen atom. The ground state energy of the hydrogen atom is 13.6 eV.
explanation: Energy of the electron in the second excited state = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{3^2} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = -1.51 eV Kinetic energy (K) in this state = 1.51 eV de-Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\left(2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.51 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63}{(43.97)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 10 -9 m = 1nm Hence, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the 2 nd excited state of hydrogen is 1nm.
solution: Energy of the electron in the second excited state = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{3^2} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = -1.51 eV Kinetic energy (K) in this state = 1.51 eV de-Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\left(2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.51 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63}{(43.97)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 10 -9 m = 1nm Hence, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the 2 nd excited state of hydrogen is 1nm.
solution_html:
Energy of the electron in the second excited state = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{3^2} \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
= -1.51 eV
Kinetic energy (K) in this state = 1.51 eV
de-Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\left(2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.51 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.63}{(43.97)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
= 10-9 m = 1nm
Hence, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the 2nd excited state of hydrogen is 1nm.

question_text: Calculate the orbital period of the electron in the first excited state of hydrogen atom.
explanation: {tex}\begin{array}{l}\text{ We know that the orbital period, }T=\frac{2\pi r}v\\r_1=0.53\times10^{-10}\mathrm m\\T_1=\frac{2\pi\times0.53\times10^{-10}}{2.19\times10^6}=1.52\times10^{-16}\mathrm s\\r_2=4r_1\text{ and }v_2=\frac12v_1\\T_2=8T_1\\\quad=8\times1.52\times10^{-16}\end{array}{/tex}

question_html: A hydrogen atom in the ground state is excited by an electron beam of 12.5 eV energy. Find out the maximum number of lines emitted by the atom from its excited state.
question_text: A hydrogen atom in the ground state is excited by an electron beam of 12.5 eV energy. Find out the maximum number of lines emitted by the atom from its excited state.
explanation: Energy in ground state, E 1 = - 13.6eV Energy supplied = 12.5eV Energy in excited state = - 13.6 + 12.5 = - 1.1eV But, E n = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 1.1 = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n 2 = 12.3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 3.5 Hence, n = 3 Maximum number of lines = 3
solution: Energy in ground state, E 1 = - 13.6eV Energy supplied = 12.5eV Energy in excited state = - 13.6 + 12.5 = - 1.1eV But, E n = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 1.1 = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n 2 = 12.3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 3.5 Hence, n = 3 Maximum number of lines = 3
solution_html:
Energy in ground state, E1 = - 13.6eV
Energy supplied = 12.5eV
Energy in excited state = - 13.6 + 12.5 = - 1.1eV
But, En = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 1.1 = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n2 = 12.3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 3.5
Hence, n = 3
Maximum number of lines = 3

question_html: A photon emitted during the de-excitation of electron from a state n to the first excited state in a hydrogen atom, irradiates a metallic cathode of work function 2 eV, in a photo cell, with a stopping potential of 0.55 V. Obtain the value of the quantum number of the state n.
question_text: A photon emitted during the de-excitation of electron from a state n to the first excited state in a hydrogen atom, irradiates a metallic cathode of work function 2 eV, in a photo cell, with a stopping potential of 0.55 V. Obtain the value of the quantum number of the state n.
explanation: Given: Work function {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2eV, Stopping potential V o = 0.55 V Thus, Stopping potential energy = eV o = 0.55 eV = K.E. Applying Einstein photoelectric equation, E = {tex}\phi{/tex} + K.E h {tex}\nu{/tex} = 2 + 0.55 = 2.55 eV {tex}\begin{array}{l}\text{ Also, }E_n=-\frac{13.6}{n^2},\text{ and we know }E_2=\frac{-13.6}{2^2}=-3.4eV\\\text{ The energy difference }\Delta E=-3.4-(-2.55)\mathrm{eV}=-0.85\mathrm{eV}\\\therefore\quad\frac{-13.6}{n^2}=-0.85\\\Rightarrow n=4\end{array}{/tex}
solution: Given: Work function {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2eV, Stopping potential V o = 0.55 V Thus, Stopping potential energy = eV o = 0.55 eV = K.E. Applying Einstein photoelectric equation, E = {tex}\phi{/tex} + K.E h {tex}\nu{/tex} = 2 + 0.55 = 2.55 eV {tex}\begin{array}{l}\text{ Also, }E_n=-\frac{13.6}{n^2},\text{ and we know }E_2=\frac{-13.6}{2^2}=-3.4eV\\\text{ The energy difference }\Delta E=-3.4-(-2.55)\mathrm{eV}=-0.85\mathrm{eV}\\\therefore\quad\frac{-13.6}{n^2}=-0.85\\\Rightarrow n=4\end{array}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Work function {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2eV, Stopping potential Vo = 0.55 V
Thus, Stopping potential energy = eVo = 0.55 eV = K.E.
Applying Einstein photoelectric equation,
E = {tex}\phi{/tex} + K.E
h{tex}\nu{/tex} = 2 + 0.55 = 2.55 eV
{tex}\begin{array}{l}\text{ Also, }E_n=-\frac{13.6}{n^2},\text{ and we know }E_2=\frac{-13.6}{2^2}=-3.4eV\\\text{ The energy difference }\Delta E=-3.4-(-2.55)\mathrm{eV}=-0.85\mathrm{eV}\\\therefore\quad\frac{-13.6}{n^2}=-0.85\\\Rightarrow n=4\end{array}{/tex}

question_html: Obtain Bohr’s quantisation condition for angular momentum of electron orbiting in nth orbit in hydrogen atom on the basis of the wave picture of an electron using de Broglie hypothesis.
question_text: Obtain Bohr’s quantisation condition for angular momentum of electron orbiting in n th orbit in hydrogen atom on the basis of the wave picture of an electron using de Broglie hypothesis.
explanation: Let {tex}\lambda{/tex} be the deBroglie wavelength associated with electron orbiting (with speed v) in the with n th orbit (of radius r) in hydrogen atom. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{mv}{/tex} Now, 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{nh}{mv}{/tex} Or mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{mv}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} L = mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\lambda{/tex} be the deBroglie wavelength associated with electron orbiting (with speed v) in the with n th orbit (of radius r) in hydrogen atom. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{mv}{/tex} Now, 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{nh}{mv}{/tex} Or mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{mv}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} L = mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\lambda{/tex} be the deBroglie wavelength associated with electron orbiting (with speed v) in the with nth orbit (of radius r) in hydrogen atom.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{mv}{/tex}
Now, 2 {tex}\pi{/tex}r = n{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{nh}{mv}{/tex}
Or mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{mv}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} L = mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: State Bohr's quantization condition of angular momentum. Calculate the shortest wavelength of the Bracket series and state to which part of the electromagnetic spectrum does it belong.
question_text: State Bohr's quantization condition of angular momentum. Calculate the shortest wavelength of the Bracket series and state to which part of the electromagnetic spectrum does it belong.
explanation: Bohr’s quantization condition of angular momentum: According to Bohr, the electron can revolve in certain discrete orbits called stationary orbits. The total angular momentum (L) of the revolving electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} . Thus, angular momentum, L = mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex} where n = 1, 2, 3.... Brackett series is obtained when an electron jumps to fourth orbit (n 1 = 4) from any outer orbit (n 2 = 5, 6, 7.....) of the hydrogen atom. The wavelength of a spectral line is given as {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} = RZ {tex}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex} where R is Rydberg’s constant (1.097 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -1 ) and Z is the atomic number (Z = 1 for hydrogen atom). For Brackett series, n 1 = 4 and n 2 = 5, 6, 7… Therefore, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex} For the shortest wavelength, n 2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} . Therefore, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]=R\left(\frac{1}{4^{2}}-0\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}= \frac{R}{16}=\frac{1.097 \times 10^{7}}{16}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{16}{1.097 \times 10^{7}}{/tex} = 14.585 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1458.5 nm. The shortest wavelength of the Brackett series is 1458.5 nm. This wavelength belongs to infrared range of electromagnetic spectrum.
solution: Bohr’s quantization condition of angular momentum: According to Bohr, the electron can revolve in certain discrete orbits called stationary orbits. The total angular momentum (L) of the revolving electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} . Thus, angular momentum, L = mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex} where n = 1, 2, 3.... Brackett series is obtained when an electron jumps to fourth orbit (n 1 = 4) from any outer orbit (n 2 = 5, 6, 7.....) of the hydrogen atom. The wavelength of a spectral line is given as {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} = RZ {tex}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex} where R is Rydberg’s constant (1.097 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -1 ) and Z is the atomic number (Z = 1 for hydrogen atom). For Brackett series, n 1 = 4 and n 2 = 5, 6, 7… Therefore, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex} For the shortest wavelength, n 2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} . Therefore, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]=R\left(\frac{1}{4^{2}}-0\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}= \frac{R}{16}=\frac{1.097 \times 10^{7}}{16}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{16}{1.097 \times 10^{7}}{/tex} = 14.585 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1458.5 nm. The shortest wavelength of the Brackett series is 1458.5 nm. This wavelength belongs to infrared range of electromagnetic spectrum.
solution_html:
Bohr’s quantization condition of angular momentum: According to Bohr, the electron can revolve in certain discrete orbits called stationary orbits. The total angular momentum (L) of the revolving electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}.
Thus, angular momentum, L = mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex}
where n = 1, 2, 3....
Brackett series is obtained when an electron jumps to fourth orbit (n1 = 4) from any outer orbit (n2 = 5, 6, 7.....) of the hydrogen atom.
The wavelength of a spectral line is given as
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} = RZ{tex}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex}
where R is Rydberg’s constant (1.097 {tex}\times{/tex}107 m-1) and Z is the atomic number (Z = 1 for hydrogen atom). For Brackett series, n1 = 4 and n2 = 5, 6, 7…
Therefore,
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex}
For the shortest wavelength, n2 = {tex}\infty{/tex}. Therefore,
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]=R\left(\frac{1}{4^{2}}-0\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}= \frac{R}{16}=\frac{1.097 \times 10^{7}}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{16}{1.097 \times 10^{7}}{/tex} = 14.585 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1458.5 nm.
The shortest wavelength of the Brackett series is 1458.5 nm.
This wavelength belongs to infrared range of electromagnetic spectrum.

subject: Physics
question_html: Prove that the magnetic moment of the electron revolving around a nucleus in an orbit of radius r with orbital speed v is equal to {tex}\frac{evr}{2}{/tex}. Hence using Bohr’s postulate of quantization of angular momentum, deduce the expression for the magnetic moment of hydrogen atom in the ground state.
question_text: Prove that the magnetic moment of the electron revolving around a nucleus in an orbit of radius r with orbital speed v is equal to {tex}\frac{evr}{2}{/tex} . Hence using Bohr’s postulate of quantization of angular momentum, deduce the expression for the magnetic moment of hydrogen atom in the ground state.
explanation: An electron in a circular motion around the nucleus constitutes a current loop which behaves like a magnetic dipole. Current associated with the revolving electron, {tex}\mathrm{I}=\frac{e}{\mathrm{T}}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{e}{2 \pi r} v{/tex} Magnetic moment of the loop, {tex}\mu{/tex} = IA {tex}\mu=\mathrm{I} \mathrm{A}=\frac{e v}{2 \pi r} \pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{e v r}{2}{/tex} Apply Bohr's postulate for angular momentum, mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{2\pi}{/tex} For ground state, n = 1 mvr {tex}=\frac{h}{2 \pi} \ or\; vr=\frac{h}{2 \pi m}{/tex} ...(i) Now, magnetic moment, {tex}\mu=\frac{e v r}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{e}{2}\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi m}\right){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mu=\frac{h}{4 \pi} \frac{e}{m}{/tex}
solution: An electron in a circular motion around the nucleus constitutes a current loop which behaves like a magnetic dipole. Current associated with the revolving electron, {tex}\mathrm{I}=\frac{e}{\mathrm{T}}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{e}{2 \pi r} v{/tex} Magnetic moment of the loop, {tex}\mu{/tex} = IA {tex}\mu=\mathrm{I} \mathrm{A}=\frac{e v}{2 \pi r} \pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{e v r}{2}{/tex} Apply Bohr's postulate for angular momentum, mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{2\pi}{/tex} For ground state, n = 1 mvr {tex}=\frac{h}{2 \pi} \ or\; vr=\frac{h}{2 \pi m}{/tex} ...(i) Now, magnetic moment, {tex}\mu=\frac{e v r}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{e}{2}\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi m}\right){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mu=\frac{h}{4 \pi} \frac{e}{m}{/tex}
solution_html:
An electron in a circular motion around the nucleus constitutes a current loop which behaves like a magnetic dipole. Current associated with the revolving electron,
{tex}\mathrm{I}=\frac{e}{\mathrm{T}}{/tex}
and {tex}\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{e}{2 \pi r} v{/tex}

Magnetic moment of the loop, {tex}\mu{/tex} = IA
{tex}\mu=\mathrm{I} \mathrm{A}=\frac{e v}{2 \pi r} \pi r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{e v r}{2}{/tex}
Apply Bohr's postulate for angular momentum,
mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{2\pi}{/tex}
For ground state, n = 1
mvr {tex}=\frac{h}{2 \pi} \ or\; vr=\frac{h}{2 \pi m}{/tex} ...(i)
Now, magnetic moment,
{tex}\mu=\frac{e v r}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{e}{2}\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi m}\right){/tex}
Thus, {tex}\mu=\frac{h}{4 \pi} \frac{e}{m}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Explain briefly how Rutherford scattering of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle by a target nucleus can provide information on the size of nucleus.
  2. Show that density of nucleus is independent of its mass number A.
question_text: Explain briefly how Rutherford scattering of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle by a target nucleus can provide information on the size of nucleus. Show that density of nucleus is independent of its mass number A.
explanation: Many of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles passed through the foil. A few particles were deflected by more than 90 o angle. Rutherford argued that to deflect the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles backward, those must experienced a large repulsive force. Thus he concluded that most of the part of an atom is the empty space and its positive charge is concentrated tightly at its centre and its size is very small as compared to the size of atom. Radius of the nucleus of mass number A, R = R 0 A {tex}{\frac 1 3}{/tex} , where, R 0 is constant. Volume of the nucleus, V = {tex} \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R_0 A^{\frac1 3}\right)^3{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi A R_0{ }^3{/tex} Density {tex}(\rho){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}=\frac{m A}{\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3 A\right)} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 m}{4 \pi R_0{ }^3}{/tex} i.e., Density is independent of mass number A.
solution: Many of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles passed through the foil. A few particles were deflected by more than 90 o angle. Rutherford argued that to deflect the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles backward, those must experienced a large repulsive force. Thus he concluded that most of the part of an atom is the empty space and its positive charge is concentrated tightly at its centre and its size is very small as compared to the size of atom. Radius of the nucleus of mass number A, R = R 0 A {tex}{\frac 1 3}{/tex} , where, R 0 is constant. Volume of the nucleus, V = {tex} \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R_0 A^{\frac1 3}\right)^3{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi A R_0{ }^3{/tex} Density {tex}(\rho){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}=\frac{m A}{\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3 A\right)} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 m}{4 \pi R_0{ }^3}{/tex} i.e., Density is independent of mass number A.
solution_html:
1. Many of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles passed through the foil. A few particles were deflected by more than 90o angle.
    Rutherford argued that to deflect the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles backward, those must experienced a large repulsive force.
    Thus he concluded that most of the part of an atom is the empty space and its positive charge is concentrated tightly at its centre and its size is very small as compared to the size of atom.
  2. Radius of the nucleus of mass number A, R = R0A{tex}{\frac 1 3}{/tex}, where, R0 is constant.
    Volume of the nucleus,
     V = {tex} \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R_0 A^{\frac1 3}\right)^3{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{4}{3} \pi A R_0{ }^3{/tex}
    Density {tex}(\rho){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}=\frac{m A}{\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3 A\right)} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 m}{4 \pi R_0{ }^3}{/tex}
    i.e., Density is independent of mass number A.

question_html:
Obtain the first Bohr’s radius and the ground state energy of a muonic hydrogen atom i.e. an atom where the electron is replaced by a negatively charged muon {tex}({\mu ^ - }){/tex} of mass about 207 me that orbits around a proton.
(Given for hydrogen atom, radius of first orbit and ground state energy are 0.53 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m and -13.6 eV respectively)
question_text: Obtain the first Bohr’s radius and the ground state energy of a muonic hydrogen atom i.e. an atom where the electron is replaced by a negatively charged muon {tex}({\mu ^ - }){/tex} of mass about 207 m e that orbits around a proton. (Given for hydrogen atom, radius of first orbit and ground state energy are 0.53 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m and -13.6 eV respectively)
explanation: A mounic hydrogen is the atom in which a negatively charged muon of mass about 207 m e revolves around a proton. In Bohr's atom model as, r {tex}\propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mu}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mu}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}{207 \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{1}{207}{/tex} Here r e is radius of first orbit of electron in hydrogen atom = 0.53 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} = 0.53 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m {tex}\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{\mu}}=\frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{e}}}{207}=\frac{0.53 \times 10^{-10}}{207}=2.56 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{m}{/tex} Again in Bhor's atomic model, E {tex}\propto{/tex} m {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mu}}{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mu}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{207 \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}, \mathrm{E}_{\mu}=207 \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{e}}{/tex} As ground state energy of electron in hydrogen atom is E e = -13.6 eV {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mu}{/tex} = 207(-13.6) eV = -2815.2 eV = -2.8152 KeV
solution: A mounic hydrogen is the atom in which a negatively charged muon of mass about 207 m e revolves around a proton. In Bohr's atom model as, r {tex}\propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mu}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mu}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}{207 \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{1}{207}{/tex} Here r e is radius of first orbit of electron in hydrogen atom = 0.53 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} = 0.53 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m {tex}\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{\mu}}=\frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{e}}}{207}=\frac{0.53 \times 10^{-10}}{207}=2.56 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{m}{/tex} Again in Bhor's atomic model, E {tex}\propto{/tex} m {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mu}}{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mu}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{207 \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}, \mathrm{E}_{\mu}=207 \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{e}}{/tex} As ground state energy of electron in hydrogen atom is E e = -13.6 eV {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mu}{/tex} = 207(-13.6) eV = -2815.2 eV = -2.8152 KeV
solution_html:
A mounic hydrogen is the atom in which a negatively charged muon of mass about 207 me revolves around a proton.
In Bohr's atom model as, r {tex}\propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{m}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mu}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mu}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}{207 \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{1}{207}{/tex}
Here re is radius of first orbit of electron in hydrogen atom = 0.53 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}= 0.53 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m
{tex}\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{\mu}}=\frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{e}}}{207}=\frac{0.53 \times 10^{-10}}{207}=2.56 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
Again in Bhor's atomic model,
E {tex}\propto{/tex} m
{tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mu}}{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mu}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{207 \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}, \mathrm{E}_{\mu}=207 \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{e}}{/tex}
As ground state energy of electron in hydrogen atom is Ee = -13.6 eV
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mu}{/tex}= 207(-13.6) eV = -2815.2 eV = -2.8152 KeV

question_html: An electron in a hydrogen atom in the second excited state jumps to the first and ground state of the atom. Find the ratio of the wavelengths emitted during this process.
question_text: An electron in a hydrogen atom in the second excited state jumps to the first and ground state of the atom. Find the ratio of the wavelengths emitted during this process.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\frac{(2)}{(1)}=\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{27}{5}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\frac{(2)}{(1)}=\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{27}{5}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\frac{(2)}{(1)}=\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{27}{5}{/tex}
