question_html: Draw a plot of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle scattering by a thin foil of gold to show the variation of the number of the scattered particles with scattering angle. Describe briefly how the large angle scattering explains the existence of the nucleus inside the atom. Explain with the help of impact parameter picture, how Rutherford scattering serves a powerful way to determine an upper limit on the size of the nucleus.
question_text: Draw a plot of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle scattering by a thin foil of gold to show the variation of the number of the scattered particles with scattering angle. Describe briefly how the large angle scattering explains the existence of the nucleus inside the atom. Explain with the help of impact parameter picture, how Rutherford scattering serves a powerful way to determine an upper limit on the size of the nucleus.
explanation: A typical graph of the total number of α-particles scattered at different angles, in a given interval of time, is shown in Fig. The following conclusions can be drawn from the plot: Since most of the alpha particles passed through the foil without undergoing any deflection, there must be sufficient empty space within the atom. Since few alpha particles were deflected through small angles and alpha particles were positively charged particles, these could be deflected only by some positive body present within the atom. The alpha particles deflected were those which passed very close to this positive body. Since some alpha particles were deflected back and alpha particles are heavy particle these could be deflected back only when they strike some heavier body inside the atom. Since the number of alpha particles deflected back is very-very small, this shows that the heavy body present in an atom must be occupying a very-very small volume. The small heavy positively charged body present within the atom was called nucleus. The trajectory, traced by the α-particles in the coulomb field of the target nucleus, is shown below. Here, b is the impact parameter and θ is the scattering angle. It is seen that an α-particle close to the nucleus (small impact parameter) suffers large scattering. In case of head-on collision, the impact parameter is minimum and the α-particle rebounds back (θ ≅ {tex}\pi{/tex} ). For a large impact parameter, the α-particle goes nearly undeviated and has a small deflection (θ ≅ 0). The fact that only a small fraction of the number of incident particles rebound back indicates that the number of α-particles undergoing head on collision is small. This, in turn, implies that the mass of the atom is concentrated in a small volume. Rutherford scattering therefore, is a powerful way to determine an upper limit to the size of the nucleus.
solution: A typical graph of the total number of α-particles scattered at different angles, in a given interval of time, is shown in Fig. The following conclusions can be drawn from the plot: Since most of the alpha particles passed through the foil without undergoing any deflection, there must be sufficient empty space within the atom. Since few alpha particles were deflected through small angles and alpha particles were positively charged particles, these could be deflected only by some positive body present within the atom. The alpha particles deflected were those which passed very close to this positive body. Since some alpha particles were deflected back and alpha particles are heavy particle these could be deflected back only when they strike some heavier body inside the atom. Since the number of alpha particles deflected back is very-very small, this shows that the heavy body present in an atom must be occupying a very-very small volume. The small heavy positively charged body present within the atom was called nucleus. The trajectory, traced by the α-particles in the coulomb field of the target nucleus, is shown below. Here, b is the impact parameter and θ is the scattering angle. It is seen that an α-particle close to the nucleus (small impact parameter) suffers large scattering. In case of head-on collision, the impact parameter is minimum and the α-particle rebounds back (θ ≅ {tex}\pi{/tex} ). For a large impact parameter, the α-particle goes nearly undeviated and has a small deflection (θ ≅ 0). The fact that only a small fraction of the number of incident particles rebound back indicates that the number of α-particles undergoing head on collision is small. This, in turn, implies that the mass of the atom is concentrated in a small volume. Rutherford scattering therefore, is a powerful way to determine an upper limit to the size of the nucleus.
solution_html:
A typical graph of the total number of α-particles scattered at different angles, in a given interval of time, is shown in Fig.

The following conclusions can be drawn from the plot:

  1. Since most of the alpha particles passed through the foil without undergoing any deflection, there must be sufficient empty space within the atom.
  2. Since few alpha particles were deflected through small angles and alpha particles were positively charged particles, these could be deflected only by some positive body present within the atom. The alpha particles deflected were those which passed very close to this positive body.
  3. Since some alpha particles were deflected back and alpha particles are heavy particle these could be deflected back only when they strike some heavier body inside the atom.
  4. Since the number of alpha particles deflected back is very-very small, this shows that the heavy body present in an atom must be occupying a very-very small volume. The small heavy positively charged body present within the atom was called nucleus.

The trajectory, traced by the α-particles in the coulomb field of the target nucleus, is shown below. Here, b is the impact parameter and θ is the scattering angle. 


It is seen that an α-particle close to the nucleus (small impact parameter) suffers large scattering. In case of head-on collision, the impact parameter is minimum and the α-particle rebounds back (θ ≅ {tex}\pi{/tex}). For a large impact parameter, the α-particle goes nearly undeviated and has a small deflection (θ ≅ 0).
The fact that only a small fraction of the number of incident particles rebound back indicates that the number of α-particles undergoing head on collision is small. This, in turn, implies that the mass of the atom is concentrated in a small volume. Rutherford scattering therefore, is a powerful way to determine an upper limit to the size of the nucleus.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Using Bohr’s postulates, derive the expression for the orbital period of the electron moving in the nth orbit of hydrogen atom.
  2. Write Rydberg’s formula for wavelengths of the spectral lines of hydrogen atom spectrum. Mention to which series in the emission spectrum of hydrogen, H{tex}\alpha{/tex} line belongs.
question_text: Using Bohr’s postulates, derive the expression for the orbital period of the electron moving in the n th orbit of hydrogen atom. Write Rydberg’s formula for wavelengths of the spectral lines of hydrogen atom spectrum. Mention to which series in the emission spectrum of hydrogen, H {tex}\alpha{/tex} line belongs.
explanation: Orbital Period of an electron in the hydrogen atom {tex}T=\frac{2 \pi r_n}{v_n}{/tex} ... (eq n 1) From Bohr's postulates {tex}\mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex} We have {tex}r_n=\frac{\mathrm{n}^2 \mathrm{~h}^2 \varepsilon_0}{\pi \mathrm{me}^2}{/tex} and velocity of the electron in n th orbital {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{nh}}{/tex} On substituting the values of r n and v n in equation (1) we have {tex}\mathrm{T}=\frac{4 \mathrm{n}^2 \mathrm{~h}^2 \varepsilon_0}{\mathrm{me}^4}{/tex} Rydberg's formula for wavelengths of the spectral lines {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2{ }^2}\right){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{H}_\alpha{/tex} belongs to the Balmer series .
solution: Orbital Period of an electron in the hydrogen atom {tex}T=\frac{2 \pi r_n}{v_n}{/tex} ... (eq n 1) From Bohr's postulates {tex}\mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex} We have {tex}r_n=\frac{\mathrm{n}^2 \mathrm{~h}^2 \varepsilon_0}{\pi \mathrm{me}^2}{/tex} and velocity of the electron in n th orbital {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{nh}}{/tex} On substituting the values of r n and v n in equation (1) we have {tex}\mathrm{T}=\frac{4 \mathrm{n}^2 \mathrm{~h}^2 \varepsilon_0}{\mathrm{me}^4}{/tex} Rydberg's formula for wavelengths of the spectral lines {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2{ }^2}\right){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{H}_\alpha{/tex} belongs to the Balmer series .
solution_html:
1. Orbital Period of an electron in the hydrogen atom
    {tex}T=\frac{2 \pi r_n}{v_n}{/tex} ... (eqn 1)
    From Bohr's postulates
    {tex}\mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    We have {tex}r_n=\frac{\mathrm{n}^2 \mathrm{~h}^2 \varepsilon_0}{\pi \mathrm{me}^2}{/tex}
    and velocity of the electron in nth orbital
    {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{nh}}{/tex}
    On substituting the values of rn and vn in equation (1) we have
    {tex}\mathrm{T}=\frac{4 \mathrm{n}^2 \mathrm{~h}^2 \varepsilon_0}{\mathrm{me}^4}{/tex}
  2. Rydberg's formula for wavelengths of the spectral lines
    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2{ }^2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{H}_\alpha{/tex} belongs to the Balmer series.

subject: Physics
question_html: Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the radius of the nth orbit in which the electron is revolving in hydrogen atom. How does de-Broglie's hypothesis explain the stability of hydrogen atom? Explain.
question_text: Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the radius of the n th orbit in which the electron is revolving in hydrogen atom. How does de-Broglie's hypothesis explain the stability of hydrogen atom? Explain.
explanation: {tex}\frac{m v_0^2}{r_n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{Z e^2}{r_n^2}{/tex} {tex}m v_0^2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{Z e^2}{r_n}{/tex} ...(i) according to Bohr's Postulate {tex}\mathrm{m} v_0 r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{m}^2 v_0^2 r_n^2=\frac{n^2 h^2}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii) Dividing equation (ii) by equation (i) {tex}r_n=\frac{\epsilon_0 h^2}{\pi m z e^2} \times \mathrm{n}^2{/tex} According to de-Brogle electron in its circular orbit must be seen as particel wave. Therfore, {tex}2 \pi r_n=\mathrm{n} \lambda{/tex} Since, de-Brogle wave length {tex}(\lambda)=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}2 \pi r_n=\frac{n h}{m v_0}{/tex} {tex}m v_0 r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} This equation is the basis of explaining the discrete orbits and every level of H-atom, theis de-Broglie hypothesis explains the stability of H-atom.
solution: {tex}\frac{m v_0^2}{r_n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{Z e^2}{r_n^2}{/tex} {tex}m v_0^2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{Z e^2}{r_n}{/tex} ...(i) according to Bohr's Postulate {tex}\mathrm{m} v_0 r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{m}^2 v_0^2 r_n^2=\frac{n^2 h^2}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii) Dividing equation (ii) by equation (i) {tex}r_n=\frac{\epsilon_0 h^2}{\pi m z e^2} \times \mathrm{n}^2{/tex} According to de-Brogle electron in its circular orbit must be seen as particel wave. Therfore, {tex}2 \pi r_n=\mathrm{n} \lambda{/tex} Since, de-Brogle wave length {tex}(\lambda)=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}2 \pi r_n=\frac{n h}{m v_0}{/tex} {tex}m v_0 r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} This equation is the basis of explaining the discrete orbits and every level of H-atom, theis de-Broglie hypothesis explains the stability of H-atom.
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{m v_0^2}{r_n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{Z e^2}{r_n^2}{/tex}
{tex}m v_0^2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{Z e^2}{r_n}{/tex} ...(i)
according to Bohr's Postulate
{tex}\mathrm{m} v_0 r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{m}^2 v_0^2 r_n^2=\frac{n^2 h^2}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii)
Dividing equation (ii) by equation (i)
{tex}r_n=\frac{\epsilon_0 h^2}{\pi m z e^2} \times \mathrm{n}^2{/tex}
According to de-Brogle electron in its circular orbit must be seen as particel wave.
Therfore, {tex}2 \pi r_n=\mathrm{n} \lambda{/tex}
Since, de-Brogle wave length {tex}(\lambda)=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}2 \pi r_n=\frac{n h}{m v_0}{/tex}
{tex}m v_0 r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
This equation is the basis of explaining the discrete orbits and every level of H-atom, theis de-Broglie hypothesis explains the stability of H-atom.

question_html:
1. How is the stability of hydrogen atom in Bohr model explained by de-Broglie’s hypothesis?
  2. A hydrogen atom initially in the ground state absorbs a photon which excites it to n = 4 level. When it gets de-excited, find the maximum number of lines which are emitted by the atom. Identify the series to which these lines belong. Which of them has the shortest wavelength?
question_text: How is the stability of hydrogen atom in Bohr model explained by de-Broglie’s hypothesis? A hydrogen atom initially in the ground state absorbs a photon which excites it to n = 4 level. When it gets de-excited, find the maximum number of lines which are emitted by the atom. Identify the series to which these lines belong. Which of them has the shortest wavelength?
explanation: The quantised electron orbits and energy state are due to wave nature of the electron and only resonant standing waves can persist. Ace. De Broglie Apotheosis. {tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex} {tex}=\frac{n h}{m v}{/tex} {tex}m v r=\left(\frac{n h}{2 \pi}\right){/tex} Lyman series transition from n = 4 to n = 1 will have shortest wavelength.
solution: The quantised electron orbits and energy state are due to wave nature of the electron and only resonant standing waves can persist. Ace. De Broglie Apotheosis. {tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex} {tex}=\frac{n h}{m v}{/tex} {tex}m v r=\left(\frac{n h}{2 \pi}\right){/tex} Lyman series transition from n = 4 to n = 1 will have shortest wavelength.
solution_html:
1. The quantised electron orbits and energy state are due to wave nature of the electron and only resonant standing waves can persist. Ace. De Broglie Apotheosis.
    {tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{n h}{m v}{/tex}
    {tex}m v r=\left(\frac{n h}{2 \pi}\right){/tex}
  2. Lyman series
    transition from n = 4 to n = 1 will have shortest wavelength.

question_text: Draw the energy level diagram for the line spectra representing Lyman series and Balmer series in the spectrum of hydrogen atom. Using the Rydberg formula for the spectrum of hydrogen atom, calculate the largest and shortest wavelengths of the emission lines of the Balmer series in the spectrum of hydrogen atom. (Use the value of Rydberg constant R = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -1 )
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {longest }}}=R\left\{\frac{1}{n_f^2}-\frac{1}{n_C^2}\right\}=R \frac{5}{36}{/tex} {tex}\lambda \max =\frac{36}{5 R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{36}{5 \times 1.1 \times 10^7} m{/tex} = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {Smallest }}}=R\left\{\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty^2}\right\}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{R}{4}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{m m}=\frac{4}{R}=\frac{4}{1.1 \times 10^7} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {longest }}}=R\left\{\frac{1}{n_f^2}-\frac{1}{n_C^2}\right\}=R \frac{5}{36}{/tex} {tex}\lambda \max =\frac{36}{5 R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{36}{5 \times 1.1 \times 10^7} m{/tex} = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {Smallest }}}=R\left\{\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty^2}\right\}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{R}{4}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{m m}=\frac{4}{R}=\frac{4}{1.1 \times 10^7} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m
solution_html:
2. {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {longest }}}=R\left\{\frac{1}{n_f^2}-\frac{1}{n_C^2}\right\}=R \frac{5}{36}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda \max =\frac{36}{5 R}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{36}{5 \times 1.1 \times 10^7} m{/tex}
    = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7m

subject: Physics
question_html: A hydrogen atom in its excited state emits radiations of wavelengths 1218 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} and 974.3 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} when it finally comes to the ground state. Identify the energy levels from where transitions occur. Given Rydberg constant R = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 m-1. Also specify the spectral series to which these lines belong.
question_text: A hydrogen atom in its excited state emits radiations of wavelengths 1218 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} and 974.3 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} when it finally comes to the ground state. Identify the energy levels from where transitions occur. Given Rydberg constant R = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -1 . Also specify the spectral series to which these lines belong.
explanation: {tex} \lambda_1=1218 \overset \circ A{/tex} {tex} \lambda_2=974.3 \overset \circ A{/tex} {tex} \text { Rydberg const, } R=1.1 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{1218 \times 10^{-10}}=1.1 \times 10^7\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right) {/tex} {tex} n^2=\frac{1}{0.2536} {/tex} {tex} n^2=3.9429 \therefore n=1.98 \cong 2 {/tex} {tex} \text { given } \lambda_2=974.3 \overset \circ A{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{974.3 \times 10^{-10}}=1.1 \times 10^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right) {/tex} {tex} n^2=\frac{1}{0.0669} {/tex} {tex} n^2=14.9411 {/tex} {tex}\therefore n=3.86 \cong 4{/tex} These lines belong to layman series.
solution: {tex} \lambda_1=1218 \overset \circ A{/tex} {tex} \lambda_2=974.3 \overset \circ A{/tex} {tex} \text { Rydberg const, } R=1.1 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{1218 \times 10^{-10}}=1.1 \times 10^7\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right) {/tex} {tex} n^2=\frac{1}{0.2536} {/tex} {tex} n^2=3.9429 \therefore n=1.98 \cong 2 {/tex} {tex} \text { given } \lambda_2=974.3 \overset \circ A{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{974.3 \times 10^{-10}}=1.1 \times 10^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right) {/tex} {tex} n^2=\frac{1}{0.0669} {/tex} {tex} n^2=14.9411 {/tex} {tex}\therefore n=3.86 \cong 4{/tex} These lines belong to layman series.
solution_html:
{tex} \lambda_1=1218 \overset \circ A{/tex}
{tex} \lambda_2=974.3 \overset \circ A{/tex}
{tex} \text { Rydberg const, } R=1.1 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m}^{-1} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{1218 \times 10^{-10}}=1.1 \times 10^7\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} n^2=\frac{1}{0.2536} {/tex}
{tex} n^2=3.9429 \therefore n=1.98 \cong 2 {/tex}
{tex} \text { given } \lambda_2=974.3 \overset \circ A{/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{974.3 \times 10^{-10}}=1.1 \times 10^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} n^2=\frac{1}{0.0669} {/tex}
{tex} n^2=14.9411 {/tex}
{tex}\therefore n=3.86 \cong 4{/tex}
These lines belong to layman series.

subject: Physics
question_html: Use Bohr’s postulates to derive the expressions for the potential and kinetic energy of the electron moving in the nth orbit of the hydrogen atom. How is the total energy of the electron expressed in terms of its kinetic and potential energies?
question_text: Use Bohr’s postulates to derive the expressions for the potential and kinetic energy of the electron moving in the n th orbit of the hydrogen atom. How is the total energy of the electron expressed in terms of its kinetic and potential energies?
explanation: If the electron moves on a circular path of the radius r with speed V as shown in the figure F e = F c {tex} \frac{m V^2}{r} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r^2} {/tex} {tex}m V^2 =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r}{/tex} ...(i) From Bohr's postulate {tex}m V r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii) From equations (i) and (ii) {tex} V=\frac{m V^2 r}{m V r}=\frac{\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} e^2}{\frac{n h}{2 \pi}}=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{2 \pi}{n h} {/tex} {tex} V=\frac{e^2}{2 n h \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Kinetic Energy {tex} K E=\frac{1}{2} m V^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times m \times \frac{e^4}{4 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2} {/tex} {tex} K E=\frac{m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2}{/tex} Potential Energy, {tex} P E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{e \times(-e)}{r} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r}{/tex} Referrring to equation (i) {tex} \frac{-1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r}=-m V^2 {/tex} {tex} P E=-m \times\left(\frac{e^2}{2 n h \varepsilon_0}\right)^2=\frac{-m e^4}{4 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2}{/tex} Total energy KE + PE {tex} =\frac{m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2}-\frac{m e^4}{4 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{m e^4-2 m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \epsilon_0}=\frac{-m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Total Energy {tex}=\frac{-m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Total energy = - (kinetic energy) Potential energy = 2(total energy)
solution: If the electron moves on a circular path of the radius r with speed V as shown in the figure F e = F c {tex} \frac{m V^2}{r} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r^2} {/tex} {tex}m V^2 =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r}{/tex} ...(i) From Bohr's postulate {tex}m V r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii) From equations (i) and (ii) {tex} V=\frac{m V^2 r}{m V r}=\frac{\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} e^2}{\frac{n h}{2 \pi}}=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{2 \pi}{n h} {/tex} {tex} V=\frac{e^2}{2 n h \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Kinetic Energy {tex} K E=\frac{1}{2} m V^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times m \times \frac{e^4}{4 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2} {/tex} {tex} K E=\frac{m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2}{/tex} Potential Energy, {tex} P E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{e \times(-e)}{r} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r}{/tex} Referrring to equation (i) {tex} \frac{-1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r}=-m V^2 {/tex} {tex} P E=-m \times\left(\frac{e^2}{2 n h \varepsilon_0}\right)^2=\frac{-m e^4}{4 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2}{/tex} Total energy KE + PE {tex} =\frac{m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2}-\frac{m e^4}{4 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{m e^4-2 m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \epsilon_0}=\frac{-m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Total Energy {tex}=\frac{-m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex} Total energy = - (kinetic energy) Potential energy = 2(total energy)
solution_html:
If the electron moves on a circular path of the radius r with speed V as shown in the figure
Fe = Fc
{tex} \frac{m V^2}{r} =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r^2} {/tex}
{tex}m V^2 =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r}{/tex} ...(i)
From Bohr's postulate
{tex}m V r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii)
From equations (i) and (ii)
{tex} V=\frac{m V^2 r}{m V r}=\frac{\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} e^2}{\frac{n h}{2 \pi}}=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{2 \pi}{n h} {/tex}
{tex} V=\frac{e^2}{2 n h \varepsilon_0}{/tex}
Kinetic Energy
{tex} K E=\frac{1}{2} m V^2 {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times m \times \frac{e^4}{4 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2} {/tex}
{tex} K E=\frac{m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2}{/tex}
Potential Energy,
{tex} P E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{e \times(-e)}{r} {/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r}{/tex}
Referrring to equation (i)
{tex} \frac{-1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r}=-m V^2 {/tex}
{tex} P E=-m \times\left(\frac{e^2}{2 n h \varepsilon_0}\right)^2=\frac{-m e^4}{4 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2}{/tex}
Total energy
KE + PE
{tex} =\frac{m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2}-\frac{m e^4}{4 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0^2} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{m e^4-2 m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \epsilon_0}=\frac{-m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex}
Total Energy
{tex}=\frac{-m e^4}{8 n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{/tex}
Total energy = - (kinetic energy)
Potential energy = 2(total energy)

subject: Physics
question_html:
Hydrogen atoms are excited with an electron beam of energy 12.5 eV. Find

  1. The highest energy level up to which the hydrogen atoms will be excited.
  2. The longest wavelengths in the (i) Lyman series, (ii) Balmer series of the spectrum of these hydrogen atoms.
question_text: Hydrogen atoms are excited with an electron beam of energy 12.5 eV. Find The highest energy level up to which the hydrogen atoms will be excited. The longest wavelengths in the (i) Lyman series, (ii) Balmer series of the spectrum of these hydrogen atoms.
explanation: Maximum Energy that the excited hydrogen atom can have is E=-13.6eV + 12.5eV=-1.1 eV Now, {tex}E_3=\frac{-13.6}{3^2} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = -1.5 eV (< (-1.1eV) {tex}E_4=\frac{-13.6}{4^2} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = -0.85 eV (> (-1.1 eV) It follows that the electron can only be excited up to the n = 3 state. Longest wavelength of Lyman series: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]=R \cdot \frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda_L=\frac{4}{3} \times \frac{1}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3 \times 1.1 \times 10^7} m \cong 1218 A^0{/tex} Longest wavelength of Balmer series: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_B}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5 R}{36}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_B=\left(\frac{36}{5 \times 1.1 \times 10^7}\right) \mathrm{m} \approx 6560 A^0{/tex}
solution: Maximum Energy that the excited hydrogen atom can have is E=-13.6eV + 12.5eV=-1.1 eV Now, {tex}E_3=\frac{-13.6}{3^2} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = -1.5 eV (< (-1.1eV) {tex}E_4=\frac{-13.6}{4^2} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = -0.85 eV (> (-1.1 eV) It follows that the electron can only be excited up to the n = 3 state. Longest wavelength of Lyman series: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]=R \cdot \frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda_L=\frac{4}{3} \times \frac{1}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3 \times 1.1 \times 10^7} m \cong 1218 A^0{/tex} Longest wavelength of Balmer series: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_B}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5 R}{36}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_B=\left(\frac{36}{5 \times 1.1 \times 10^7}\right) \mathrm{m} \approx 6560 A^0{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Maximum Energy that the excited hydrogen atom can have is
    E=-13.6eV + 12.5eV=-1.1 eV
    Now, {tex}E_3=\frac{-13.6}{3^2} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = -1.5 eV (< (-1.1eV)
    {tex}E_4=\frac{-13.6}{4^2} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = -0.85 eV (> (-1.1 eV)
    It follows that the electron can only be excited up to the n = 3 state.
  2. Longest wavelength of Lyman series:
    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]=R \cdot \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \lambda_L=\frac{4}{3} \times \frac{1}{R}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{4}{3 \times 1.1 \times 10^7} m \cong 1218 A^0{/tex}
    Longest wavelength of Balmer series:
    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_B}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{5 R}{36}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda_B=\left(\frac{36}{5 \times 1.1 \times 10^7}\right) \mathrm{m} \approx 6560 A^0{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A hydrogen atom initially in the ground level absorbs a photon, which excites it to the n = 4 level. Determine the wavelength and frequency of photon.
question_text: A hydrogen atom initially in the ground level absorbs a photon, which excites it to the n = 4 level. Determine the wavelength and frequency of photon.
solution_html:
Energy of an electron in nth orbit of H atom,
{tex}{E_n} = \frac{{ - 13.6}}{{{n^2}}}eV{/tex}
Thus, E1 = -13.6 eV
Energy in 4th (n = 4) level,
{tex}{E_4} = \frac{{ - 13.6}}{{{4^2}}} = - 0.85{/tex} eV
Now, {tex}\Delta E = {E_4} - {E_1}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta E = - 0.85 - ( - 13.6)eV{/tex}
{tex}\Delta E = 12.75eV{/tex}
Thus, h{tex}\nu{/tex} = 12.75 eV
{tex}h\nu = 12.75 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex}
{tex}\nu = \frac{{12.75 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\nu = 3.078 \times {10^{15}}Hz{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\lambda = \frac{c}{\nu } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{3.078 \times {{10}^{15}}}}{/tex}
So, {tex}\lambda = 974.4\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Calculate the ratio of the frequencies of the radiation emitted due to the transition of the electron in a hydrogen atom from its

  1. second permitted energy level to the first level and
  2. highest permitted energy level to the second permitted level.
question_text: Calculate the ratio of the frequencies of the radiation emitted due to the transition of the electron in a hydrogen atom from its second permitted energy level to the first level and highest permitted energy level to the second permitted level.
solution_html:
We have,
h{tex}\nu{/tex} = Ef - Ei
{tex}=\frac{E_{0}}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{E_{0}}{n_{i}^{2}}{/tex}

  1. h{tex}\nu_1{/tex}= {tex}E_{0}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right)=E_{0} \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
  2. h{tex}\nu_2{/tex} = {tex}E_{0}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right)=E_{0} \times \frac{1}{4}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\nu_{1}}{\nu_{2}}=3{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State Bohr's postulate to define stable orbits in hydrogen atom. How does de-Broglie's hypothesis explain stability of these orbits?
  2. A hydrogen atom initially in the ground state absorbs a photon which excites it to the n = 4 level. Estimate the frequency of the photon.
question_text: State Bohr's postulate to define stable orbits in hydrogen atom. How does de-Broglie's hypothesis explain stability of these orbits? A hydrogen atom initially in the ground state absorbs a photon which excites it to the n = 4 level. Estimate the frequency of the photon.
explanation: An atom has a number of stable orbits in which an electron can reside without the emission of radiant energy. Each orbit corresponds, to a certain energy level. Bohr’s postulate, for stable orbits, states "The electron, in an atom, revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which its angular h momentum is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} (h = Planck's constant),'' [Also accept mvr = {tex}n \cdot \frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3, .....) ........(1) As per de-Broglie’s hypothesis, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} ........(2) For a stable orbit, we must have a circumference of the orbit = {tex}n \lambda{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3, ....) {tex}\therefore \quad 2 \pi r{/tex} = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} from 1 and 2 equation we get ; L= mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} Thus de-Broglie showed that formation of stationary pattern for integral 'n' gives rise to the stability of the atom. This is nothing but the Bohr's postulate. Energy in the n = 4 level = {tex}\frac{-E_{0}}{4^{2}}=-\frac{E_{0}}{16}{/tex} Energy required to take the electron from the ground state, to the n = 4 level = {tex}\left(-\frac{E_{0}}{16}\right)-\left(-E_{0}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1+16}{16} E_{0}=\frac{15}{16} E_{0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15}{16} \times 13.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex} Let the frequency of the photon be v, we have {tex}h \nu=\frac{15}{16} \times 13.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}{/tex} {tex}\nu=\frac{15 \times 13.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{16 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}} {/tex} Hz = 3.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz (Also accept 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz)
solution: An atom has a number of stable orbits in which an electron can reside without the emission of radiant energy. Each orbit corresponds, to a certain energy level. Bohr’s postulate, for stable orbits, states "The electron, in an atom, revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which its angular h momentum is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} (h = Planck's constant),'' [Also accept mvr = {tex}n \cdot \frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3, .....) ........(1) As per de-Broglie’s hypothesis, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} ........(2) For a stable orbit, we must have a circumference of the orbit = {tex}n \lambda{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3, ....) {tex}\therefore \quad 2 \pi r{/tex} = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} from 1 and 2 equation we get ; L= mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} Thus de-Broglie showed that formation of stationary pattern for integral 'n' gives rise to the stability of the atom. This is nothing but the Bohr's postulate. Energy in the n = 4 level = {tex}\frac{-E_{0}}{4^{2}}=-\frac{E_{0}}{16}{/tex} Energy required to take the electron from the ground state, to the n = 4 level = {tex}\left(-\frac{E_{0}}{16}\right)-\left(-E_{0}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1+16}{16} E_{0}=\frac{15}{16} E_{0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15}{16} \times 13.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex} Let the frequency of the photon be v, we have {tex}h \nu=\frac{15}{16} \times 13.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}{/tex} {tex}\nu=\frac{15 \times 13.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{16 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}} {/tex} Hz = 3.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz (Also accept 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz)
solution_html:
1. An atom has a number of stable orbits in which an electron can reside without the emission of radiant energy. Each orbit corresponds, to a certain energy level. Bohr’s postulate, for stable orbits, states "The electron, in an atom, revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which its angular h momentum is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} (h = Planck's constant),''
    [Also accept mvr = {tex}n \cdot \frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}(n = 1, 2, 3, .....) ........(1)
    As per de-Broglie’s hypothesis, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} ........(2)
    For a stable orbit, we must have a circumference of the orbit = {tex}n \lambda{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3, ....)
    {tex}\therefore \quad 2 \pi r{/tex} = n{tex}\lambda{/tex}
    from 1 and 2 equation  we get ; L= mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    Thus de-Broglie showed that formation of stationary pattern for integral 'n' gives rise to the stability of the atom.
    This is nothing but the Bohr's postulate.
  2. Energy in the n = 4 level = {tex}\frac{-E_{0}}{4^{2}}=-\frac{E_{0}}{16}{/tex}
    Energy required to take the electron from the ground state, to the
    n = 4 level = {tex}\left(-\frac{E_{0}}{16}\right)-\left(-E_{0}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{-1+16}{16} E_{0}=\frac{15}{16} E_{0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15}{16} \times 13.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex}
    Let the frequency of the photon be v, we have
    {tex}h \nu=\frac{15}{16} \times 13.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}{/tex}
    {tex}\nu=\frac{15 \times 13.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{16 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}} {/tex} Hz
    = 3.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 Hz (Also accept 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 Hz)

question_html: Monochromatic radiation of wavelength 975 {tex}\overset {o}A{/tex} excites the hydrogen atom from its ground state to a higher state. How many different spectral lines are possible in the resulting spectrum? Which transition corresponds to the longest wavelength amongst them.
question_text: Monochromatic radiation of wavelength 975 {tex}\overset {o}A{/tex} excites the hydrogen atom from its ground state to a higher state. How many different spectral lines are possible in the resulting spectrum? Which transition corresponds to the longest wavelength amongst them.
explanation: Energy corresponding to the given wavelength E (in eV) = {tex}\frac{12400}{\lambda(\operatorname{in} \overset o{A})}{/tex} = 12.71 eV The excited state : E n - E 1 = 12.71 {tex}\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}}{/tex} + 13.6 = 12.71 {tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 3.9 = 4 Total no. of spectral lines possible in the resultant spectrum is given by :- {tex}\frac{n(n-1)}{2}=6{/tex} Longest wavelength will correspond to the transition from n = 4 to n = 3
solution: Energy corresponding to the given wavelength E (in eV) = {tex}\frac{12400}{\lambda(\operatorname{in} \overset o{A})}{/tex} = 12.71 eV The excited state : E n - E 1 = 12.71 {tex}\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}}{/tex} + 13.6 = 12.71 {tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 3.9 = 4 Total no. of spectral lines possible in the resultant spectrum is given by :- {tex}\frac{n(n-1)}{2}=6{/tex} Longest wavelength will correspond to the transition from n = 4 to n = 3
solution_html:
Energy corresponding to the given wavelength
E (in eV) = {tex}\frac{12400}{\lambda(\operatorname{in} \overset o{A})}{/tex}= 12.71 eV
The excited state :
En - E1 = 12.71
{tex}\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}}{/tex}+ 13.6 = 12.71
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 3.9 = 4
Total no. of spectral lines possible in the resultant spectrum is given by :- {tex}\frac{n(n-1)}{2}=6{/tex}
Longest wavelength will correspond to the transition from n = 4 to n = 3

subject: Physics
question_html: State Bohr's postulate to define stable orbits in hydrogen atom. How does de Broglie's hypothesis explain the stability of these orbits?
question_text: State Bohr's postulate to define stable orbits in hydrogen atom. How does de Broglie's hypothesis explain the stability of these orbits?
explanation: According to Bohr's postulate for stable orbits only those circular orbits can be the allowed stationary states or stable orbits of an electron in which its angular momentum is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} That is, {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}, n=1,2,3, \ldots{/tex} According to de-Broglie's hypothesis, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} A stable orbit must contain an integral number of de-Broglie wavelengths {tex}\lambda{/tex} . So {tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \pi r=n \frac{h}{m v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad L=m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} Thus the de-Broglie's hypothesis leads to Bohr's quantization condition for stationary or stable orbits.
solution: According to Bohr's postulate for stable orbits only those circular orbits can be the allowed stationary states or stable orbits of an electron in which its angular momentum is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} That is, {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}, n=1,2,3, \ldots{/tex} According to de-Broglie's hypothesis, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} A stable orbit must contain an integral number of de-Broglie wavelengths {tex}\lambda{/tex} . So {tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \pi r=n \frac{h}{m v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad L=m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} Thus the de-Broglie's hypothesis leads to Bohr's quantization condition for stationary or stable orbits.
solution_html:
According to Bohr's postulate for stable orbits only those circular orbits can be the allowed stationary states or stable orbits of an electron in which its angular momentum is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
That is, {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}, n=1,2,3, \ldots{/tex}
According to de-Broglie's hypothesis, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex}
A stable orbit must contain an integral number of de-Broglie wavelengths {tex}\lambda{/tex}. So
{tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \pi r=n \frac{h}{m v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad L=m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
Thus the de-Broglie's hypothesis leads to Bohr's quantization condition for stationary or stable orbits.

question_html: The kinetic energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle incident on the gold foil is doubled. How does the distance of the closest approach change?
question_text: The kinetic energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle incident on the gold foil is doubled. How does the distance of the closest approach change?
explanation: As the distance of the closest approach is inversely proportional to the kinetic energy of the incident {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle, so the distance of the closest approach is halved when the kinetic energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is doubled.
solution: As the distance of the closest approach is inversely proportional to the kinetic energy of the incident {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle, so the distance of the closest approach is halved when the kinetic energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is doubled.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the frequency of radiation emitted when electron in hydrogen atom undergoes transition from higher energy state (quantum number ni) to the lower state,(nf).
When electron in hydrogen atom jumps from energy state ni = 4 to nf = 3, 2, 1, identify the spectral series to which the emission lines belong.
question_text: Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the frequency of radiation emitted when electron in hydrogen atom undergoes transition from higher energy state (quantum number n i ) to the lower state,(n f ). When electron in hydrogen atom jumps from energy state n i = 4 to n f = 3, 2, 1, identify the spectral series to which the emission lines belong.
solution_html:
Suppose m be the mass of an electron and v be its speed in nth orbit of radius r. The centripetal force for revolution is produced by electrostatic attraction between electron and nucleus.
{tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(Z e)(e)}{r^{2}}{/tex} 
or, {tex}m v^{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{r}{/tex} .....(i)
So, Kinetic energy [K] = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex}
K = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{2 r}{/tex}
Potential energy = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(Z e)(-e)}{r}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{r}{/tex}
Total energy, 
E = KE + PE = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{2 r}+\left(-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{r}\right){/tex}
E = {tex}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{2 r}{/tex}
So, En = {tex}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{2 r_{n}}{/tex} ......(ii)
Again from Bohr's postulate for quantization of angular momentum,
velocity, v = {tex}\frac{nh}{2\pi mr}{/tex}
Substituting this value of v in equation (i), we get
m({tex}\frac{nh}{2\pi mr}{/tex})2 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{r}{/tex}
or, r = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{0} h^{2} n^{2}}{\pi m Z e^{2}}{/tex} or rn = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{0} h^{2} n^{2}}{\pi m Z e^{2}}{/tex} ....(iii)
Substituting this value of rn in equation (ii), we get
En = {tex}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{Z e^{2}}{2\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{0} h^{2} n^{2}}{\pi m Z e^{2}}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{m Z^{2} e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^2 h^{2} n^{2}}{/tex}
or, En = {tex}-\frac{Z^{2} R h c}{n^{2}}{/tex}, where R = {tex}\frac{m e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} c h^{3}}{/tex}
where R is called Rydberg constant.
For hydrogen atom, Z = 1, so
En = {tex}\frac{-R c h}{n^{2}}{/tex}
If ni and nf are the quantum numbers of initial and final states and Ei & Ef are energies of electron in H-atom in an initial and final state, we have
Ei = {tex}\frac{-R h c}{n_{i}^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}E_{f}=\frac{-R h c}{n_{f}^{2}}{/tex}
If {tex}\nu{/tex} is the frequency of emitted radiation, we get
{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{E_{i}-E_{f}}{h}{/tex}
{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-R ch}{n_{i}^{2}}{/tex} - {tex}\left(\frac{-R ch}{n_{f}^{2}}\right){/tex} = Rch{tex}\left[\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{m e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}{/tex}{tex}\left[\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
This is the required expression.
If electron jumps from ni = 4 to nf = 3, 2, 1, radiation belongs to Paschen, Balmer and Lyman series.

subject: Physics
question_html: Using Rydberg formula, calculate the longest wavelengths belonging to Lyman and Balmer series. In which region of hydrogen spectrum do these transitions lie? [Given R = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 107m-1J
question_text: Using Rydberg formula, calculate the longest wavelengths belonging to Lyman and Balmer series. In which region of hydrogen spectrum do these transitions lie? [Given R = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -1 J
explanation: Rydberg's formula is {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {if }}}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}\right]{/tex} For longest wavelength o f Lyman series: n i =2, n f = 1 {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{21}}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]=\frac{3}{4} R{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \lambda_{21}=\frac{4}{3 R}=\frac{4}{3 \times 1.1 \times 10^{7}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 1.21 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m = 121 nm This transition lies in the ultraviolet region. For longest wavelength o f Balmer series: n i = 3, n f = 2 {tex}\therefore \quad \lambda_{32}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right]=R\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right]=\frac{5}{36} R{/tex} or {tex}\lambda_{32}=\frac{36}{5 R}=\frac{36}{5 \times 1.1 \times 10^{7}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 6.545 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m = 655 nm This transition lies in the visible region.
solution: Rydberg's formula is {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {if }}}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}\right]{/tex} For longest wavelength o f Lyman series: n i =2, n f = 1 {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{21}}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]=\frac{3}{4} R{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \lambda_{21}=\frac{4}{3 R}=\frac{4}{3 \times 1.1 \times 10^{7}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 1.21 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m = 121 nm This transition lies in the ultraviolet region. For longest wavelength o f Balmer series: n i = 3, n f = 2 {tex}\therefore \quad \lambda_{32}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right]=R\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right]=\frac{5}{36} R{/tex} or {tex}\lambda_{32}=\frac{36}{5 R}=\frac{36}{5 \times 1.1 \times 10^{7}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 6.545 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m = 655 nm This transition lies in the visible region.
solution_html:
Rydberg's formula is {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {if }}}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
For longest wavelength o f Lyman series: ni =2, nf = 1
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{21}}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]=\frac{3}{4} R{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \lambda_{21}=\frac{4}{3 R}=\frac{4}{3 \times 1.1 \times 10^{7}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
= 1.21 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7m = 121 nm
This transition lies in the ultraviolet region.
For longest wavelength o f Balmer series: ni = 3, nf = 2
{tex}\therefore \quad \lambda_{32}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right]=R\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right]=\frac{5}{36} R{/tex}
or {tex}\lambda_{32}=\frac{36}{5 R}=\frac{36}{5 \times 1.1 \times 10^{7}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
= 6.545 {tex}\times{/tex} 107m
= 655 nm
This transition lies in the visible region.

question_html: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. If an electron makes a transition from an energy level -1.51 eV to - 3.4 eV, then calculate the wavelength of the spectral line emitted and name the series of hydrogen spectrum to which it belongs.
question_text: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. If an electron makes a transition from an energy level -1.51 eV to - 3.4 eV, then calculate the wavelength of the spectral line emitted and name the series of hydrogen spectrum to which it belongs.
explanation: Energy difference = Energy of emitted photon = E 2 - E 1 = -1.51 - (-3.4) = 1.89 eV = 1.89 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h c}{E_2-E_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.89 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{19.8}{3.024} \times 10^{-7}{/tex} = 6.548 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m = 6548 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} This wavelength belongs to Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum.
solution: Energy difference = Energy of emitted photon = E 2 - E 1 = -1.51 - (-3.4) = 1.89 eV = 1.89 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h c}{E_2-E_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.89 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{19.8}{3.024} \times 10^{-7}{/tex} = 6.548 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m = 6548 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} This wavelength belongs to Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum.
solution_html:
Energy difference = Energy of emitted photon
 = E2 - E1
= -1.51 - (-3.4) = 1.89 eV = 1.89 {tex}\times{/tex}1.6 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 J
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h c}{E_2-E_1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.89 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{19.8}{3.024} \times 10^{-7}{/tex}
= 6.548 {tex}\times{/tex}10-7 m = 6548 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}
This wavelength belongs to Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum.

question_html:
Define the distance of closest approach.
An {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle of kinetic energy K is bombarded on a thin gold foil. The distance of the closest approach is r. What will be the distance of closest approach for an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle of double the kinetic energy?
Write two important limitations of Rutherford nuclear model of the atom.
question_text: Define the distance of closest approach. An {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle of kinetic energy K is bombarded on a thin gold foil. The distance of the closest approach is r. What will be the distance of closest approach for an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle of double the kinetic energy? Write two important limitations of Rutherford nuclear model of the atom.
explanation: When an {tex}\alpha{/tex} - particle is bombarded over a gold nucleus, it is repelled by electrostatic repulsion. As a result KE of α -particle is converted into electrostatic PE. At a certain distance between the a-particle and nucleus at which the moving charge loses all its kinetic energy and becomes stationary momentarily. This distance (r) is known as distance of the closest approach. In this process, all the kinetic energy (K) of moving particle is converted into potential energy. According to the question- {tex}\frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { 2 e \times Z e } { r } = K{/tex} ................(i) Let r 0 be the new distance of the closest approach for a twice energetic {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. Then, we have, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \times \frac { 2 e \times Z e } { r _ { 0 } } = 2 K{/tex} ..........(ii) On dividing Eq (i) by Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac { r _ { 0 } } { r } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow r _ { 0 } = \frac { r } { 2 }{/tex} Limitations of Rutherford nuclear model are as follows : A moving charge in a circular path must radiate energy because it is continuously subjected to a force. Due to this, continuous loss of energy of electrons, the radii of the orbits should be decreasing continuously. So, the concept of fixed orbit is erroneous. Due to this continuous decrease in radii of orbit, a charge moves over a spiral path must radiates a continuous spectrum. Hence, line emission spectrum of H-atom is not explained by Rutherford's model.
solution: When an {tex}\alpha{/tex} - particle is bombarded over a gold nucleus, it is repelled by electrostatic repulsion. As a result KE of α -particle is converted into electrostatic PE. At a certain distance between the a-particle and nucleus at which the moving charge loses all its kinetic energy and becomes stationary momentarily. This distance (r) is known as distance of the closest approach. In this process, all the kinetic energy (K) of moving particle is converted into potential energy. According to the question- {tex}\frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { 2 e \times Z e } { r } = K{/tex} ................(i) Let r 0 be the new distance of the closest approach for a twice energetic {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. Then, we have, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \times \frac { 2 e \times Z e } { r _ { 0 } } = 2 K{/tex} ..........(ii) On dividing Eq (i) by Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac { r _ { 0 } } { r } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow r _ { 0 } = \frac { r } { 2 }{/tex} Limitations of Rutherford nuclear model are as follows : A moving charge in a circular path must radiate energy because it is continuously subjected to a force. Due to this, continuous loss of energy of electrons, the radii of the orbits should be decreasing continuously. So, the concept of fixed orbit is erroneous. Due to this continuous decrease in radii of orbit, a charge moves over a spiral path must radiates a continuous spectrum. Hence, line emission spectrum of H-atom is not explained by Rutherford's model.
solution_html:
When an {tex}\alpha{/tex}- particle is bombarded over a gold nucleus, it is repelled by electrostatic repulsion. As a result KE of α-particle is converted into electrostatic PE. At a certain distance between the a-particle and nucleus at which the moving charge loses all its kinetic energy and becomes stationary momentarily. This distance (r) is known as distance of the closest approach. In this process, all the kinetic energy (K) of moving particle is converted into potential energy.
According to the question-
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { 2 e \times Z e } { r } = K{/tex}................(i)
Let r0 be the new distance of the closest approach for a twice energetic {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle. Then, we have,
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \times \frac { 2 e \times Z e } { r _ { 0 } } = 2 K{/tex}..........(ii)
On dividing Eq (i) by Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}\frac { r _ { 0 } } { r } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow r _ { 0 } = \frac { r } { 2 }{/tex}

Limitations of Rutherford nuclear model are as follows:

  1. A moving charge in a circular path must radiate energy because it is continuously subjected to a force. Due to this, continuous loss of energy of electrons, the radii of the orbits should be decreasing continuously. So, the concept of fixed orbit is erroneous.
  2. Due to this continuous decrease in radii of orbit, a charge moves over a spiral path must radiates a continuous spectrum. Hence, line emission spectrum of H-atom is not explained by Rutherford's model.

question_html: Define the distance of closest approach. An {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle of kinetic energy K is bombarded on a thin gold foil. The distance of the closest approach is r. What will be the distance of the closest approach for an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle of double the kinetic energy?
question_text: Define the distance of closest approach. An {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle of kinetic energy K is bombarded on a thin gold foil. The distance of the closest approach is r. What will be the distance of the closest approach for an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle of double the kinetic energy?
explanation: It is the distance of the charged particle from the centre of the nucleus, at which the whole of the initial kinetic energy of the (far off) charged particle gets converted into the electric potential energy of the system. The distance of closest approach (r c ) is given by {tex}r_{c}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{ Z e^{2}}{K}{/tex} here K is kinetic energy. 'K' is doubled, r c becomes {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex}
solution: It is the distance of the charged particle from the centre of the nucleus, at which the whole of the initial kinetic energy of the (far off) charged particle gets converted into the electric potential energy of the system. The distance of closest approach (r c ) is given by {tex}r_{c}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{ Z e^{2}}{K}{/tex} here K is kinetic energy. 'K' is doubled, r c becomes {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is the distance of the charged particle from the centre of the nucleus, at which the whole of the initial kinetic energy of the (far off) charged particle gets converted into the electric potential energy of the system.
The distance of closest approach (rc) is given by
{tex}r_{c}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{ Z e^{2}}{K}{/tex}  here K is kinetic energy.
'K' is doubled, rc becomes {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Write two important limitations of Rutherford nuclear model of the atom.
question_text: Write two important limitations of Rutherford nuclear model of the atom.
explanation: According to the Rutherford model, an electron orbiting around the nucleus continuously radiates energy due to the acceleration; hence the atom will not remain stable. This model could not explain the origin of the line spectra of atoms. Or As electron spirals inwards; its angular velocity and frequency change continuously; therefore it will emit a continuous spectrum.
solution: According to the Rutherford model, an electron orbiting around the nucleus continuously radiates energy due to the acceleration; hence the atom will not remain stable. This model could not explain the origin of the line spectra of atoms. Or As electron spirals inwards; its angular velocity and frequency change continuously; therefore it will emit a continuous spectrum.
solution_html:
1.  According to the Rutherford model, an electron orbiting around the nucleus continuously radiates energy due to the acceleration; hence the atom will not remain stable.
  2. This model could not explain the origin of the line spectra of atoms. Or As electron spirals inwards; its angular velocity and frequency change continuously; therefore it will emit a continuous spectrum.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define distance of closest approach.
question_text: Define distance of closest approach.
explanation: The distance of the closest approach is the distance between the centre of the nucleus and the point from which a charged particle approaching directly towards the nucleus, returns and at which the entire kinetic energy of the particle will be completely converted into electrostatic potential energy.
solution: The distance of the closest approach is the distance between the centre of the nucleus and the point from which a charged particle approaching directly towards the nucleus, returns and at which the entire kinetic energy of the particle will be completely converted into electrostatic potential energy.
solution_html: The distance of the closest approach is the distance between the centre of the nucleus and the point from which a charged particle approaching directly towards the nucleus, returns and at which the entire kinetic energy of the particle will be completely converted into electrostatic potential energy.

subject: Physics
question_html: A 12.5 eV electron beam is used to excite a hydrogen atom at room temperature. Determine the wavelengths and the corresponding series of the lines emitted.
question_text: A 12.5 eV electron beam is used to excite a hydrogen atom at room temperature. Determine the wavelengths and the corresponding series of the lines emitted.
explanation: {tex}E_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E 1 = -13.6 eV, E 2 = -3.4 eV, E 3 = -1.5 eV {tex}\Delta E{/tex} = E 3 - E 1 = -1.5 - (13.6) = 12.1 eV Clearly, the 12.5 eV electron beam can Excite the electrons upto n = 3 level only. Energy values of the emitted corresponding to the three possible emitted photons lines are: {tex}\Delta E_{3 \rightarrow 1}{/tex} = E 3 - E 1 = -1.5 + 13.6 = 12.1 eV {tex}\Delta E_{2 \rightarrow 1}{/tex} = E 2 - E 1 = -3.4 + 13.6 = 10.2 eV {tex}\Delta E_{3 \rightarrow 2}{/tex} = E 3 - E 2 = - 1.5 + 3.4 = 1.9 eV The corresponding wavelengths of the emitted lines will be {tex}\lambda_{31}=\frac{h c}{\Delta E_{3} \rightarrow 1}=\frac{1237.5 \mathrm{eVnm}}{12.1 \mathrm{eV}} \simeq{/tex} 102.3 nm [Lyman series] {tex}\lambda_{21}=\frac{h c}{\Delta E_{2 \rightarrow 1}}=\frac{1237.5 \mathrm{eVnm}}{10.2 \mathrm{eV}}={/tex} 121.3 nm. [Lymlan series] {tex}\lambda_{32}=\frac{h c}{\Delta E_{3 \rightarrow 2}}=\frac{1237.5 \mathrm{eVnm}}{1.9 \mathrm{eV}}={/tex} 651.3 nm. [Balmer series]
solution: {tex}E_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E 1 = -13.6 eV, E 2 = -3.4 eV, E 3 = -1.5 eV {tex}\Delta E{/tex} = E 3 - E 1 = -1.5 - (13.6) = 12.1 eV Clearly, the 12.5 eV electron beam can Excite the electrons upto n = 3 level only. Energy values of the emitted corresponding to the three possible emitted photons lines are: {tex}\Delta E_{3 \rightarrow 1}{/tex} = E 3 - E 1 = -1.5 + 13.6 = 12.1 eV {tex}\Delta E_{2 \rightarrow 1}{/tex} = E 2 - E 1 = -3.4 + 13.6 = 10.2 eV {tex}\Delta E_{3 \rightarrow 2}{/tex} = E 3 - E 2 = - 1.5 + 3.4 = 1.9 eV The corresponding wavelengths of the emitted lines will be {tex}\lambda_{31}=\frac{h c}{\Delta E_{3} \rightarrow 1}=\frac{1237.5 \mathrm{eVnm}}{12.1 \mathrm{eV}} \simeq{/tex} 102.3 nm [Lyman series] {tex}\lambda_{21}=\frac{h c}{\Delta E_{2 \rightarrow 1}}=\frac{1237.5 \mathrm{eVnm}}{10.2 \mathrm{eV}}={/tex} 121.3 nm. [Lymlan series] {tex}\lambda_{32}=\frac{h c}{\Delta E_{3 \rightarrow 2}}=\frac{1237.5 \mathrm{eVnm}}{1.9 \mathrm{eV}}={/tex} 651.3 nm. [Balmer series]
solution_html:
{tex}E_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} E1 = -13.6 eV, E2 = -3.4 eV, E3 = -1.5 eV
{tex}\Delta E{/tex} = E3 - E1 = -1.5 - (13.6) = 12.1 eV
Clearly, the 12.5 eV electron beam can Excite the electrons upto n = 3 level only.
Energy values of the emitted corresponding to the three possible emitted photons lines are:
{tex}\Delta E_{3 \rightarrow 1}{/tex} = E3 - E1 = -1.5 + 13.6 = 12.1 eV
{tex}\Delta E_{2 \rightarrow 1}{/tex} = E2 - E1 = -3.4 + 13.6 = 10.2 eV
{tex}\Delta E_{3 \rightarrow 2}{/tex} = E3 - E2 = - 1.5 + 3.4 = 1.9 eV
The corresponding wavelengths of the emitted lines will be
{tex}\lambda_{31}=\frac{h c}{\Delta E_{3} \rightarrow 1}=\frac{1237.5 \mathrm{eVnm}}{12.1 \mathrm{eV}} \simeq{/tex} 102.3 nm [Lyman series]
{tex}\lambda_{21}=\frac{h c}{\Delta E_{2 \rightarrow 1}}=\frac{1237.5 \mathrm{eVnm}}{10.2 \mathrm{eV}}={/tex} 121.3 nm. [Lymlan series]
{tex}\lambda_{32}=\frac{h c}{\Delta E_{3 \rightarrow 2}}=\frac{1237.5 \mathrm{eVnm}}{1.9 \mathrm{eV}}={/tex} 651.3 nm. [Balmer series]

subject: Physics
question_html: Derive the Bohr's quantisation condition for angular momentum of the orbiting electron in hydrogen atom, using de Broglie's hypothesis.
question_text: Derive the Bohr's quantisation condition for angular momentum of the orbiting electron in hydrogen atom, using de Broglie's hypothesis.
explanation: By de Broglie's hypothesis, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} Using de Broglie's condition of stationary orbits, {tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex} or {tex}2 \pi r=n \times \frac{h}{m v}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad L=m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} This is Bohr's quantisation condition for the angular momentum of orbiting electrons in hydrogen atom.
solution: By de Broglie's hypothesis, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} Using de Broglie's condition of stationary orbits, {tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex} or {tex}2 \pi r=n \times \frac{h}{m v}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad L=m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} This is Bohr's quantisation condition for the angular momentum of orbiting electrons in hydrogen atom.
solution_html:
By de Broglie's hypothesis, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex}
Using de Broglie's condition of stationary orbits,
{tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex} or {tex}2 \pi r=n \times \frac{h}{m v}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad L=m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
This is Bohr's quantisation condition for the angular momentum of orbiting electrons in hydrogen atom.

subject: Physics
question_html: The short wavelength limit for the Lyman series of the hydrogen spectrum is 913.4 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^ \circ {/tex}. Calculate the short wavelength limit for Balmer series of the hydrogen spectrum.
question_text: The short wavelength limit for the Lyman series of the hydrogen spectrum is 913.4 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^ \circ {/tex} . Calculate the short wavelength limit for Balmer series of the hydrogen spectrum.
explanation: For shortest wavelength of Lyman series, {tex}n_{1}=1, n_{2}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{L S}}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=R{/tex} or {tex}\lambda_{L S}=\frac{1}{R}{/tex} For shortest wavelength of Balmer series, n 1 = 2 {tex}n_{2}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{B S}}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{R}{4}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{B S}=\frac{4}{R}=4 \lambda_{L S}=4 \times 913.4 \mathrm{~A}=3653.6 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
solution: For shortest wavelength of Lyman series, {tex}n_{1}=1, n_{2}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{L S}}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=R{/tex} or {tex}\lambda_{L S}=\frac{1}{R}{/tex} For shortest wavelength of Balmer series, n 1 = 2 {tex}n_{2}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{B S}}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{R}{4}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{B S}=\frac{4}{R}=4 \lambda_{L S}=4 \times 913.4 \mathrm{~A}=3653.6 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
solution_html:
For shortest wavelength of Lyman series, {tex}n_{1}=1, n_{2}=\infty{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{L S}}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=R{/tex}  or {tex}\lambda_{L S}=\frac{1}{R}{/tex}
For shortest wavelength of Balmer series, n1 = 2 {tex}n_{2}=\infty{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{B S}}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{R}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{B S}=\frac{4}{R}=4 \lambda_{L S}=4 \times 913.4 \mathrm{~A}=3653.6 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}

question_text: Calculate the shortest wavelength of the photons emitted in the Bracket series of hydrogen spectrum. Which part of the e.m. spectrum, does it belong to? R = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -1
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{s}}=\frac{1}{R}\left[\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{R}{16}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{s}=\frac{16}{R}=\frac{16}{1.1 \times 10^{7}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 14.54 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m = 1454 mn This wavelength belongs to the infrared region of the e.m. spectrum,

solution_html:

{tex}\lambda_{s}=\frac{16}{R}=\frac{16}{1.1 \times 10^{7}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 14.54 {tex}\times{/tex} 107m = 1454 mn

question_html: The photon emitted during the de-excitation from the first excited level to the ground state of hydrogen atom is used to irradiate a photocathode of a photocell, in which stopping potential of 5 V is used. Calculate the work function of the cathode used.
question_text: The photon emitted during the de-excitation from the first excited level to the ground state of hydrogen atom is used to irradiate a photocathode of a photocell, in which stopping potential of 5 V is used. Calculate the work function of the cathode used.
explanation: Energy of incident photon = E 2 - E 1 = -3.4 - (13.6) = 10.2 eV K.E. of photo electron = eV 0 =5 eV By conservation of energy, Energy of incident photon = K.E. of photo electron + Work function 10.2 eV = 5 eV + W 0 {tex}​​​​​​​\therefore{/tex} W 0 = 5.2 eV
solution: Energy of incident photon = E 2 - E 1 = -3.4 - (13.6) = 10.2 eV K.E. of photo electron = eV 0 =5 eV By conservation of energy, Energy of incident photon = K.E. of photo electron + Work function 10.2 eV = 5 eV + W 0 {tex}​​​​​​​\therefore{/tex} W 0 = 5.2 eV
solution_html:
Energy of incident photon = E2 - E1 = -3.4 - (13.6) = 10.2 eV
K.E. of photo electron = eV0=5 eV
By conservation of energy,
Energy of incident photon = K.E. of photo electron + Work function
10.2 eV = 5 eV + W0
{tex}​​​​​​​\therefore{/tex} W0 = 5.2 eV

subject: Physics
question_html: Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the orbital period of the electron moving in the nth orbit of the hydrogen atom.
question_text: Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the orbital period of the electron moving in the nth orbit of the hydrogen atom.
explanation: Centripetal force = Electrostatic attraction {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{k e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} m v 2 r = k e 2 ...(i) Bohr's quantization condition for angular momentum is {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii) Dividing (i) by (ii), we get {tex}v=\frac{2 \pi k e^{2}}{n h}{/tex} {tex}T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}=\frac{2 \pi m v r}{m v^{2}}=\frac{2 \pi\left(\frac{n h}{2 \pi}\right)}{m\left(\frac{2 \pi k e^{2}}{n h}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{n^{3} h^{3}}{4 \pi^{2} m k^{2} e^{4}} \quad\left[k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\right]{/tex}
solution: Centripetal force = Electrostatic attraction {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{k e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} m v 2 r = k e 2 ...(i) Bohr's quantization condition for angular momentum is {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii) Dividing (i) by (ii), we get {tex}v=\frac{2 \pi k e^{2}}{n h}{/tex} {tex}T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}=\frac{2 \pi m v r}{m v^{2}}=\frac{2 \pi\left(\frac{n h}{2 \pi}\right)}{m\left(\frac{2 \pi k e^{2}}{n h}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{n^{3} h^{3}}{4 \pi^{2} m k^{2} e^{4}} \quad\left[k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Centripetal force = Electrostatic attraction {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{k e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
m v2 r = k e2 ...(i)

Bohr's quantization condition for angular momentum is
{tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii)
Dividing (i) by (ii), we get
{tex}v=\frac{2 \pi k e^{2}}{n h}{/tex}
{tex}T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}=\frac{2 \pi m v r}{m v^{2}}=\frac{2 \pi\left(\frac{n h}{2 \pi}\right)}{m\left(\frac{2 \pi k e^{2}}{n h}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{n^{3} h^{3}}{4 \pi^{2} m k^{2} e^{4}} \quad\left[k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\right]{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: State Bohr's postulates. Using these postulates derive an expression for the total energy of an electron in the nth orbit of an atom. What does negative value of this energy signify ? What is Bohr's radius?
question_text: State Bohr's postulates. Using these postulates derive an expression for the total energy of an electron in the n th orbit of an atom. What does negative value of this energy signify ? What is Bohr's radius?
explanation: The postulates of Bohr's theory are as follows: The electrons in an atom revolve around the nucleus only in certain selected circular orbits. The energy of the electron cannot change continuously. The angular momentum of an electron around the nucleus is quantised. Energy of an electron in the nth orbit: Let z be the atomic number of a hydrogen atom. Since the electron moves in a circular orbit, the electrostatic force of attraction between the nucleus and the electron provides the centripetal force. That is {tex}\frac{kze^2}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2 }{r}{/tex} K = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 Nm 2 /C 2 ...(i) Total energy of the electron, E n is the sum of the kinetic and the potential energies E n = {tex}\frac12{/tex} mv 2 - {tex}\frac{kze^2}{r}{/tex} ...(ii) from Bohr's second postulate v = {tex}\frac{2nh}{2\pi mr}{/tex} Substituting this value of v in eqn (i) we get r = {tex}\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m k z e^2}{/tex} ...(iii) From eqn. (i) mv 2 = {tex}\frac{k z e^2}{r}{/tex} ...(iv) Substituting in eqn. (ii) E n = {tex}-\frac{k z e^2}{2 r}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{k z e^2}{2} \cdot \frac{4 \pi^2 m k z e^2}{n^2 h^2}{/tex} or, E n = {tex}\frac{-2 \pi^2 m k^2 z^2 e^4}{n^2 h^2}{/tex} Significance of negative sign: Negative sign indicates that the electron is bound to the nucleus because of the force of attraction and energy must be supplied to remove it away from the nucleus. Bohr's radius is equal to the most probable distance between the nucleus and the electron in a hydrogen atom in its ground state.
solution: The postulates of Bohr's theory are as follows: The electrons in an atom revolve around the nucleus only in certain selected circular orbits. The energy of the electron cannot change continuously. The angular momentum of an electron around the nucleus is quantised. Energy of an electron in the nth orbit: Let z be the atomic number of a hydrogen atom. Since the electron moves in a circular orbit, the electrostatic force of attraction between the nucleus and the electron provides the centripetal force. That is {tex}\frac{kze^2}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2 }{r}{/tex} K = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 Nm 2 /C 2 ...(i) Total energy of the electron, E n is the sum of the kinetic and the potential energies E n = {tex}\frac12{/tex} mv 2 - {tex}\frac{kze^2}{r}{/tex} ...(ii) from Bohr's second postulate v = {tex}\frac{2nh}{2\pi mr}{/tex} Substituting this value of v in eqn (i) we get r = {tex}\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m k z e^2}{/tex} ...(iii) From eqn. (i) mv 2 = {tex}\frac{k z e^2}{r}{/tex} ...(iv) Substituting in eqn. (ii) E n = {tex}-\frac{k z e^2}{2 r}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{k z e^2}{2} \cdot \frac{4 \pi^2 m k z e^2}{n^2 h^2}{/tex} or, E n = {tex}\frac{-2 \pi^2 m k^2 z^2 e^4}{n^2 h^2}{/tex} Significance of negative sign: Negative sign indicates that the electron is bound to the nucleus because of the force of attraction and energy must be supplied to remove it away from the nucleus. Bohr's radius is equal to the most probable distance between the nucleus and the electron in a hydrogen atom in its ground state.
solution_html:
The postulates of Bohr's theory are as follows:

  1. The electrons in an atom revolve around the nucleus only in certain selected circular orbits.
  2. The energy of the electron cannot change continuously.
  3. The angular momentum of an electron around the nucleus is quantised.

Energy of an electron in the nth orbit: Let z be the atomic number of a hydrogen atom. Since the electron moves in a circular orbit, the electrostatic force of attraction between the nucleus and the electron provides the centripetal force. That is
{tex}\frac{kze^2}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2 }{r}{/tex}K
= 9 {tex}\times{/tex}109 Nm2/C2 ...(i)
Total energy of the electron, En is the sum of the kinetic and the potential energies
En = {tex}\frac12{/tex}mv2 - {tex}\frac{kze^2}{r}{/tex} ...(ii)
from Bohr's second postulate
v = {tex}\frac{2nh}{2\pi mr}{/tex}
Substituting this value of v in eqn (i) we get
r = {tex}\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m k z e^2}{/tex} ...(iii)
From eqn. (i)
mv2 = {tex}\frac{k z e^2}{r}{/tex} ...(iv)
Substituting in eqn. (ii)
En = {tex}-\frac{k z e^2}{2 r}{/tex}
= {tex}-\frac{k z e^2}{2} \cdot \frac{4 \pi^2 m k z e^2}{n^2 h^2}{/tex}
or,
En = {tex}\frac{-2 \pi^2 m k^2 z^2 e^4}{n^2 h^2}{/tex}
Significance of negative sign: Negative sign indicates that the electron is bound to the nucleus because of the force of attraction and energy must be supplied to remove it away from the nucleus. Bohr's radius is equal to the most probable distance between the nucleus and the electron in a hydrogen atom in its ground state.

Obtain the expression for the distance of closest approach d in terms of the kinetic energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle K.

Obtain the expression for the distance of closest approach d in terms of the kinetic energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle K.

Kinetic energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is given as, {tex}K = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { 2 e . Z e } { d { } }{/tex} where d is the distance of closest approach. Or {tex}d ^ { } = \frac { 2 Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } K } {/tex} This is the required expression for the distance of closest approach d in terms of kinetic energy K.

Or {tex}d ^ { } = \frac { 2 Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } K } {/tex} This is the required expression for the distance of closest approach d in terms of kinetic energy K.

When alpha particle approaches Nucleus,Kinetic energy of alpha particle will be converted into potential energy of the system.

Kinetic energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle is given as,
{tex}K = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { 2 e . Z e } { d { } }{/tex}
 where d is the distance of closest approach.
Or {tex}d ^ { } = \frac { 2 Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } K } {/tex}
This is the required expression for the distance of closest approach d in terms of kinetic energy K.

question_html: Find out the wavelength of the electron orbiting in the ground state of hydrogen atom.
question_text: Find out the wavelength of the electron orbiting in the ground state of hydrogen atom.
explanation: Radius of ground state of hydrogen atom {tex}=0.53A^\circ = 0.53 \times 10^{-10}m{/tex} . According to de Broglie relation 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = nλ For ground state n=1 {tex}2 \times 3.14 \times 0.53 \times 10^{-10}= 1 \times \lambda{/tex} therefore, {tex}λ = 3.32 \times 10^{-10}m{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = 3.32\mathop A^\circ{/tex}
solution: Radius of ground state of hydrogen atom {tex}=0.53A^\circ = 0.53 \times 10^{-10}m{/tex} . According to de Broglie relation 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r = nλ
For ground state n=1
 {tex}2 \times 3.14 \times 0.53 \times 10^{-10}= 1 \times \lambda{/tex}
therefore,  {tex}λ = 3.32 \times 10^{-10}m{/tex}
