question_html: Find the wavelength of the electron orbiting in the first excited state in hydrogen atom.
question_text: Find the wavelength of the electron orbiting in the first excited state in hydrogen atom.
explanation: For electron in first excited state i.e. n = 2. So, if λ be its wavelength (De-Broglie), then we have n λ = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r n ...(i) where,r n is the radius of second orbit. {tex}r _ { n } \approx 0.5 \times n ^ { 2 } ( \text { in }\mathop A\limits^o ){/tex} {tex}= 0.5 \times 4 = 2 \mathop A\limits^o{/tex} Substitute r n in Eq(i) , we get {tex}\therefore \quad 2 \times \lambda = 2 \times \pi \times 2 { \mathop A\limits^o }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 2 \pi \mathop A\limits^o = 6.28\mathop A\limits^o{/tex}
solution: For electron in first excited state i.e. n = 2. So, if λ be its wavelength (De-Broglie), then we have n λ = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r n ...(i) where,r n is the radius of second orbit. {tex}r _ { n } \approx 0.5 \times n ^ { 2 } ( \text { in }\mathop A\limits^o ){/tex} {tex}= 0.5 \times 4 = 2 \mathop A\limits^o{/tex} Substitute r n in Eq(i) , we get {tex}\therefore \quad 2 \times \lambda = 2 \times \pi \times 2 { \mathop A\limits^o }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 2 \pi \mathop A\limits^o = 6.28\mathop A\limits^o{/tex}
solution_html:
For electron in first excited state i.e. n = 2.

So, if λ be its wavelength (De-Broglie), then we have

n λ = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rn  ...(i)

where,rn is the radius of second orbit.
{tex}r _ { n } \approx 0.5 \times n ^ { 2 } ( \text { in }\mathop A\limits^o ){/tex}{tex}= 0.5 \times 4 = 2 \mathop A\limits^o{/tex}

Substitute rn in Eq(i) , we get 
{tex}\therefore \quad 2 \times \lambda = 2 \times \pi \times 2 { \mathop A\limits^o }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 2 \pi \mathop A\limits^o = 6.28\mathop A\limits^o{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: For a given impact parameter b, does the angle of deflection increase or decrease with increase in energy?
question_text: For a given impact parameter b, does the angle of deflection increase or decrease with increase in energy?
explanation: Impact factor, b = {tex}\frac{1}{4\pi\in_0}\frac{Ze^2 \ cot \theta/2}{E}{/tex} For given value of b, when E is increased, {tex}\cot\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} increases ; {tex}\tan\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} decreases ; {tex}\theta{/tex} decreases. Thus, the angle of deflection decreases.
solution: Impact factor, b = {tex}\frac{1}{4\pi\in_0}\frac{Ze^2 \ cot \theta/2}{E}{/tex} For given value of b, when E is increased, {tex}\cot\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} increases ; {tex}\tan\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} decreases ; {tex}\theta{/tex} decreases. Thus, the angle of deflection decreases.
solution_html:
Impact factor, b = {tex}\frac{1}{4\pi\in_0}\frac{Ze^2 \ cot \theta/2}{E}{/tex}
For given value of b, when E is increased, {tex}\cot\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} increases ; {tex}\tan\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} decreases ; {tex}\theta{/tex} decreases. Thus, the angle of deflection decreases.

subject: Physics
question_html: The short wavelength limit for the Lyman series of the hydrogen spectrum is 913.4 A. Calculate the short wavelength limit for Balmer series of the hydrogen spectrum.
question_text: The short wavelength limit for the Lyman series of the hydrogen spectrum is 913.4 A. Calculate the short wavelength limit for Balmer series of the hydrogen spectrum.
explanation: Rydberg formula for wavelength for the hydrogen spectrum is given by {tex}\frac1\lambda=R_H\left(\frac1{n^2f}-\frac1{n_i^2}\right){/tex} For short wavelength of Lyman series, {tex}\frac1{\lambda_L}=R_H\left(\frac1{\infty^2}-1\right)\Rightarrow R_H=\frac1{913.4}A^{-1}{/tex} For short limit of wavelength for Balmer series, {tex}\frac1{\lambda_B}=R_H\left(\frac1{\infty^2}-\frac1{2^2}\right)=\frac{R_H}4{/tex} So, {tex}\lambda_B=\frac4{R_H}=4\times913.4=365.36\mathrm{nm}{/tex}
solution: Rydberg formula for wavelength for the hydrogen spectrum is given by {tex}\frac1\lambda=R_H\left(\frac1{n^2f}-\frac1{n_i^2}\right){/tex} For short wavelength of Lyman series, {tex}\frac1{\lambda_L}=R_H\left(\frac1{\infty^2}-1\right)\Rightarrow R_H=\frac1{913.4}A^{-1}{/tex} For short limit of wavelength for Balmer series, {tex}\frac1{\lambda_B}=R_H\left(\frac1{\infty^2}-\frac1{2^2}\right)=\frac{R_H}4{/tex} So, {tex}\lambda_B=\frac4{R_H}=4\times913.4=365.36\mathrm{nm}{/tex}
solution_html:
Rydberg formula for wavelength for the hydrogen spectrum is given by
{tex}\frac1\lambda=R_H\left(\frac1{n^2f}-\frac1{n_i^2}\right){/tex}
For short wavelength of Lyman series,
{tex}\frac1{\lambda_L}=R_H\left(\frac1{\infty^2}-1\right)\Rightarrow R_H=\frac1{913.4}A^{-1}{/tex}
For short limit of wavelength for Balmer series,
{tex}\frac1{\lambda_B}=R_H\left(\frac1{\infty^2}-\frac1{2^2}\right)=\frac{R_H}4{/tex}
So, {tex}\lambda_B=\frac4{R_H}=4\times913.4=365.36\mathrm{nm}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: State Bohr postulate of hydrogen atom that gives the relationship for the frequency of emitted photon in a transition.
question_text: State Bohr postulate of hydrogen atom that gives the relationship for the frequency of emitted photon in a transition.
explanation: Bohr’s third postulate states that an electron might make a transition from one of its specified non-radiating orbits to another of lower energy. When it does so, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states. The frequency of the emitted photon is then given by h {tex}\nu{/tex} = E i – E f where E i and E f are the energies of the initial and final states and E i > E f .
solution: Bohr’s third postulate states that an electron might make a transition from one of its specified non-radiating orbits to another of lower energy. When it does so, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states. The frequency of the emitted photon is then given by h {tex}\nu{/tex} = E i – E f where E i and E f are the energies of the initial and final states and E i > E f .
solution_html:
Bohr’s third postulate states that an electron might make a transition from one of its specified non-radiating orbits to another of lower energy. When it does so, a photon is emitted having energy equal to the energy difference between the initial and final states. The frequency of the emitted photon is then given by
h{tex}\nu{/tex} = Ei – Ef
where Ei and Ef are the energies of the initial and final states and Ei > Ef.

subject: Physics
question_html: Calculate the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron orbiting in the n = 2 state of hydrogen atom.
question_text: Calculate the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron orbiting in the n = 2 state of hydrogen atom.
explanation: Energy of electron at n = 2 is {tex}E = \frac { - 13.6 Z^2 } { n ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - 13.6 (1)^2 } { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } = - 3.4 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} we know that , {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex} {tex}= h / \sqrt { 2 m E }{/tex} [ K = E ] {tex}= \frac { 6.62 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { \sqrt { 2 \times 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 3.4 \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } }{/tex} {tex} = 0.0665 \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} {tex}=0.067 \;nm{/tex}
solution: Energy of electron at n = 2 is {tex}E = \frac { - 13.6 Z^2 } { n ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - 13.6 (1)^2 } { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } = - 3.4 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} we know that , {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex} {tex}= h / \sqrt { 2 m E }{/tex} [ K = E ] {tex}= \frac { 6.62 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { \sqrt { 2 \times 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 3.4 \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } }{/tex} {tex} = 0.0665 \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} {tex}=0.067 \;nm{/tex}
solution_html:
Energy of electron at n = 2 is 
{tex}E = \frac { - 13.6 Z^2 } { n ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - 13.6 (1)^2 } { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } = - 3.4 \mathrm { eV }{/tex}
we know that , 
{tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex}
{tex}= h / \sqrt { 2 m E }{/tex} [ K = E ]
{tex}= \frac { 6.62 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { \sqrt { 2 \times 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 3.4 \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } }{/tex}
{tex} = 0.0665 \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex}
{tex}=0.067 \;nm{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Use de-Broglie's hypothesis to write the relation for the nth radius of Bohr orbit in terms of Bohr's quantization condition of orbital angular momentum.
question_text: Use de-Broglie's hypothesis to write the relation for the nth radius of Bohr orbit in terms of Bohr's quantization condition of orbital angular momentum.
explanation: According to Bohr's postulates, {tex}m v r = \frac { n h } { 2 \pi }{/tex} .........(1) (where, mvr =angular momentum of an electron and n is an integer). Also, the centripetal force, mv 2 /r (experienced by the electron) is due to the electrostatic attraction, {tex}\frac { k Z e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} where, Z= atomic number of the atom. Therefore, {tex}\frac{mv^2}r=\frac{kZe^2}{r^2}{/tex} Substituting the value of v 2 from Eq. (i), we obtain {tex}\frac { m } { r } \cdot \frac { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { k Z e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \\ \therefore r = \frac { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m k Z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
solution: According to Bohr's postulates, {tex}m v r = \frac { n h } { 2 \pi }{/tex} .........(1) (where, mvr =angular momentum of an electron and n is an integer). Also, the centripetal force, mv 2 /r (experienced by the electron) is due to the electrostatic attraction, {tex}\frac { k Z e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} where, Z= atomic number of the atom. Therefore, {tex}\frac{mv^2}r=\frac{kZe^2}{r^2}{/tex} Substituting the value of v 2 from Eq. (i), we obtain {tex}\frac { m } { r } \cdot \frac { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { k Z e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \\ \therefore r = \frac { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m k Z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to Bohr's postulates,
{tex}m v r = \frac { n h } { 2 \pi }{/tex}.........(1) (where, mvr =angular momentum of an electron and n is an integer).
Also, the centripetal force, mv2 /r (experienced by the electron) is due to the electrostatic attraction,{tex}\frac { k Z e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
where, Z= atomic number of the atom.
Therefore, {tex}\frac{mv^2}r=\frac{kZe^2}{r^2}{/tex}
Substituting the value of v2 from Eq. (i), we obtain
{tex}\frac { m } { r } \cdot \frac { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { k Z e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \\ \therefore r = \frac { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m k Z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the ratio between the wavelengths of the most energetic spectral lines in the Balmer and Paschen series of the hydrogen spectrum.
question_text: Find the ratio between the wavelengths of the most energetic spectral lines in the Balmer and Paschen series of the hydrogen spectrum.
explanation: As per the Bohr's Model, the wavelengths are given by {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { n_1 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n_2 ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} For Balmer series, {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { B } } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} For highest energy n {tex}\to \infty{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { B } } = \frac { R } { 2 ^ { 2 } } = \frac { R } { 4 } \Rightarrow \lambda _ { B } = \frac { 4 } { R }{/tex} For Paschen series, {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { p } } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} For highest energy n {tex}\to \infty{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda _ { p } = \frac { 9 } { R } \Rightarrow \lambda _ { B } : \lambda _ { P } = \frac { 4 } { R } : \frac { 9 } { R } \Rightarrow 4 : 9{/tex}
solution: As per the Bohr's Model, the wavelengths are given by {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { n_1 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n_2 ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} For Balmer series, {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { B } } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} For highest energy n {tex}\to \infty{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { B } } = \frac { R } { 2 ^ { 2 } } = \frac { R } { 4 } \Rightarrow \lambda _ { B } = \frac { 4 } { R }{/tex} For Paschen series, {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { p } } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} For highest energy n {tex}\to \infty{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda _ { p } = \frac { 9 } { R } \Rightarrow \lambda _ { B } : \lambda _ { P } = \frac { 4 } { R } : \frac { 9 } { R } \Rightarrow 4 : 9{/tex}
solution_html:
As per the Bohr's Model, the wavelengths are given by
{tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { n_1 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n_2 ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
For Balmer series, {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { B } } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
For highest energy n {tex}\to \infty{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { B } } = \frac { R } { 2 ^ { 2 } } = \frac { R } { 4 } \Rightarrow \lambda _ { B } = \frac { 4 } { R }{/tex}
For Paschen series,{tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { p } } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
For highest energy n {tex}\to \infty{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda _ { p } = \frac { 9 } { R } \Rightarrow \lambda _ { B } : \lambda _ { P } = \frac { 4 } { R } : \frac { 9 } { R } \Rightarrow 4 : 9{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Calculate the shortest wavelength in the Balmer series of hydrogen atom. In which region (infrared, visible, ultraviolet) of hydrogen spectrum does this wavelength lie?
question_text: Calculate the shortest wavelength in the Balmer series of hydrogen atom. In which region (infrared, visible, ultraviolet) of hydrogen spectrum does this wavelength lie?
explanation: We know that for Balmer series, {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right) , n _ { 2 } = 3,4,5...{/tex} For shortest wavelength, the spectral series is given by n 1 = 2 and n 2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \infty ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R \times \frac { 1 } { 4 } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { \lambda } = \frac { R } { 4 } \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac { 4 } { R }{/tex} {tex}\lambda = \frac { 4 } { 1.097 \times 10 ^ { 7 } } \left[ \because R = 1.097 \times 10 ^ { 7 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 1 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = 3.64 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} This wavelength lies in visible region.
solution: We know that for Balmer series, {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right) , n _ { 2 } = 3,4,5...{/tex} For shortest wavelength, the spectral series is given by n 1 = 2 and n 2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \infty ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R \times \frac { 1 } { 4 } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { \lambda } = \frac { R } { 4 } \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac { 4 } { R }{/tex} {tex}\lambda = \frac { 4 } { 1.097 \times 10 ^ { 7 } } \left[ \because R = 1.097 \times 10 ^ { 7 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 1 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = 3.64 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} This wavelength lies in visible region.
solution_html:
We know that for Balmer series,
{tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right) , n _ { 2 } = 3,4,5...{/tex}
For shortest wavelength, the spectral series is given by n1 = 2 and n2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \infty ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R \times \frac { 1 } { 4 } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { \lambda } = \frac { R } { 4 } \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac { 4 } { R }{/tex}
{tex}\lambda = \frac { 4 } { 1.097 \times 10 ^ { 7 } } \left[ \because R = 1.097 \times 10 ^ { 7 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 1 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = 3.64 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
This wavelength lies in visible region.

subject: Physics
question_html: An electron jumps from fourth to first orbit in an atom. How many maximum number of spectral lines can be emitted by the atom? To which series these lines correspond?
question_text: An electron jumps from fourth to first orbit in an atom. How many maximum number of spectral lines can be emitted by the atom? To which series these lines correspond?
explanation: ​​​​​The maximum number of spectral lines obtained when electron on fourth orbital jumps to ground state can be found as: {tex}n_2-n_1=4-1=3{/tex} The electron can make a transition from 4→3, 4→2, 4→1 = 3 3→2, 3→1 = 2 2→1 = 1 Total Number of spectral lines = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 lines. These lines corresponds to Paschen series.
solution: ​​​​​The maximum number of spectral lines obtained when electron on fourth orbital jumps to ground state can be found as: {tex}n_2-n_1=4-1=3{/tex} The electron can make a transition from 4→3, 4→2, 4→1 = 3 3→2, 3→1 = 2 2→1 = 1 Total Number of spectral lines = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 lines. These lines corresponds to Paschen series.
solution_html:
​​​​​The maximum number of spectral lines obtained when electron on fourth orbital jumps to ground state can be found as:
{tex}n_2-n_1=4-1=3{/tex}
The electron can make a transition from 
4→3, 4→2, 4→1 = 3
3→2, 3→1 = 2
2→1 = 1
Total Number of spectral lines = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 lines.

These lines corresponds to Paschen series.

question_html: Calculate the shortest wavelength of the spectral lines emitted in the Balmer series. (Given Rydberg constant, R = 107 m-1)
question_text: Calculate the shortest wavelength of the spectral lines emitted in the Balmer series. (Given Rydberg constant, R = 10 7 m -1 )
explanation: from Rydberg's law ; {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right){/tex} For the shortest wavelength, {tex}n=\infty{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{R}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=\frac{4}{R}=4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
solution: from Rydberg's law ; {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right){/tex} For the shortest wavelength, {tex}n=\infty{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{R}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=\frac{4}{R}=4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
solution_html:
from Rydberg's law ;{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right){/tex}
For the shortest wavelength, {tex}n=\infty{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{R}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=\frac{4}{R}=4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
The electron, in a hydrogen atom, is in its second excited state.
Calculate the wavelength of the lines in the Lyman series, that can be emitted through the permissible transitions of this electron.
(Given the value of Rydberg constant, R = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 m-1)
question_text: The electron, in a hydrogen atom, is in its second excited state. Calculate the wavelength of the lines in the Lyman series, that can be emitted through the permissible transitions of this electron. (Given the value of Rydberg constant, R = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -1 )
explanation: For the second excited state, n = 3 Hence two possible transitions of the Lyman series : 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1 and 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1. Wavelength for transition 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1 , n f = 1, n i = 3 from the rydberg's formula, we get {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.1 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{9}\right){/tex} {tex}=1.1 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{8}{9}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{9}{8 \times 1.1 \times 10^{-7}}{/tex} = 1.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 = 102.3 nm For transition 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1, n f = 1, n i = 2 {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.1 \times 10^{7}\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda{/tex} = 121.2 nm
solution: For the second excited state, n = 3 Hence two possible transitions of the Lyman series : 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1 and 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1. Wavelength for transition 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1 , n f = 1, n i = 3 from the rydberg's formula, we get {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.1 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{9}\right){/tex} {tex}=1.1 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{8}{9}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{9}{8 \times 1.1 \times 10^{-7}}{/tex} = 1.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 = 102.3 nm For transition 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1, n f = 1, n i = 2 {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.1 \times 10^{7}\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda{/tex} = 121.2 nm
solution_html:
For the second excited state, n = 3
Hence two possible transitions of the Lyman series : 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1 and 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1.
Wavelength for transition 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1 , nf = 1, ni = 3

from the rydberg's formula, we get
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.1 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{9}\right){/tex}
{tex}=1.1 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{8}{9}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{9}{8 \times 1.1 \times 10^{-7}}{/tex}
= 1.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
= 102.3 nm
For transition 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1, nf = 1, ni = 2
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.1 \times 10^{7}\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda{/tex} = 121.2 nm

subject: Physics
question_html: The energy levels of a hypothetical atom are given below. Which of the shown transitions will result in the emission of photon of wavelength 275 nm?
question_text: The energy levels of a hypothetical atom are given below. Which of the shown transitions will result in the emission of photon of wavelength 275 nm?
explanation: Energy of a photon corresponding to a wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}E=h\nu=h \frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{275 \times 10^{-9}} \mathrm{J}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{275 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 3 \times 10^{2}}{275 \times 1.6}{/tex} Energy of photon is given by E= 4.5 eV The calculated energy of the photon matches with transition B.
solution: Energy of a photon corresponding to a wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}E=h\nu=h \frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{275 \times 10^{-9}} \mathrm{J}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{275 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 3 \times 10^{2}}{275 \times 1.6}{/tex} Energy of photon is given by E= 4.5 eV The calculated energy of the photon matches with transition B.
solution_html:
1. Energy of a photon corresponding to a wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}
    {tex}E=h\nu=h \frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{275 \times 10^{-9}} \mathrm{J}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{275 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 3 \times 10^{2}}{275 \times 1.6}{/tex}
    Energy of photon is given by E= 4.5 eV
  2. The calculated energy of the photon matches with transition B.

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the relation between the three wavelengths {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex}, {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_{3}{/tex} from the energy level diagram shown below.
question_text: Find the relation between the three wavelengths {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} , {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_{3}{/tex} from the energy level diagram shown below.
explanation: By Bohr's frequency condition, {tex}E_{C}-E_{B}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}, \quad E_{B}-E_{A}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} and {tex}E_{C}-E_{A}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{3}}{/tex} But E C - E A = (E C - E B ) + (E B - E A ) {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex}
solution: By Bohr's frequency condition, {tex}E_{C}-E_{B}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}, \quad E_{B}-E_{A}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} and {tex}E_{C}-E_{A}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{3}}{/tex} But E C - E A = (E C - E B ) + (E B - E A ) {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
By Bohr's frequency condition, {tex}E_{C}-E_{B}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}, \quad E_{B}-E_{A}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} and {tex}E_{C}-E_{A}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{3}}{/tex}
But EC - EA = (EC - EB) + (EB - EA)
{tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State Bohr's quantization condition for defining stationary orbits. How does de-Broglie hypothesis explain the stationary orbits?
  2. Find the relation between the three wavelengths {tex}\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_{3} {/tex} from the energy level diagram shown below:
question_text: State Bohr's quantization condition for defining stationary orbits. How does de-Broglie hypothesis explain the stationary orbits? Find the relation between the three wavelengths {tex}\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_{3} {/tex} from the energy level diagram shown below:
explanation: Only those orbits are stable for which the angular momentum, of revolving electron, is an integral multiple of h/2 {tex}\pi{/tex} . {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} i.e., angular momentum of orbiting electron is quantized. According to de-Broglie hypothesis Linear momentum {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} And for a circular orbit, L = r n p where 'r n ‘ is the radius of n th orbit {tex}=\frac{r_{n} h}{\lambda}{/tex} Also, {tex}\mathrm{L}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{n}h}{\lambda}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \pi r_{n}=n \lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Circumference of permitted orbits are integral multiples of the wave-length {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}E_{C}-E_{B}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}E_{B}-E_{A}=\frac{\hbar c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}E_{C}-E_{A}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{3}}{/tex} ...(iii) Adding (i) & (ii) {tex}E_{C}-E_{A}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} ...(iv) Using equation (iii) and (iv) {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex}
solution: Only those orbits are stable for which the angular momentum, of revolving electron, is an integral multiple of h/2 {tex}\pi{/tex} . {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} i.e., angular momentum of orbiting electron is quantized. According to de-Broglie hypothesis Linear momentum {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} And for a circular orbit, L = r n p where 'r n ‘ is the radius of n th orbit {tex}=\frac{r_{n} h}{\lambda}{/tex} Also, {tex}\mathrm{L}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{n}h}{\lambda}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \pi r_{n}=n \lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Circumference of permitted orbits are integral multiples of the wave-length {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}E_{C}-E_{B}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}E_{B}-E_{A}=\frac{\hbar c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}E_{C}-E_{A}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{3}}{/tex} ...(iii) Adding (i) & (ii) {tex}E_{C}-E_{A}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} ...(iv) Using equation (iii) and (iv) {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Only those orbits are stable for which the angular momentum, of revolving electron, is an integral multiple of h/2{tex}\pi{/tex}. {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} i.e., angular momentum of orbiting electron is quantized.
    According to de-Broglie hypothesis
    Linear momentum
    {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex}
    And for a circular orbit, L = rnp where 'rn‘ is the radius of nth orbit
    {tex}=\frac{r_{n} h}{\lambda}{/tex}
    Also, {tex}\mathrm{L}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{n}h}{\lambda}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \pi r_{n}=n \lambda{/tex}
     {tex}\therefore{/tex} Circumference of permitted orbits are integral multiples of the wave-length {tex}\lambda{/tex}
  2. {tex}E_{C}-E_{B}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} ...(i)
    {tex}E_{B}-E_{A}=\frac{\hbar c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} ...(ii)
    {tex}E_{C}-E_{A}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{3}}{/tex} ...(iii)
    Adding (i) & (ii) 
    {tex}E_{C}-E_{A}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} ...(iv)
    Using equation (iii) and (iv)
    {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Define ionization energy. How would the ionization energy change when electron in hydrogen atom is replaced by a particle of mas 200 times that of the electron but having the same charge?
question_text: Define ionization energy. How would the ionization energy change when electron in hydrogen atom is replaced by a particle of mas 200 times that of the electron but having the same charge?
explanation: Ionisation energy is defined as the energy required to free an electron from the ground state of an atom. {tex}\text { I.E. }=E_{\infty}-E_{1}=\frac{2 \pi^{2} m k^{2} e^{4}}{h^{3}} \Rightarrow \text { I.E. } \propto m{/tex} Thus the I.E. will become 200 times that of the electron.
solution: Ionisation energy is defined as the energy required to free an electron from the ground state of an atom. {tex}\text { I.E. }=E_{\infty}-E_{1}=\frac{2 \pi^{2} m k^{2} e^{4}}{h^{3}} \Rightarrow \text { I.E. } \propto m{/tex} Thus the I.E. will become 200 times that of the electron.
solution_html:
Ionisation energy is defined as the energy required to free an electron from the ground state of an atom.
{tex}\text { I.E. }=E_{\infty}-E_{1}=\frac{2 \pi^{2} m k^{2} e^{4}}{h^{3}} \Rightarrow \text { I.E. } \propto m{/tex}
Thus the I.E. will become 200 times that of the electron.

question_html: Calculate the shortest wavelength of Lyman series. Given Rydberg's constant, R =10967700 m-1.
question_text: Calculate the shortest wavelength of Lyman series. Given Rydberg's constant, R =10967700 m -1 .
explanation: For shortest wavelength of Lyman series, n 1 = 1, n 2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{s}}=R\left[\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=R{/tex} or {tex}\lambda_{s}=\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{10967700} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 911.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 m = 911.6 {tex}\dot{A}{/tex}
solution: For shortest wavelength of Lyman series, n 1 = 1, n 2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{s}}=R\left[\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=R{/tex} or {tex}\lambda_{s}=\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{10967700} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 911.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 m = 911.6 {tex}\dot{A}{/tex}
solution_html:
For shortest wavelength of Lyman series, n1 = 1, n2={tex}\infty{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{s}}=R\left[\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=R{/tex}
or {tex}\lambda_{s}=\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{10967700} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
= 911.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 m = 911.6 {tex}\dot{A}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: When is Ha line of the Balmer series in the emission spectrum of hydrogen atom obtained? Calculate the frequency of the photon emitted during this transition.
question_text: When is Ha line of the Balmer series in the emission spectrum of hydrogen atom obtained? Calculate the frequency of the photon emitted during this transition.
explanation: H {tex}\alpha{/tex} line of the Balmer series of H-atom isobtained when electron transition occurs from n 2 =3 to n 1 =2 level. {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right]=\frac{5}{36} R{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{c}{\lambda}=c \times \frac{5}{36} R{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \times 1.097 \times 10^{7}}{36}{/tex} Hz = 4.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz
solution: H {tex}\alpha{/tex} line of the Balmer series of H-atom isobtained when electron transition occurs from n 2 =3 to n 1 =2 level. {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right]=\frac{5}{36} R{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{c}{\lambda}=c \times \frac{5}{36} R{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \times 1.097 \times 10^{7}}{36}{/tex} Hz = 4.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz
solution_html:
H{tex}\alpha{/tex} line of the Balmer series of H-atom isobtained when electron transition occurs from n2 =3 to n1 =2 level.
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right]=\frac{5}{36} R{/tex}
{tex}v=\frac{c}{\lambda}=c \times \frac{5}{36} R{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \times 1.097 \times 10^{7}}{36}{/tex} Hz = 4.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz

subject: Physics
question_html: Use de-Broglie's hypothesis to write the relation for the radius of nth Bohr orbit in terms of Bohr's quantization condition of orbital angular momentum.
question_text: Use de-Broglie's hypothesis to write the relation for the radius of nth Bohr orbit in terms of Bohr's quantization condition of orbital angular momentum.
explanation: According to de-Broglie hypothesis, wavelength associated with an electron revolving in nth orbit is {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v_{n}}{/tex} A circular orbit can be a stationary energy state only if it contains integral number of {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad 2 \pi r_{n}=n \lambda_{r}{/tex} n = 1,2,3,..... or {tex}2 \pi r_{n}=\frac{n h}{m v_{n}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad r_{n}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v_{n}}{/tex}
solution: According to de-Broglie hypothesis, wavelength associated with an electron revolving in nth orbit is {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v_{n}}{/tex} A circular orbit can be a stationary energy state only if it contains integral number of {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad 2 \pi r_{n}=n \lambda_{r}{/tex} n = 1,2,3,..... or {tex}2 \pi r_{n}=\frac{n h}{m v_{n}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad r_{n}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v_{n}}{/tex}
solution_html:
According to de-Broglie hypothesis, wavelength associated with an electron revolving in nth orbit is {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v_{n}}{/tex}
A circular orbit can be a stationary energy state only if it contains integral number of {tex}\lambda{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad 2 \pi r_{n}=n \lambda_{r}{/tex} n = 1,2,3,..... or {tex}2 \pi r_{n}=\frac{n h}{m v_{n}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad r_{n}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v_{n}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Show that the radius of the orbit in hydrogen atom varies as n2, where n is the principal quantum number of the atom.
question_text: Show that the radius of the orbit in hydrogen atom varies as n 2 , where n is the principal quantum number of the atom.
explanation: According to the Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom, the angular momentum of revolving electron is given by {tex}mvr=\frac {nh}{2\pi}{/tex} ........(i) where, m = mass of the electron, v = velocity of the electron. r = radius of the orbit, h = Planck's constant and n = principal quantum number of the atom. If an electron of mass m and velocity v is moving in a circular orbit of radius r, then the centripetal force is given by F c = mv 2 /r........(ii) Also, if the charge on the nucleus is Ze, then the force of electrostatic attraction between the nucleus and the electron will provide the necessary centripetal force {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F c = F e {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { k e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \quad [ \therefore Z = 1 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = \frac { e ^ { 2 } \cdot k } { m v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ........(iii) {tex}r = \frac { k e ^ { 2 } 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } } { m \cdot n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [from eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = \frac { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } } { k e ^ { 2 } 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m } \Rightarrow r \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex}
solution: According to the Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom, the angular momentum of revolving electron is given by {tex}mvr=\frac {nh}{2\pi}{/tex} ........(i) where, m = mass of the electron, v = velocity of the electron. r = radius of the orbit, h = Planck's constant and n = principal quantum number of the atom. If an electron of mass m and velocity v is moving in a circular orbit of radius r, then the centripetal force is given by F c = mv 2 /r........(ii) Also, if the charge on the nucleus is Ze, then the force of electrostatic attraction between the nucleus and the electron will provide the necessary centripetal force {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F c = F e {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { k e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \quad [ \therefore Z = 1 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = \frac { e ^ { 2 } \cdot k } { m v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ........(iii) {tex}r = \frac { k e ^ { 2 } 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } } { m \cdot n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [from eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = \frac { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } } { k e ^ { 2 } 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m } \Rightarrow r \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom, the angular momentum of revolving electron is given by
{tex}mvr=\frac {nh}{2\pi}{/tex}........(i)
where, m = mass of the electron, v = velocity of the electron.
r = radius of the orbit, h = Planck's constant and n = principal quantum number of the atom.
If an electron of mass m and velocity v is moving in a circular orbit of radius r, then the centripetal force is given by
Fc = mv2/r........(ii)
Also, if the charge on the nucleus is Ze, then the force of electrostatic attraction between the nucleus and the electron will provide the necessary centripetal force
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Fc = Fe
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { k e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \quad [ \therefore Z = 1 ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = \frac { e ^ { 2 } \cdot k } { m v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ........(iii)
{tex}r = \frac { k e ^ { 2 } 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } } { m \cdot n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [from eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = \frac { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } } { k e ^ { 2 } 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m } \Rightarrow r \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: The kinetic energy of the electron orbiting in the first excited state of hydrogen atom is 3.4 eV. Determine the de-Broglie wavelength associated with it.
question_text: The kinetic energy of the electron orbiting in the first excited state of hydrogen atom is 3.4 eV. Determine the de-Broglie wavelength associated with it.
explanation: Kinetic energy of the electron in the first excited state is 3.4eV, i.e., {tex}\frac{p^2}{2 m}{/tex} = 3.4 eV = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 p 2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 p = {tex}\sqrt{18.2 \times 3.4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-50}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{99.008} \times 10^{-25}{/tex} = 9.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -25 de Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.95 \times 10^{-25}}{/tex} = 0.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 = 0.67 nm
solution: Kinetic energy of the electron in the first excited state is 3.4eV, i.e., {tex}\frac{p^2}{2 m}{/tex} = 3.4 eV = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 p 2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 p = {tex}\sqrt{18.2 \times 3.4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-50}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{99.008} \times 10^{-25}{/tex} = 9.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -25 de Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.95 \times 10^{-25}}{/tex} = 0.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 = 0.67 nm
solution_html:
Kinetic energy of the electron in the first excited state is 3.4eV, i.e.,
{tex}\frac{p^2}{2 m}{/tex} = 3.4 eV = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 
p2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex}10-31 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19
p = {tex}\sqrt{18.2 \times 3.4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-50}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{99.008} \times 10^{-25}{/tex} = 9.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-25
de Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.95 \times 10^{-25}}{/tex} = 0.67 {tex}\times{/tex}10-9 = 0.67 nm

subject: Physics
question_html:
The figure shows energy level diagram of hydrogen atom.

  1. Find out the transition which results in the emission of a photon of wavelength 496 nm.
  2. Which transition corresponds to the emission of radiation of maximum wavelength? Justify your answer.
question_text: The figure shows energy level diagram of hydrogen atom. Find out the transition which results in the emission of a photon of wavelength 496 nm. Which transition corresponds to the emission of radiation of maximum wavelength? Justify your answer.
explanation: According to the question, Given, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 496 nm = 496 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -9 m {tex}\because E = \frac { h c } { \lambda } = \frac { 6.62 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } } { 496 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } \mathrm { J }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 6.6 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } } { 496 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } = 2.5 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} This equals (nearly) the difference (E 4 - E 2 ). Hence, the required transition is (n = 4) to (n = 2) Energy of emitted photon is given by, {tex}E = \frac { h c } { \lambda } \therefore \lambda _ { \max } \propto \frac { 1 } { E _ { \mathrm { mm } } }{/tex} The transition n = 4 to n = 3 corresponds to emission of radiation of maximum wavelength. It is so because this transmission gives out the photon of least energy.
solution: According to the question, Given, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 496 nm = 496 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -9 m {tex}\because E = \frac { h c } { \lambda } = \frac { 6.62 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } } { 496 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } \mathrm { J }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 6.6 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } } { 496 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } = 2.5 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} This equals (nearly) the difference (E 4 - E 2 ). Hence, the required transition is (n = 4) to (n = 2) Energy of emitted photon is given by, {tex}E = \frac { h c } { \lambda } \therefore \lambda _ { \max } \propto \frac { 1 } { E _ { \mathrm { mm } } }{/tex} The transition n = 4 to n = 3 corresponds to emission of radiation of maximum wavelength. It is so because this transmission gives out the photon of least energy.
solution_html:
1. According to the question, 
    Given, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 496 nm = 496{tex} \times {/tex}10-9 m
    {tex}\because E = \frac { h c } { \lambda } = \frac { 6.62 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } } { 496 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } \mathrm { J }{/tex}{tex}= \frac { 6.6 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } } { 496 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } = 2.5 \mathrm { eV }{/tex}

    This equals (nearly) the difference (E4 - E2). Hence, the required transition is (n =  4) to (n  = 2)

  2. Energy of emitted photon is given by,
     {tex}E = \frac { h c } { \lambda } \therefore \lambda _ { \max } \propto \frac { 1 } { E _ { \mathrm { mm } } }{/tex}
    The transition n =  4 to n = 3 corresponds to emission of radiation of maximum wavelength. It is so because this transmission gives out the photon of least energy.

subject: Physics
question_html: In the study of Geiger-Marsden experiment on the scattering of particles by a thin foil of gold, draw the trajectory of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles in the Coulomb field of the target nucleus. Explain briefly how one gets the information on the size of the nucleus from this study.
question_text: In the study of Geiger-Marsden experiment on the scattering of particles by a thin foil of gold, draw the trajectory of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles in the Coulomb field of the target nucleus. Explain briefly how one gets the information on the size of the nucleus from this study.
explanation: The trajectory of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles in the Coulomb field of the target nucleus is shown below: From this experiment, the following is observed. Most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles pass straight through the gold foil. It means that they do not suffer any collision with gold atoms. About one {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle in every 8000 {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles deflect by more than 90°. As most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles go undeflected and only a few get deflected, this shows that most of the space in an atom is empty. Thus, with the help of these observations regarding the deflection of a-particles, the size of the nucleus was predicted. At the distance of head on approach, the entire kinetic energy of α-particle is converted into electrostatic potential energy. This distance of head on approach gives an upper limit of the size of nucleus (denoted by r 0 ) and is given by: {tex}E_k=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{(Ze)(2e)}{r_0}{/tex} {tex}r_0=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{2Ze^2}{E_k}{/tex} This is about {tex}10^{-14}\mathrm m{/tex} .
solution: The trajectory of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles in the Coulomb field of the target nucleus is shown below: From this experiment, the following is observed. Most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles pass straight through the gold foil. It means that they do not suffer any collision with gold atoms. About one {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle in every 8000 {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles deflect by more than 90°. As most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles go undeflected and only a few get deflected, this shows that most of the space in an atom is empty. Thus, with the help of these observations regarding the deflection of a-particles, the size of the nucleus was predicted. At the distance of head on approach, the entire kinetic energy of α-particle is converted into electrostatic potential energy. This distance of head on approach gives an upper limit of the size of nucleus (denoted by r 0 ) and is given by: {tex}E_k=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{(Ze)(2e)}{r_0}{/tex} {tex}r_0=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{2Ze^2}{E_k}{/tex} This is about {tex}10^{-14}\mathrm m{/tex} .

The trajectory of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles in the Coulomb field of the target nucleus is shown below:


From this experiment, the following is observed.

  1. Most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles pass straight through the gold foil. It means that they do not suffer any collision with gold atoms.
  2. About one {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle in every 8000 {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles deflect by more than 90°. As most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles go undeflected and only a few get deflected, this shows that most of the space in an atom is empty. Thus, with the help of these observations regarding the deflection of a-particles, the size of the nucleus was predicted.

At the distance of head on approach, the entire kinetic energy of α-particle is converted into electrostatic potential energy. This distance of head on approach gives an upper limit of the size of nucleus (denoted by r0 ) and is given by:
{tex}E_k=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{(Ze)(2e)}{r_0}{/tex}
{tex}r_0=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{2Ze^2}{E_k}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: In a Geiger-Marsden experiment, what is the distance of closest approach to the nucleus of a 7.7 MeV {tex}\alpha - {/tex}particle before it comes momentarily to rest and reverses its direction?
question_text: In a Geiger-Marsden experiment, what is the distance of closest approach to the nucleus of a 7.7 MeV {tex}\alpha - {/tex} particle before it comes momentarily to rest and reverses its direction?
explanation: The key idea here is that throughout the scattering process, the total mechanical energy of the system consisting of an α-particle and a gold nucleus is conserved. The system’s initial mechanical energy is E i , before the particle and nucleus interact, and it is equal to its mechanical energy E f when the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle momentarily stops. The initial energy E i is just the kinetic energy K of the incoming {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. The final energy E f is just the electric potential energy U of the system. The potential energy U can be calculated from Eq. Let d be the centre-to-centre distance between the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle and the gold nucleus when the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is at its stopping point. Then we can write the conservation of energy E i = E f as {tex}K=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(2 e)(Z e)}{d}=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}{/tex} Thus the distance of closest approach d is given by:- {tex}d=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} K}{/tex} The maximum kinetic energy found in α-particles of natural origin is 7.7 MeV or 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –12 J. Since {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 N m 2 /C 2 . Therefore with e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 C, Z = 79 we have, {tex}d=\frac{(2)\left(9.0 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)^{2} \mathrm{79}}{1.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{J}}{/tex} d (Au) = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –14 m = 30 fm. (1 fm (i.e. fermi) = 10 –15 m.) The radius of gold nucleus is, therefore, less than 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –14 m. This is not in very good agreement with the observed result as the actual radius of gold nucleus is 6 fm. The cause of discrepancy is that the distance of closest approach is considerably larger than the sum of the radii of the gold nucleus and the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. Thus, the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle reverses its motion without ever actually touching the gold nucleus.
solution: The key idea here is that throughout the scattering process, the total mechanical energy of the system consisting of an α-particle and a gold nucleus is conserved. The system’s initial mechanical energy is E i , before the particle and nucleus interact, and it is equal to its mechanical energy E f when the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle momentarily stops. The initial energy E i is just the kinetic energy K of the incoming {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. The final energy E f is just the electric potential energy U of the system. The potential energy U can be calculated from Eq. Let d be the centre-to-centre distance between the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle and the gold nucleus when the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is at its stopping point. Then we can write the conservation of energy E i = E f as {tex}K=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(2 e)(Z e)}{d}=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}{/tex} Thus the distance of closest approach d is given by:- {tex}d=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} K}{/tex} The maximum kinetic energy found in α-particles of natural origin is 7.7 MeV or 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –12 J. Since {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 N m 2 /C 2 . Therefore with e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 C, Z = 79 we have, {tex}d=\frac{(2)\left(9.0 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)^{2} \mathrm{79}}{1.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{J}}{/tex} d (Au) = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –14 m = 30 fm. (1 fm (i.e. fermi) = 10 –15 m.) The radius of gold nucleus is, therefore, less than 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –14 m. This is not in very good agreement with the observed result as the actual radius of gold nucleus is 6 fm. The cause of discrepancy is that the distance of closest approach is considerably larger than the sum of the radii of the gold nucleus and the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. Thus, the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle reverses its motion without ever actually touching the gold nucleus.
solution_html:
The key idea here is that throughout the scattering process, the total mechanical energy of the system consisting of an α-particle and a gold nucleus is conserved. The system’s initial mechanical energy is Ei, before the particle and nucleus interact, and it is equal to its mechanical energy Ef when the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle momentarily stops. The initial energy Ei is just the kinetic energy K of the incoming {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle. The final energy Ef is just the electric potential energy U of the system. The potential energy U can be calculated from Eq. Let d be the centre-to-centre distance between the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle and the gold nucleus when the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle is at its stopping point. Then we can write the conservation of energy Ei = Ef as
{tex}K=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(2 e)(Z e)}{d}=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}{/tex}
Thus the distance of closest approach d is given by:-
{tex}d=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} K}{/tex}
The maximum kinetic energy found in α-particles of natural origin is 7.7 MeV or 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–12 J. Since {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 N m2/C2. Therefore with e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–19 C, Z = 79 we have,
{tex}d=\frac{(2)\left(9.0 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)^{2} \mathrm{79}}{1.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{J}}{/tex}
d (Au) = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–14 m = 30 fm. (1 fm (i.e. fermi) = 10–15 m.)
The radius of gold nucleus is, therefore, less than 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–14 m. This is not in very good agreement with the observed result as the actual radius of gold nucleus is 6 fm. The cause of discrepancy is that the
distance of closest approach is considerably larger than the sum of the radii of the gold nucleus and the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle. Thus, the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle reverses its motion without ever actually touching the gold nucleus.

question_html: State Bohr's postulate of hydrogen atom which successfully explains the emission lines in the spectrum of hydrogen atom.
question_text: State Bohr's postulate of hydrogen atom which successfully explains the emission lines in the spectrum of hydrogen atom.
explanation: An atom emits radiation in the form of discrete energy photons only when an electron jumps from a higher to a lower orbit and the energy of the photon is equal to the difference in energy in the two orbits. Thus, h v =E 2 - E 1
solution: An atom emits radiation in the form of discrete energy photons only when an electron jumps from a higher to a lower orbit and the energy of the photon is equal to the difference in energy in the two orbits. Thus, h v =E 2 - E 1

subject: Physics
question_html: Given the ground state energy E0 = -13.6 eV and Bohr radius a0 = 0.53 {tex}{\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} }{/tex}. Find out how de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron orbiting in the ground state would change when it jumps into the first excited state
question_text: Given the ground state energy E 0 = -13.6 eV and Bohr radius a 0 = 0.53 {tex}{\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} }{/tex} . Find out how de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron orbiting in the ground state would change when it jumps into the first excited state
explanation: de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{E_{2}}{E_{1}}}{/tex} As {tex}E_{n} \propto \frac{1}{n^{2}}{/tex} , so for n = 2, {tex}E_{2}=\frac{E_{1}}{2^{2}}=\frac{E_{1}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \lambda_{2}=2 \lambda_{1}{/tex}
solution: de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{E_{2}}{E_{1}}}{/tex} As {tex}E_{n} \propto \frac{1}{n^{2}}{/tex} , so for n = 2, {tex}E_{2}=\frac{E_{1}}{2^{2}}=\frac{E_{1}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \lambda_{2}=2 \lambda_{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
de-Broglie wavelength,{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{E_{2}}{E_{1}}}{/tex}
As {tex}E_{n} \propto \frac{1}{n^{2}}{/tex}, so for n = 2,
{tex}E_{2}=\frac{E_{1}}{2^{2}}=\frac{E_{1}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \lambda_{2}=2 \lambda_{1}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: When an electron in hydrogen atom jumps from the third excited state to the ground state, how would the de-Broglie wavelength associate with the electron change? Justify your answer.
question_text: When an electron in hydrogen atom jumps from the third excited state to the ground state, how would the de-Broglie wavelength associate with the electron change? Justify your answer.
explanation: de Broglie wavelength associated with a moving charge particle having a KE 'K' can be given as {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} {tex}\left[K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{p^2}{2 m}\right]{/tex} ...(i) The kinetic energy of the electron in any orbit of hydrogen atom can be given as K = -E = {tex}-\left(\frac{13.6}{n^2} \mathrm{eV}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{13.6}{n^2}{/tex} eV ...(ii) Let K 1 and K 4 be the KE of the electron in ground state and third excited state, where n 1 = 1 shows ground state and n 2 = 4 shows third excited state. Using the concept of equation (i) & (ii), we have {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_4}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{K_4}{K_1}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{n_1^{\prime 2}}{n_2^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_4}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{1^2}{4^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_4}{4}{/tex} i.e., the wavelength in the ground state will decrease.
solution: de Broglie wavelength associated with a moving charge particle having a KE 'K' can be given as {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} {tex}\left[K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{p^2}{2 m}\right]{/tex} ...(i) The kinetic energy of the electron in any orbit of hydrogen atom can be given as K = -E = {tex}-\left(\frac{13.6}{n^2} \mathrm{eV}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{13.6}{n^2}{/tex} eV ...(ii) Let K 1 and K 4 be the KE of the electron in ground state and third excited state, where n 1 = 1 shows ground state and n 2 = 4 shows third excited state. Using the concept of equation (i) & (ii), we have {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_4}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{K_4}{K_1}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{n_1^{\prime 2}}{n_2^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_4}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{1^2}{4^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_4}{4}{/tex} i.e., the wavelength in the ground state will decrease.
solution_html:
de Broglie wavelength associated with a moving charge particle having a KE 'K' can be given as
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex}{tex}\left[K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{p^2}{2 m}\right]{/tex} ...(i)
The kinetic energy of the electron in any orbit of hydrogen atom can be given as
K = -E = {tex}-\left(\frac{13.6}{n^2} \mathrm{eV}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{13.6}{n^2}{/tex}eV ...(ii)
Let K1 and K4 be the KE of the electron in ground state and third excited state, where n1 = 1 shows ground state and n2 = 4 shows third excited state.
Using the concept of equation (i) & (ii), we have
{tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_4}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{K_4}{K_1}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{n_1^{\prime 2}}{n_2^2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_4}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{1^2}{4^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_4}{4}{/tex}
i.e., the wavelength in the ground state will decrease.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Write two important limitations of the Rutherford model which could not explain the observed features of atomic spectra. How were these explained in Bohr's model of a hydrogen atom? Use the Rydberg formula to calculate the wavelength of the Ha line. (Take R = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 m-1).
  2. Using Bohr's postulates, obtain the expression for the radius of the nth orbit in hydrogen atom.
question_text: Write two important limitations of the Rutherford model which could not explain the observed features of atomic spectra. How were these explained in Bohr's model of a hydrogen atom? Use the Rydberg formula to calculate the wavelength of the Ha line. (Take R = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -1 ). Using Bohr's postulates, obtain the expression for the radius of the n th orbit in hydrogen atom.
explanation: Electron moving in a circular orbit around the nucleus would get accelerated, therefore it would spiral into the nucleus, as it looses its energy. It must emit a continuous spectrum. According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, Electron in an atom can revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy. Energy is released/absorbed only, when an electron jumps from one stable orbit to another stable orbit. This results in a discrete spectrum {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.1 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right){/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 656.3 nm We know that when an electron revolves in a stable orbit, the centripetal force is provided electrostatic force of attraction acting on it due to a portion present in the nucleus. {tex}\therefore \frac{m v_n^2}{r_n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{e^2}{r_n^2} \Rightarrow v_n^2=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m r_n}{/tex} and from Bohr's quantum condition, we have {tex}m v_n \cdot r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} \text { or } v_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r_n}{/tex} Squaring (ii) and then equation it with (i), we get {tex}\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r_n^2}=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0, m \cdot r_n} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r_n=\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2} \times \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m}{e^2}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 h^2}{\pi m e^2} \cdot n^2{/tex}
solution: Electron moving in a circular orbit around the nucleus would get accelerated, therefore it would spiral into the nucleus, as it looses its energy. It must emit a continuous spectrum. According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, Electron in an atom can revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy. Energy is released/absorbed only, when an electron jumps from one stable orbit to another stable orbit. This results in a discrete spectrum {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.1 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right){/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 656.3 nm We know that when an electron revolves in a stable orbit, the centripetal force is provided electrostatic force of attraction acting on it due to a portion present in the nucleus. {tex}\therefore \frac{m v_n^2}{r_n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{e^2}{r_n^2} \Rightarrow v_n^2=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m r_n}{/tex} and from Bohr's quantum condition, we have {tex}m v_n \cdot r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} \text { or } v_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r_n}{/tex} Squaring (ii) and then equation it with (i), we get {tex}\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r_n^2}=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0, m \cdot r_n} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r_n=\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2} \times \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m}{e^2}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 h^2}{\pi m e^2} \cdot n^2{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Electron moving in a circular orbit around the nucleus would get accelerated, therefore it would spiral into the nucleus, as it looses its energy.
    2. It must emit a continuous spectrum.
      According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom,
      1. Electron in an atom can revolve in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy.
      2. Energy is released/absorbed only, when an electron jumps from one stable orbit to another stable orbit. This results in a discrete spectrum
        {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right){/tex}
        {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.1 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right){/tex}
        {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 656.3 nm
  2. We know that when an electron revolves in a stable orbit, the centripetal force is provided electrostatic force of attraction acting on it due to a portion present in the nucleus.
    {tex}\therefore \frac{m v_n^2}{r_n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{e^2}{r_n^2} \Rightarrow v_n^2=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m r_n}{/tex}
    and from Bohr's quantum condition, we have
    {tex}m v_n \cdot r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} \text { or } v_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r_n}{/tex}
    Squaring (ii) and then equation it with (i), we get
    {tex}\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r_n^2}=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0, m \cdot r_n} {/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow r_n=\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2} \times \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m}{e^2}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 h^2}{\pi m e^2} \cdot n^2{/tex}

question_html: In Rutherford scattering experiment, draw the trajectory traced by a-particles in the Coulomb field of target nucleus and explain how this led to estimate the size of the nucleus.
question_text: In Rutherford scattering experiment, draw the trajectory traced by a-particles in the Coulomb field of target nucleus and explain how this led to estimate the size of the nucleus.
explanation: The trajectory, traced by the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles in the Coulomb field of target nucleus, has the form shown below. The size of the nucleus was estimated by observing the distance (d) of closest approach, of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles. This distance is given by: {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{(Z e)(2 e)}{d}{/tex} = K where K = kinetic energy of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles when they are far away from the target nuclei.
solution: The trajectory, traced by the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles in the Coulomb field of target nucleus, has the form shown below. The size of the nucleus was estimated by observing the distance (d) of closest approach, of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles. This distance is given by: {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{(Z e)(2 e)}{d}{/tex} = K where K = kinetic energy of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles when they are far away from the target nuclei.
solution_html:
The trajectory, traced by the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles in the Coulomb field of target nucleus, has the form shown below.

The size of the nucleus was estimated by observing the distance (d) of closest approach, of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles. This distance is given by:
{tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{(Z e)(2 e)}{d}{/tex} = K
where K = kinetic energy of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles when they are far away from the target nuclei.

question_html:
Figure shows energy level diagram of hydrogen atom,

  1. Find out the transition which results in the emission of a photon of wavelength 496 nm.
  2. Which transition corresponds to the emission of radiation of maximum wavelength? Justify your answer.
question_text: Figure shows energy level diagram of hydrogen atom, Find out the transition which results in the emission of a photon of wavelength 496 nm. Which transition corresponds to the emission of radiation of maximum wavelength? Justify your answer.
explanation: The energy levels of a hydrogen atom are: E 1 = -13.6 eV, & E 2 = -3.4 eV E 3 = -1.51 eV, & E 4 = -0.85 eV Also, h = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 Js, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 Energy of a photon of wavelength 496 nm, {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{496 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV} \simeq 2.5 \mathrm{eV}{/tex} Now, E 4 - E 2 = -0.85 + 3.4 = 2.55 {tex}\simeq {/tex} E Hence, the transition from n = 4 to n = 2 level results in the emission of a photon of wavelength 496 nm. The transition n = 4 to n = 3 level corresponds to the emission of maximum wavelength because this transition gives out a photon of minimum energy
solution: The energy levels of a hydrogen atom are: E 1 = -13.6 eV, & E 2 = -3.4 eV E 3 = -1.51 eV, & E 4 = -0.85 eV Also, h = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 Js, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 Energy of a photon of wavelength 496 nm, {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{496 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV} \simeq 2.5 \mathrm{eV}{/tex} Now, E 4 - E 2 = -0.85 + 3.4 = 2.55 {tex}\simeq {/tex} E Hence, the transition from n = 4 to n = 2 level results in the emission of a photon of wavelength 496 nm. The transition n = 4 to n = 3 level corresponds to the emission of maximum wavelength because this transition gives out a photon of minimum energy
solution_html:
1. The energy levels of a hydrogen  atom are:
    E1  = -13.6 eV, & E2 = -3.4 eV
    E3 = -1.51 eV, & E4 = -0.85 eV
    Also, h = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1
    Energy of a photon of wavelength 496 nm,
    {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{496 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV} \simeq 2.5 \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
    Now,  E4 - E2 = -0.85 + 3.4 = 2.55 {tex}\simeq {/tex} E
    Hence, the transition from n = 4 to n = 2 level results in the emission of a photon of wavelength 496 nm.
  2. The transition n = 4 to n = 3 level corresponds to the emission of maximum wavelength because this transition gives out a photon of minimum energy

question_text: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. What are the kinetic and potential energies of the electron in this state?
explanation: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. Given, total ground state energy = -13.6 eV We know that, Kinetic energy = - Total Energy = - (-13.6 eV) = 13.6 eV and Potential energy = 2 (TE) = 2 {tex} \times {/tex} (-13.6 ) = - 27.2 eV

question_html: Using Bohr's postulates of the atomic model, derive the expression for the radius of « 111 electron orbit. Hence obtain the expression for Bohr's radius. Show graphically the (nature of) variation of the radius of the orbit with the principal quantum number n.
question_text: Using Bohr's postulates of the atomic model, derive the expression for the radius of « 111 electron orbit. Hence obtain the expression for Bohr's radius. Show graphically the (nature of) variation of the radius of the orbit with the principal quantum number n.
explanation: For a circular orbit of the electron, {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{k Z e \cdot e}{r^{2}}=\frac{k Z e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}m v^{2}=\frac{k Z e^{2}}{r}{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{k Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}{/tex} ..........(i) Using Bohr's quantization condition for angular momentum, {tex}L=m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} ............(ii) From (i) and (ii), {tex}\frac{k Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} or {tex}v=\frac{2 \pi k Z e^{2}}{n h}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m} \cdot \frac{n h}{2 \pi k Z e^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k Z e^{2}}{/tex} Bohr's radius, {tex}r_{0}=\frac{h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k e^{2}}{/tex} [n = 1, Z = 1] As {tex}r \propto n^{2}{/tex} the graph of r versus n is a parabola as shown in Fig.
solution: For a circular orbit of the electron, {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{k Z e \cdot e}{r^{2}}=\frac{k Z e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}m v^{2}=\frac{k Z e^{2}}{r}{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{k Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}{/tex} ..........(i) Using Bohr's quantization condition for angular momentum, {tex}L=m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} ............(ii) From (i) and (ii), {tex}\frac{k Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} or {tex}v=\frac{2 \pi k Z e^{2}}{n h}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m} \cdot \frac{n h}{2 \pi k Z e^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k Z e^{2}}{/tex} Bohr's radius, {tex}r_{0}=\frac{h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k e^{2}}{/tex} [n = 1, Z = 1] As {tex}r \propto n^{2}{/tex} the graph of r versus n is a parabola as shown in Fig.
solution_html:
For a circular orbit of the electron, {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{k Z e \cdot e}{r^{2}}=\frac{k Z e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}m v^{2}=\frac{k Z e^{2}}{r}{/tex}
or {tex}r=\frac{k Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}{/tex} ..........(i)
Using Bohr's quantization condition for angular momentum,
{tex}L=m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex}
or {tex}r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} ............(ii)
From (i) and (ii),
{tex}\frac{k Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex}
or {tex}v=\frac{2 \pi k Z e^{2}}{n h}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m} \cdot \frac{n h}{2 \pi k Z e^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k Z e^{2}}{/tex}
Bohr's radius, {tex}r_{0}=\frac{h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k e^{2}}{/tex} [n = 1, Z = 1]
As {tex}r \propto n^{2}{/tex} the graph of r versus n is a parabola as shown in Fig.

question_text: When a hydrogen atom is raised from the ground state to an excited state,

options.text: the P.E. decreases and K.E. increases
options.html: the P.E. decreases and K.E. increases

options.text: the P.E. increases and K.E. decreases
options.html: the P.E. increases and K.E. decreases
options.explanation: The potential and kinetic energies of the electron in an orbit of radius r of the hydrogen atom are given by {tex}E_p\;=\;-\frac1{4{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} ε}}_0}\cdot\frac{e^2}r{/tex} and {tex}E_k\;=\;\frac1{4{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} ε}}_0}\cdot\frac{e^2}{2r}{/tex} When the atom goes to excited state, r increases. As a result, potential energy increases (becomes less negative) and kinetic energy decreases.

correct_option_text: the P.E. increases and K.E. decreases
explanation: The potential and kinetic energies of the electron in an orbit of radius r of the hydrogen atom are given by {tex}E_p\;=\;-\frac1{4{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} ε}}_0}\cdot\frac{e^2}r{/tex} and {tex}E_k\;=\;\frac1{4{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} ε}}_0}\cdot\frac{e^2}{2r}{/tex} When the atom goes to excited state, r increases. As a result, potential energy increases (becomes less negative) and kinetic energy decreases.
