question_html:
1. In hydrogen atom, an electron undergoes transition from second excited state to the first excited state and then to the ground state. Identify the spectral series to which these transitions belong.
  2. Find out the ratio of the wavelengths of the emitted radiations in the two cases.
question_text: In hydrogen atom, an electron undergoes transition from second excited state to the first excited state and then to the ground state. Identify the spectral series to which these transitions belong. Find out the ratio of the wavelengths of the emitted radiations in the two cases.
explanation: When an electron undergoes transition from second excited state to the first excited state it is known as Balmer series and then to the ground state is known as Lyman series. The wavelength of the emitted radiations in the two cases are: For {tex}n _ { 2 } \stackrel { \lambda _ { L } } { \longrightarrow } n _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Delta E{/tex} {tex}= (-3.40 + 13.6) = 10.20 eV{/tex} {tex}\lambda _ { L } = \frac { 12.43 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } } { 10.2 } = 1.218 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m }=1281 \mathop A\limits_{}^o{/tex} For {tex}n _ { 3 } \stackrel { \lambda _ { B } } { \longrightarrow } n _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Delta E{/tex} {tex}= (-1.5 + 3.4) = 1.9 eV{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda _ { B } = \frac { 12.43 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } } { 1.9 } = 6.54 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m } = 6540\mathop A\limits_{}^o {/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \lambda _ { B} } { \lambda _ { L } } = \frac { 6540\mathop A\limits_{}^o } { 1281 \mathop A\limits_{}^o } = 5.10{/tex}
solution: When an electron undergoes transition from second excited state to the first excited state it is known as Balmer series and then to the ground state is known as Lyman series. The wavelength of the emitted radiations in the two cases are: For {tex}n _ { 2 } \stackrel { \lambda _ { L } } { \longrightarrow } n _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Delta E{/tex} {tex}= (-3.40 + 13.6) = 10.20 eV{/tex} {tex}\lambda _ { L } = \frac { 12.43 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } } { 10.2 } = 1.218 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m }=1281 \mathop A\limits_{}^o{/tex} For {tex}n _ { 3 } \stackrel { \lambda _ { B } } { \longrightarrow } n _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Delta E{/tex} {tex}= (-1.5 + 3.4) = 1.9 eV{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda _ { B } = \frac { 12.43 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } } { 1.9 } = 6.54 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m } = 6540\mathop A\limits_{}^o {/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \lambda _ { B} } { \lambda _ { L } } = \frac { 6540\mathop A\limits_{}^o } { 1281 \mathop A\limits_{}^o } = 5.10{/tex}
solution_html:
1. When an electron undergoes transition from second excited state to the first excited state it is known as Balmer series and then to the ground state is known as Lyman series.
  2. The wavelength of the emitted radiations in the two cases are: 
    For {tex}n _ { 2 } \stackrel { \lambda _ { L } } { \longrightarrow } n _ { 1 }{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta E{/tex} {tex}= (-3.40 + 13.6) = 10.20 eV{/tex}
     {tex}\lambda _ { L } = \frac { 12.43 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } } { 10.2 } = 1.218 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m }=1281 \mathop A\limits_{}^o{/tex}
    For {tex}n _ { 3 } \stackrel { \lambda _ { B } } { \longrightarrow } n _ { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta E{/tex} {tex}=  (-1.5 + 3.4) = 1.9 eV{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda _ { B } = \frac { 12.43 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } } { 1.9 } = 6.54 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m } = 6540\mathop A\limits_{}^o {/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \lambda _ { B} } { \lambda _ { L } } = \frac { 6540\mathop A\limits_{}^o } { 1281 \mathop A\limits_{}^o } = 5.10{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is - 13.6 eV. If an electron makes a transition from an energy level - 0.85 eV to - 1.51 eV, calculate the wavelength of the spectral line emitted. To which series of hydrogen spectrum does this wavelength belong?
question_text: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is - 13.6 eV. If an electron makes a transition from an energy level - 0.85 eV to - 1.51 eV, calculate the wavelength of the spectral line emitted. To which series of hydrogen spectrum does this wavelength belong?
explanation: According to Bohr's theory of hydrogen energy of photon released, E 2 - E 1 = h {tex}\nu{/tex} Given, E 1 = -1.51 eV, E 2 = - 0.85 eV Thus, E 2 - E 1 = -0.85 - (-1.51) = 1.51 - 0.85 = 0.66 eV So, the wavelength of emitted spectral line, {tex}\lambda = \frac { 1242 \mathrm { eV } - \mathrm { nm } } { \mathrm { E } ( \mathrm { in } \mathrm { eV } ) } = \frac { 1242 \mathrm { eV } - \mathrm { nm } } { 0.66 \mathrm { eV } } = 1.88 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} The wavelength belongs to Paschen series of hydrogen spectrum.
solution: According to Bohr's theory of hydrogen energy of photon released, E 2 - E 1 = h {tex}\nu{/tex} Given, E 1 = -1.51 eV, E 2 = - 0.85 eV Thus, E 2 - E 1 = -0.85 - (-1.51) = 1.51 - 0.85 = 0.66 eV So, the wavelength of emitted spectral line, {tex}\lambda = \frac { 1242 \mathrm { eV } - \mathrm { nm } } { \mathrm { E } ( \mathrm { in } \mathrm { eV } ) } = \frac { 1242 \mathrm { eV } - \mathrm { nm } } { 0.66 \mathrm { eV } } = 1.88 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} The wavelength belongs to Paschen series of hydrogen spectrum.
solution_html:
According to Bohr's theory of hydrogen energy of photon released,
E2 - E1 = h{tex}\nu{/tex}
Given, E1 = -1.51 eV, E2 = - 0.85 eV
Thus, E2 - E1 = -0.85 - (-1.51) = 1.51 - 0.85 = 0.66 eV
So, the wavelength of emitted spectral line, {tex}\lambda = \frac { 1242 \mathrm { eV } - \mathrm { nm } } { \mathrm { E } ( \mathrm { in } \mathrm { eV } ) } = \frac { 1242 \mathrm { eV } - \mathrm { nm } } { 0.66 \mathrm { eV } } = 1.88 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
The wavelength belongs to Paschen series of hydrogen spectrum.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Using Bohr's second postulate of quantisation of orbital angular momentum show that the circumference of the electron in the nth orbital state in hydrogen atom is n-times the de-Broglie wavelength associated with it.
  2. The electron in hydrogen atom is initially in the third excited state. What is the maximum number of spectral lines which can be emitted when it finally moves to the ground state?
question_text: Using Bohr's second postulate of quantisation of orbital angular momentum show that the circumference of the electron in the nth orbital state in hydrogen atom is n-times the de-Broglie wavelength associated with it. The electron in hydrogen atom is initially in the third excited state. What is the maximum number of spectral lines which can be emitted when it finally moves to the ground state?
explanation: Only those orbits are stable for which the angular momentum of revolving electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi}\right){/tex} where h is the planck's constant. According to Bohr's second postulate mvr n = n {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r n = {tex}\frac{n h}{m v}{/tex} But {tex}\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} (By de Broglie hypothesis) {tex}\therefore{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r n = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} For third excited state, n = 4 For ground state, n = 1 Hence possible transitions are n i = 4 to n f = 3, 2, 1 n i = 3 to n f = 2, 1 n i = 2 to n f = 1 Total number of transitions = 6 E C - E B = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_1}{/tex} ...(i) E B - E A = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_2}{/tex} ...(ii) E C - E A = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_3}{/tex} ...(iii) Adding (i) and (ii), we have E C - E A = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_1}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_2}{/tex} ...(iv) From (iii) and (iv), we have {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_1}+\frac{h c}{\lambda_2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}+\frac{1}{\lambda_2}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_3{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_1 \lambda_2}{\lambda_1+\lambda_2}{/tex}
solution: Only those orbits are stable for which the angular momentum of revolving electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi}\right){/tex} where h is the planck's constant. According to Bohr's second postulate mvr n = n {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r n = {tex}\frac{n h}{m v}{/tex} But {tex}\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} (By de Broglie hypothesis) {tex}\therefore{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r n = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} For third excited state, n = 4 For ground state, n = 1 Hence possible transitions are n i = 4 to n f = 3, 2, 1 n i = 3 to n f = 2, 1 n i = 2 to n f = 1 Total number of transitions = 6 E C - E B = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_1}{/tex} ...(i) E B - E A = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_2}{/tex} ...(ii) E C - E A = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_3}{/tex} ...(iii) Adding (i) and (ii), we have E C - E A = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_1}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_2}{/tex} ...(iv) From (iii) and (iv), we have {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_1}+\frac{h c}{\lambda_2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}+\frac{1}{\lambda_2}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_3{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_1 \lambda_2}{\lambda_1+\lambda_2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Only those orbits are stable for which the angular momentum of revolving electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi}\right){/tex} where h is the planck's constant.
    According to Bohr's second postulate
    mvrn = n {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rn = {tex}\frac{n h}{m v}{/tex}
    But {tex}\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} (By de Broglie hypothesis)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rn = n{tex}\lambda{/tex}

  2. For third excited state, n = 4
    For ground state, n = 1
    Hence possible transitions are
    ni=  4 to nf = 3, 2, 1
    ni = 3 to nf = 2, 1
    ni = 2 to nf = 1

    Total number of transitions = 6
    EC - EB = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_1}{/tex} ...(i)
    EB - EA = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_2}{/tex} ...(ii)
    EC - EA = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_3}{/tex} ...(iii)
    Adding (i) and (ii), we have
    EC - EA = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_1}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_2}{/tex} ...(iv)
    From (iii) and (iv), we have
    {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_1}+\frac{h c}{\lambda_2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}+\frac{1}{\lambda_2}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda_3{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_1 \lambda_2}{\lambda_1+\lambda_2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: In a Geiger-Marsden experiment, calculate the distance of the closest approach to the nucleus of Z = 80, when an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle of 8 MeV energy impinges on it before it comes to momentarily rest and reverses its direction. How will the distance of the closest approach be affected when the kinetic energy of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle is doubled?
question_text: In a Geiger-Marsden experiment, calculate the distance of the closest approach to the nucleus of Z = 80, when an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle of 8 MeV energy impinges on it before it comes to momentarily rest and reverses its direction. How will the distance of the closest approach be affected when the kinetic energy of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is doubled?
explanation: Given, Z = 80, alpha particle energy = K = 8 MeV = 8 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 6 eV = 8 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 6 {tex} \times {/tex} 1.6 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -19 J {tex}\because \text { Energy, } K = \frac { ( Z e ) ( 2 e ) } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r _ { 0 } }{/tex} Radius of closest approach, {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { 0 } = 2 Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } ( K ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { 0 } = \frac { 9 \times 10 ^ { 9 } \times 2 \times 80 \times \left( 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 8 \times 10 ^ { 6 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { 0 } = \frac { 9 \times 10 ^ { 9 } \times 2 \times 80 \times \left( 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { ^ { } 12.8 \times 10 ^ { - 13 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { 0 } = 2.88 \times 10 ^ { - 14 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} As, {tex}r _ { 0 } \propto ( \mathbf { l } / K ){/tex} If KE gets doubled, distance of closest approach reduces to half.
solution: Given, Z = 80, alpha particle energy = K = 8 MeV = 8 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 6 eV = 8 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 6 {tex} \times {/tex} 1.6 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -19 J {tex}\because \text { Energy, } K = \frac { ( Z e ) ( 2 e ) } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r _ { 0 } }{/tex} Radius of closest approach, {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { 0 } = 2 Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } ( K ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { 0 } = \frac { 9 \times 10 ^ { 9 } \times 2 \times 80 \times \left( 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 8 \times 10 ^ { 6 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { 0 } = \frac { 9 \times 10 ^ { 9 } \times 2 \times 80 \times \left( 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { ^ { } 12.8 \times 10 ^ { - 13 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { 0 } = 2.88 \times 10 ^ { - 14 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} As, {tex}r _ { 0 } \propto ( \mathbf { l } / K ){/tex} If KE gets doubled, distance of closest approach reduces to half.
solution_html:
Given, Z = 80,
alpha particle energy = K = 8 MeV = 8{tex}\times {/tex}106 eV = 8 {tex} \times {/tex} 106 {tex} \times {/tex} 1.6 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-19 J
{tex}\because \text { Energy, } K = \frac { ( Z e ) ( 2 e ) } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r _ { 0 } }{/tex}
Radius of closest approach,
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { 0 } = 2 Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } ( K ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { 0 } = \frac { 9 \times 10 ^ { 9 } \times 2 \times 80 \times \left( 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 8 \times 10 ^ { 6 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { 0 } = \frac { 9 \times 10 ^ { 9 } \times 2 \times 80 \times \left( 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { ^ { } 12.8 \times 10 ^ { - 13 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { 0 } = 2.88 \times 10 ^ { - 14 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
As, {tex}r _ { 0 } \propto ( \mathbf { l } / K ){/tex}
If KE gets doubled, distance of closest approach reduces to half.

question_text: In a Geiger-Marsden experiment, calculate the distance of closest approach to the nucleus of Z = 80, when an a-particle of 8 MeV energy impinges on it before it comes momentarily to rest and reverses its direction.

question_html: The electron in a given Bohr orbit has a total energy of - 1.5 eV. Calculate its (i) kinetic energy (ii) potential energy (iii) wavelength of radiation emitted, when this electron makes a transition to the ground state. [Given, energy in the ground state = - 13.6 eV]
question_text: The electron in a given Bohr orbit has a total energy of - 1.5 eV. Calculate its (i) kinetic energy (ii) potential energy (iii) wavelength of radiation emitted, when this electron makes a transition to the ground state. [Given, energy in the ground state = - 13.6 eV]
explanation: The kinetic energy, E K = -E = - (-1.5) = 1.5 eV The potential energy, E p = 2E = -1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} ​​​​​​ 2 = - 3 eV As a result of transition of electron from excited state to ground state, Energy of radiation = -1.5 - (- 13.6) = 12.1 eV [ {tex}\because{/tex} Ground state energy of H-atom = - 13.6 eV] Now, {tex}E = h \nu = h \frac { c } { \lambda }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad h c / \lambda = 12.1 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} = energy of radiation {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 1 } { \lambda } = \frac { 12.1 \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } { 6.62 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 1.025 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m } = 1025 \mathop A\limits^o {/tex}
solution: The kinetic energy, E K = -E = - (-1.5) = 1.5 eV The potential energy, E p = 2E = -1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} ​​​​​​ 2 = - 3 eV As a result of transition of electron from excited state to ground state, Energy of radiation = -1.5 - (- 13.6) = 12.1 eV [ {tex}\because{/tex} Ground state energy of H-atom = - 13.6 eV] Now, {tex}E = h \nu = h \frac { c } { \lambda }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad h c / \lambda = 12.1 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} = energy of radiation {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 1 } { \lambda } = \frac { 12.1 \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } { 6.62 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 1.025 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m } = 1025 \mathop A\limits^o {/tex}
solution_html:
1. The kinetic energy, EK = -E = - (-1.5) = 1.5 eV
  2. The potential energy, Ep = 2E = -1.5 {tex}\times{/tex}​​​​​​ 2 = - 3 eV
  3. As a result of transition of electron from excited state to ground state,
    Energy of radiation = -1.5 - (- 13.6) = 12.1 eV [ {tex}\because{/tex} Ground state energy of H-atom = - 13.6 eV]
    Now, {tex}E = h \nu = h \frac { c } { \lambda }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad h c / \lambda = 12.1 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} = energy of radiation
    {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 1 } { \lambda } = \frac { 12.1 \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } { 6.62 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 1.025 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m } = 1025 \mathop A\limits^o {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Show that Bohr's second postulate "The electron revolves around the nucleus only in certain fixed orbits without radiating energy" can be explained on the basis of de-Broglie hypothesis of wave nature of electron.
question_text: Show that Bohr's second postulate "The electron revolves around the nucleus only in certain fixed orbits without radiating energy" can be explained on the basis of de-Broglie hypothesis of wave nature of electron.
explanation: When an electron of mass m is confined to move on a line of length l with velocity v, the de-broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} associated with electron is {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{mv}=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} or {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}=\frac{h}{2\ l/n}=\frac{nh}{2\ l}{/tex} When electron revolves in a circular orbit of radius r; then 2 l = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}p=\frac{nh}{2 \ \pi \ r}{/tex} or {tex}p\times r=\frac{nh}{2 \ \pi }{/tex} i.e. angular momentum (p {tex}\times{/tex} r) of electron is integral multiple of h/2 {tex}\pi{/tex} . This is Bohr's quantisation condition of angular momentum.
solution: When an electron of mass m is confined to move on a line of length l with velocity v, the de-broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} associated with electron is {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{mv}=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} or {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}=\frac{h}{2\ l/n}=\frac{nh}{2\ l}{/tex} When electron revolves in a circular orbit of radius r; then 2 l = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}p=\frac{nh}{2 \ \pi \ r}{/tex} or {tex}p\times r=\frac{nh}{2 \ \pi }{/tex} i.e. angular momentum (p {tex}\times{/tex} r) of electron is integral multiple of h/2 {tex}\pi{/tex} . This is Bohr's quantisation condition of angular momentum.
solution_html:
When an electron of mass m is confined to move on a line of length l with velocity v, the de-broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} associated with electron is
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{mv}=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} or {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}=\frac{h}{2\ l/n}=\frac{nh}{2\ l}{/tex}
When electron revolves in a circular orbit of radius r; then 2 l = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}p=\frac{nh}{2 \ \pi \ r}{/tex} or {tex}p\times r=\frac{nh}{2 \ \pi }{/tex}
i.e. angular momentum (p {tex}\times{/tex} r) of electron is integral multiple of h/2{tex}\pi{/tex}. This is Bohr's quantisation condition of angular momentum.

question_html: Define ionisation energy. What is its value for a hydrogen atoms?
question_text: Define ionisation energy. What is its value for a hydrogen atoms?
explanation: The amount of energy required to remove the outermost electron from an isolated, gaseous atom in its ground state to form a cation is known as ionization energy. For hydrogen, it is 13.6 eV.
solution: The amount of energy required to remove the outermost electron from an isolated, gaseous atom in its ground state to form a cation is known as ionization energy. For hydrogen, it is 13.6 eV.
solution_html:
The amount of energy required to remove the outermost electron from an isolated, gaseous atom in its ground state to form a cation is known as ionization energy.
For hydrogen, it is 13.6 eV.

question_html: Using the postulates of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, obtain an expression for the frequency of radiation emitted when the atom makes a transition from the higher energy state with quantum number ni to the lower energy state with quantum number nf(nf <ni).
question_text: Using the postulates of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, obtain an expression for the frequency of radiation emitted when the atom makes a transition from the higher energy state with quantum number n i to the lower energy state with quantum number n f (n f <n i ).
explanation:
Let an electron revolves around the nucleus of hydrogen atom. The necessary centripetal force is provided by electrostatic force of attraction. {tex}	herefore \quad  rac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } =  rac { k e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow r =  rac { k e ^ { 2 } } { m v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .......(i) By Bohr's second postulates, {tex}mvr =  rac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex} where, n = 1, 2, 3.... {tex}r =  rac{nh}{2\pi mv}{/tex} ...(ii) On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex} rac { k e ^ { 2 } } { m v ^ { 2 } } =  rac { n h } { 2 \pi m v } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v =  rac { 2 \pi k e ^ { 2 } } { n h }{/tex} Substituting in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}r =  rac { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m k e ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(iii) Now, kinetic energy of electron {tex}	ext {KE} =  rac{1}{2 m v ^ { 2 }} {/tex} {tex}=  rac{k e ^ { 2 }}{2 r}{/tex} Also, potential energy, PE = {tex} rac{-ke^2}{2r}{/tex} Energy of electron in n th orbit, {tex}E _ { n } = -  rac { k e ^ { 2 } } { 2 r } {/tex} {tex}= -  rac { k e ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \cdot  rac { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m k e ^ { 2 } } { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}E _ { n } = -  rac { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } m k ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(iv) where, {tex}R =  rac { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } m k ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { c h ^ { 3 } } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E _ { n } = -  rac { R h c } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(v) where, n = 1, 2, 3 ... For {tex}n = n _ { i } \Rightarrow E _ { n } \propto  rac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}E _ { n _ { i } } = -  rac { R h c } { n _ { i } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex}E _ { n _ { f } } = -  rac { R h c } { n _ { f } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} By Bohr's postulates, {tex}E _ { n _ { f } } - E _ { n _ { t } } = h 
u {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow R h c \left[  rac{1}{n _ { i } ^ { 2 }} -  rac{1}{n _ { f } ^ { 2 }} ight] = h 
u{/tex} {tex}
u= R c \left[  rac{n}{n _ { i } ^ { 2 }} -  rac{1}{n _ { f } ^ { 2 }} ight]{/tex} This is required expression for frequency associated with photon.
solution:
Let an electron revolves around the nucleus of hydrogen atom. The necessary centripetal force is provided by electrostatic force of attraction. {tex}	herefore \quad  rac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } =  rac { k e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow r =  rac { k e ^ { 2 } } { m v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .......(i) By Bohr's second postulates, {tex}mvr =  rac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex} where, n = 1, 2, 3.... {tex}r =  rac{nh}{2\pi mv}{/tex} ...(ii) On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex} rac { k e ^ { 2 } } { m v ^ { 2 } } =  rac { n h } { 2 \pi m v } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v =  rac { 2 \pi k e ^ { 2 } } { n h }{/tex} Substituting in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}r =  rac { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m k e ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(iii) Now, kinetic energy of electron {tex}	ext {KE} =  rac{1}{2 m v ^ { 2 }} {/tex} {tex}=  rac{k e ^ { 2 }}{2 r}{/tex} Also, potential energy, PE = {tex} rac{-ke^2}{2r}{/tex} Energy of electron in n th orbit, {tex}E _ { n } = -  rac { k e ^ { 2 } } { 2 r } {/tex} {tex}= -  rac { k e ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \cdot  rac { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m k e ^ { 2 } } { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}E _ { n } = -  rac { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } m k ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(iv) where, {tex}R =  rac { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } m k ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { c h ^ { 3 } } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E _ { n } = -  rac { R h c } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(v) where, n = 1, 2, 3 ... For {tex}n = n _ { i } \Rightarrow E _ { n } \propto  rac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}E _ { n _ { i } } = -  rac { R h c } { n _ { i } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex}E _ { n _ { f } } = -  rac { R h c } { n _ { f } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} By Bohr's postulates, {tex}E _ { n _ { f } } - E _ { n _ { t } } = h 
u {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow R h c \left[  rac{1}{n _ { i } ^ { 2 }} -  rac{1}{n _ { f } ^ { 2 }} ight] = h 
u{/tex} {tex}
u= R c \left[  rac{n}{n _ { i } ^ { 2 }} -  rac{1}{n _ { f } ^ { 2 }} ight]{/tex} This is required expression for frequency associated with photon.
solution_html:
Let an electron revolves around the nucleus of hydrogen atom. The necessary centripetal force is provided by electrostatic force of attraction.

{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { k e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow r = \frac { k e ^ { 2 } } { m v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .......(i)
By Bohr's second postulates,
{tex}mvr = \frac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex} where, n = 1, 2, 3....
 {tex}r = \frac{nh}{2\pi mv}{/tex} ...(ii)
On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\frac { k e ^ { 2 } } { m v ^ { 2 } } = \frac { n h } { 2 \pi m v } {/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow v = \frac { 2 \pi k e ^ { 2 } } { n h }{/tex}
Substituting in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}r = \frac { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m k e ^ { 2 } }{/tex}...(iii)
Now, kinetic energy of electron
{tex}\text {KE} = \frac{1}{2 m v ^ { 2 }} {/tex}{tex}= \frac{k e ^ { 2 }}{2 r}{/tex}
Also, potential energy, PE = {tex}\frac{-ke^2}{2r}{/tex}
Energy of electron in nth orbit,
{tex}E _ { n } = - \frac { k e ^ { 2 } } { 2 r } {/tex}{tex}= - \frac { k e ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \cdot \frac { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } m k e ^ { 2 } } { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}E _ { n } = - \frac { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } m k ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { n ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(iv)
where, {tex}R = \frac { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } m k ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { c h ^ { 3 } } {/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow E _ { n } = - \frac { R h c } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(v)
where, n = 1, 2, 3 ...
For  {tex}n = n _ { i } \Rightarrow E _ { n } \propto \frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}E _ { n _ { i } } = - \frac { R h c } { n _ { i } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex}E _ { n _ { f } } = - \frac { R h c } { n _ { f } ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
By Bohr's postulates,
{tex}E _ { n _ { f } } - E _ { n _ { t } } = h \nu {/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow R h c \left[ \frac{1}{n _ { i } ^ { 2 }} - \frac{1}{n _ { f } ^ { 2 }} \right] = h \nu{/tex}
{tex}\nu= R c \left[ \frac{n}{n _ { i } ^ { 2 }} - \frac{1}{n _ { f } ^ { 2 }} \right]{/tex}
This is required expression for frequency associated with photon.

question_html: Using postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom, show that (i) radii of orbits increases as n2 and (ii) the total energy of electron increases as {tex} \frac{1}{n^2}{/tex}, where n is the principal quantum number of the atom.
question_text: Using postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom, show that (i) radii of orbits increases as n 2 and (ii) the total energy of electron increases as {tex} \frac{1}{n^2}{/tex} , where n is the principal quantum number of the atom.
explanation: In an nucleus we know that the centripetal force is provided by the electrostatic force of attraction, therefore, {tex}\frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { ( Z e ) \times e } { r ^ { 2 } } \text { or } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} ...................(i) From the first postulate of Bohr's atomic model, the angular momentum of the electron is {tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex} .............(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}r = n ^ { 2 } \frac { h ^ { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...........(iii) This is the equation for the radii of the permitted orbits. According to this equation, {tex}r _ { n } \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex} Force acting on electron due to nucleus is, {tex}F = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 }{/tex} The acceleration of electron = v 2 /r (towards the centre) From Newton's second law, {tex}F = m \left( v ^ { 2 } / r \right) \Rightarrow \frac { z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 } } = m \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { r } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m v ^ { 2 }{/tex} ................(i) From Bohr's quantisation rules, {tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex} ..........(ii) Substituting the value of r from Eq (i), we get {tex}m v \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( m v ^ { 2 } \right) } = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi } \Rightarrow v = Z e ^ { 2 } / 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } h n{/tex} ..........(iii) So, kinetic energy, {tex}K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ............(iv) Potential energy of the atom, U {tex}= - \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} ...........(v) Using value of r from (iii) in Eq. (v), we get {tex}U = \frac { - Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( \varepsilon _ { 0 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } / \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { - Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } m } { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} The total energy in n th energy level of hydrogen atom is given by: {tex}E = K + U = + \frac { m Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } - \frac { m Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } m } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} i.e total energy of electron varies as {tex}\frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .
solution: In an nucleus we know that the centripetal force is provided by the electrostatic force of attraction, therefore, {tex}\frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { ( Z e ) \times e } { r ^ { 2 } } \text { or } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} ...................(i) From the first postulate of Bohr's atomic model, the angular momentum of the electron is {tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex} .............(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}r = n ^ { 2 } \frac { h ^ { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...........(iii) This is the equation for the radii of the permitted orbits. According to this equation, {tex}r _ { n } \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex} Force acting on electron due to nucleus is, {tex}F = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 }{/tex} The acceleration of electron = v 2 /r (towards the centre) From Newton's second law, {tex}F = m \left( v ^ { 2 } / r \right) \Rightarrow \frac { z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 } } = m \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { r } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m v ^ { 2 }{/tex} ................(i) From Bohr's quantisation rules, {tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex} ..........(ii) Substituting the value of r from Eq (i), we get {tex}m v \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( m v ^ { 2 } \right) } = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi } \Rightarrow v = Z e ^ { 2 } / 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } h n{/tex} ..........(iii) So, kinetic energy, {tex}K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ............(iv) Potential energy of the atom, U {tex}= - \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} ...........(v) Using value of r from (iii) in Eq. (v), we get {tex}U = \frac { - Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( \varepsilon _ { 0 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } / \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { - Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } m } { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} The total energy in n th energy level of hydrogen atom is given by: {tex}E = K + U = + \frac { m Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } - \frac { m Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } m } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} i.e total energy of electron varies as {tex}\frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. In an nucleus we know that the centripetal force is provided by the electrostatic force of attraction, therefore, {tex}\frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { ( Z e ) \times e } { r ^ { 2 } } \text { or } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} ...................(i)
    From the first postulate of Bohr's atomic model, the angular momentum of the electron is
    {tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex}.............(ii)
    From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
    {tex}r = n ^ { 2 } \frac { h ^ { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...........(iii)
    This is the equation for the radii of the permitted orbits.
    According to this equation, {tex}r _ { n } \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex}
  2. Force acting on electron due to nucleus is, {tex}F = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    The acceleration of electron = v2/r (towards the centre)
    From Newton's second law,
    {tex}F = m \left( v ^ { 2 } / r \right) \Rightarrow \frac { z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 } } = m \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { r } \right){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m v ^ { 2 }{/tex}................(i)
    From Bohr's quantisation rules,
    {tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex}..........(ii)
    Substituting the value of r from Eq (i), we get
    {tex}m v \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( m v ^ { 2 } \right) } = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi } \Rightarrow v = Z e ^ { 2 } / 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } h n{/tex}..........(iii)
    So, kinetic energy, {tex}K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ............(iv)
    Potential energy of the atom, U {tex}= - \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} ...........(v)
    Using value of r from (iii) in Eq. (v), we get
    {tex}U = \frac { - Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( \varepsilon _ { 0 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } / \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { - Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } m } { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
    The total energy in nth energy level of hydrogen atom is given by:
    {tex}E = K + U = + \frac { m Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } - \frac { m Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } m } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
    i.e total energy of electron varies as {tex}\frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex}.

question_html: Using Bohr’s postulates derive the expression for the frequency of radiation emitted when electron in hydrogen atom undergoes transition from higher energy state (quantum number ni) to the lower state, (nf). When electron in hydrogen atom jumps from energy state ni = 4 to nf = 3, 2, 1, identify the spectral series to which the emission lines belong.
question_text: Using Bohr’s postulates derive the expression for the frequency of radiation emitted when electron in hydrogen atom undergoes transition from higher energy state (quantum number n i ) to the lower state, (n f ). When electron in hydrogen atom jumps from energy state n i = 4 to n f = 3, 2, 1, identify the spectral series to which the emission lines belong.
explanation:
According to Bohr’s frequency condition, if an electron jumps from an energy level E i to E f , then the frequency of the emitted radiation is given by {tex}h
u = E_i -E_f{/tex} ...(i) Let n i and n f be the corresponding orbits, then E i = {tex}- rac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{n_{i}^{2} h^{2}}{/tex} and E f = {tex}- rac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{n_{f}^{2} h^{2}}{/tex} Substituting in equation (i), we have {tex}h
u{/tex} = {tex}- rac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{n_{i}^{2} h^{2}}-\left(- rac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{n_{f}^{2} h^{2}}ight){/tex} = {tex} rac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{h^{2}}\left( rac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}- rac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}ight){/tex} Rewriting the above equation, we have {tex}
u{/tex} = {tex} rac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4}k^2}{h^{3}}\left( rac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}- rac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}ight){/tex} This gives the frequency of the emitted radiation. When n i = 4 and n f = 3, Paschen series When n i = 4 and n f = 2, Balmer series When n i = 4 and n f = 1, Lyman series
solution:
According to Bohr’s frequency condition, if an electron jumps from an energy level E i to E f , then the frequency of the emitted radiation is given by {tex}h
u = E_i -E_f{/tex} ...(i) Let n i and n f be the corresponding orbits, then E i = {tex}- rac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{n_{i}^{2} h^{2}}{/tex} and E f = {tex}- rac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{n_{f}^{2} h^{2}}{/tex} Substituting in equation (i), we have {tex}h
u{/tex} = {tex}- rac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{n_{i}^{2} h^{2}}-\left(- rac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{n_{f}^{2} h^{2}}ight){/tex} = {tex} rac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{h^{2}}\left( rac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}- rac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}ight){/tex} Rewriting the above equation, we have {tex}
u{/tex} = {tex} rac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4}k^2}{h^{3}}\left( rac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}- rac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}ight){/tex} This gives the frequency of the emitted radiation. When n i = 4 and n f = 3, Paschen series When n i = 4 and n f = 2, Balmer series When n i = 4 and n f = 1, Lyman series
solution_html:
According to Bohr’s frequency condition, if an electron jumps from an energy level Ei to Ef, then the frequency of the emitted radiation is given by
{tex}h\nu = E_i -E_f{/tex}...(i)
Let ni and nf be the corresponding orbits, then 
Ei = {tex}-\frac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{n_{i}^{2} h^{2}}{/tex}
and Ef = {tex}-\frac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{n_{f}^{2} h^{2}}{/tex}
Substituting in equation (i), we have 
{tex}h\nu{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{n_{i}^{2} h^{2}}-\left(-\frac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{n_{f}^{2} h^{2}}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4} k^{2}}{h^{2}}\left(\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}\right){/tex}
Rewriting the above equation, we have 
{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi^{2} m e^{4}k^2}{h^{3}}\left(\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}\right){/tex}
This gives the frequency of the emitted radiation.
When ni = 4 and nf = 3, Paschen series
When ni = 4 and nf = 2, Balmer series
When ni = 4 and nf = 1, Lyman series

question_html:
The ground state energy of hydrogen atoms is -13.6 eV.

  1. Which are the potential and kinetic energy of an electron in the third excited state?
  2. If the electron jumps to the ground state from the third excited state, calculate the frequency of the photon emitted.
question_text: The ground state energy of hydrogen atoms is -13.6 eV. Which are the potential and kinetic energy of an electron in the third excited state? If the electron jumps to the ground state from the third excited state, calculate the frequency of the photon emitted.
explanation: The third excited state corresponds to n = 4. Thus, energy of electron in the fourth orbit of the H-atom, E 4 = {tex}- \frac{{ 13.6}}{{n^2}}=- \frac{{ 13.6}}{{4^2}}{/tex} = -0.85 eV Now, {tex}E = - (K.E.) = \frac{1}{2}(P.E.){/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Potential energy of electron in third excited state = 2E 4 = 2(-0.85) = -1.7 eV And Kinetic energy of electron in third excited state = -(E 4 ) = -(-0.85) = 0.85 eV {tex}\Delta{/tex} E = E 4 - E 1 = -0.85 - (-13.6) = 12.75 eV Thus, frequency is, {tex}\nu = \frac{{\Delta E}}{h} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{{12.75 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}Hz{/tex} {tex} \approx 2.9 \times {10^{15}}Hz{/tex}
solution: The third excited state corresponds to n = 4. Thus, energy of electron in the fourth orbit of the H-atom, E 4 = {tex}- \frac{{ 13.6}}{{n^2}}=- \frac{{ 13.6}}{{4^2}}{/tex} = -0.85 eV Now, {tex}E = - (K.E.) = \frac{1}{2}(P.E.){/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Potential energy of electron in third excited state = 2E 4 = 2(-0.85) = -1.7 eV And Kinetic energy of electron in third excited state = -(E 4 ) = -(-0.85) = 0.85 eV {tex}\Delta{/tex} E = E 4 - E 1 = -0.85 - (-13.6) = 12.75 eV Thus, frequency is, {tex}\nu = \frac{{\Delta E}}{h} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{{12.75 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}Hz{/tex} {tex} \approx 2.9 \times {10^{15}}Hz{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The third excited state corresponds to n = 4.
    Thus, energy of electron in the fourth orbit of the H-atom,
    E4 = {tex}- \frac{{ 13.6}}{{n^2}}=- \frac{{ 13.6}}{{4^2}}{/tex} = -0.85 eV
    Now,
    {tex}E = - (K.E.) = \frac{1}{2}(P.E.){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Potential energy of electron in third excited state = 2E4 = 2(-0.85) = -1.7 eV
    And Kinetic energy of electron in third excited state = -(E4) = -(-0.85) = 0.85 eV
  2. {tex}\Delta{/tex}E = E4 - E1 = -0.85 - (-13.6) = 12.75 eV 
    Thus, frequency is,
    {tex}\nu = \frac{{\Delta E}}{h} {/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{12.75 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}Hz{/tex}
    {tex} \approx 2.9 \times {10^{15}}Hz{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: What is the significance of the negative energy of an electron in the orbit?
question_text: What is the significance of the negative energy of an electron in the orbit?
explanation: This signifies that the electron is bound to the nucleus. Due to electrostatic attraction between electron and nucleus, the P.E. is negative and is greater than K.E. of the electron. The total energy of the electron is negative. It cannot escape from the atom.
solution: This signifies that the electron is bound to the nucleus. Due to electrostatic attraction between electron and nucleus, the P.E. is negative and is greater than K.E. of the electron. The total energy of the electron is negative. It cannot escape from the atom.
solution_html: This signifies that the electron is bound to the nucleus. Due to electrostatic attraction between electron and nucleus, the P.E. is negative and is greater than K.E. of the electron. The total energy of the electron is negative. It cannot escape from the atom.

question_html: The wavelength of the first line of Lyman series of hydrogen atom is equal to that of the second line of Balmer series for a hydrogen like ion. The atomic number Z of hydrogen like ion is:
question_text: The wavelength of the first line of Lyman series of hydrogen atom is equal to that of the second line of Balmer series for a hydrogen like ion. The atomic number Z of hydrogen like ion is:

options.explanation: The first line of Lyman series of the hydrogen atom, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{L}}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]{/tex} The second line of Balmer series of hydrogen-like ion, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{B}}=R Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{L}=\lambda_{B} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{4} R=\frac{3}{16} R Z^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Z 2 = 4 or Z = 2

explanation: The first line of Lyman series of the hydrogen atom, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{L}}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]{/tex} The second line of Balmer series of hydrogen-like ion, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{B}}=R Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{L}=\lambda_{B} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{4} R=\frac{3}{16} R Z^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Z 2 = 4 or Z = 2

subject: Physics
question_html: Draw a schematic arrangement of the Geiger-Marsden experiment for studying a-particle scattering by a thin foil of gold. Describe briefly by drawing trajectories of the scattered {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles, how this study can be used to estimate the size of the nucleus?
question_text: Draw a schematic arrangement of the Geiger-Marsden experiment for studying a-particle scattering by a thin foil of gold. Describe briefly by drawing trajectories of the scattered {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles, how this study can be used to estimate the size of the nucleus?
explanation: The Schematic diagram of Geiger-Marsden experiment is shown below: The trajectories of the scattered {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles is: From this experiment, the following is observed. Most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles pass straight through the gold foil. It means that they do not suffer any collision with gold atoms. About one {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle in every 8000 {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles deflect by more than 90°. As most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles go undeflected and only a few get deflected, this shows that most of the space in an atom is empty. Thus, with the help of these observations regarding the deflection of a-particles, the size of the nucleus was predicted. At the distance of head on approach, the entire kinetic energy of α-particle is converted into electrostatic potential energy. This distance of head on approach gives an upper limit of the size of nucleus (denoted by r 0 ) and is given by: {tex}E_k=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{(Ze)(2e)}{r_0}{/tex} {tex}r_0=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{2Ze^2}{E_k}{/tex} This is about {tex}10^{-14}\mathrm m{/tex} .
solution: The Schematic diagram of Geiger-Marsden experiment is shown below: The trajectories of the scattered {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles is: From this experiment, the following is observed. Most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles pass straight through the gold foil. It means that they do not suffer any collision with gold atoms. About one {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle in every 8000 {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles deflect by more than 90°. As most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles go undeflected and only a few get deflected, this shows that most of the space in an atom is empty. Thus, with the help of these observations regarding the deflection of a-particles, the size of the nucleus was predicted. At the distance of head on approach, the entire kinetic energy of α-particle is converted into electrostatic potential energy. This distance of head on approach gives an upper limit of the size of nucleus (denoted by r 0 ) and is given by: {tex}E_k=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{(Ze)(2e)}{r_0}{/tex} {tex}r_0=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{2Ze^2}{E_k}{/tex} This is about {tex}10^{-14}\mathrm m{/tex} .
solution_html:
The Schematic diagram of Geiger-Marsden experiment is shown below:

The trajectories of the scattered {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles is:

From this experiment, the following is observed.

  1. Most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles pass straight through the gold foil. It means that they do not suffer any collision with gold atoms.
  2. About one {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle in every 8000 {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles deflect by more than 90°. As most of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles go undeflected and only a few get deflected, this shows that most of the space in an atom is empty. Thus, with the help of these observations regarding the deflection of a-particles, the size of the nucleus was predicted.

At the distance of head on approach, the entire kinetic energy of α-particle is converted into electrostatic potential energy. This distance of head on approach gives an upper limit of the size of nucleus (denoted by r0 ) and is given by:
{tex}E_k=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{(Ze)(2e)}{r_0}{/tex}
{tex}r_0=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{2Ze^2}{E_k}{/tex}
This is about {tex}10^{-14}\mathrm m{/tex}.

question_html:
Find the ratio of energies of photons produced due to transition of an electron of hydrogen atom from its

  1. second permitted energy level to the first permitted level and
  2. the highest permitted energy level to the first permitted level.
question_text: Find the ratio of energies of photons produced due to transition of an electron of hydrogen atom from its second permitted energy level to the first permitted level and the highest permitted energy level to the first permitted level.
explanation: The second permitted energy level to the first level = E 2 - E 1 = (-3.4 eV) - (-13.6 eV) = 10.2 eV The highest permitted energy level to the first permitted level = E {tex}\infty{/tex} - E 1 = 0 - (-13.6) = 13.6 eV Ratio of energies of photon = {tex}\frac{10.2}{13.6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac34{/tex}
solution: The second permitted energy level to the first level = E 2 - E 1 = (-3.4 eV) - (-13.6 eV) = 10.2 eV The highest permitted energy level to the first permitted level = E {tex}\infty{/tex} - E 1 = 0 - (-13.6) = 13.6 eV Ratio of energies of photon = {tex}\frac{10.2}{13.6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac34{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The second permitted energy level to the first level = E2 - E1 = (-3.4 eV) - (-13.6 eV) = 10.2 eV 
  2. The highest permitted energy level to the first permitted level = E{tex}\infty{/tex} - E1 = 0 - (-13.6) = 13.6 eV
    Ratio of energies of photon = {tex}\frac{10.2}{13.6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac34{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: What is the ratio of radii of the orbits corresponding to first excited state and the ground state, in a hydrogen atom?
question_text: What is the ratio of radii of the orbits corresponding to first excited state and the ground state, in a hydrogen atom?
explanation: For first excited state n = 2. Ground state occurs for n = 1, {tex}\because \quad r _ { n } = r _ { 0 } n ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad r \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because r _ { 0 } = 0.53 \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{r _ { 1 }}{r _ { 2 }} = \left(\frac{ n _ { 1 }}{n _ { 2 }} \right) ^ { 2 } = (\frac{ 2 }{1 }) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} r 1 : r 2 = 4 : 1 where, r 1 and r 2 are radii corresponding to first excited state and ground state of the atom, respectively.
solution: For first excited state n = 2. Ground state occurs for n = 1, {tex}\because \quad r _ { n } = r _ { 0 } n ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad r \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because r _ { 0 } = 0.53 \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{r _ { 1 }}{r _ { 2 }} = \left(\frac{ n _ { 1 }}{n _ { 2 }} \right) ^ { 2 } = (\frac{ 2 }{1 }) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} r 1 : r 2 = 4 : 1 where, r 1 and r 2 are radii corresponding to first excited state and ground state of the atom, respectively.
solution_html:
For first excited state n = 2.
Ground state occurs for n = 1,
{tex}\because \quad r _ { n } = r _ { 0 } n ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\because \quad r \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because r _ { 0 } = 0.53 \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{r _ { 1 }}{r _ { 2 }} = \left(\frac{ n _ { 1 }}{n _ { 2 }} \right) ^ { 2 } = (\frac{ 2 }{1 }) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} r1 : r2 = 4 : 1
where, r1 and r2 are radii corresponding to first excited state and ground state of the atom, respectively.

question_html: The radius of innermost electron orbit of a hydrogen atom is 5.3 {tex} \times {/tex}10-11 m. What is the radius of orbit in the second excited state?
question_text: The radius of innermost electron orbit of a hydrogen atom is 5.3 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -11 m. What is the radius of orbit in the second excited state?
explanation: The radius of atom whose principal quantum number is n is given by, r = n 2 r 0 where, r 0 = radius of innermost electron orbit for hydrogen atom and r 0 = 5.3 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -11 m For second excited state, n = 3 {tex}\therefore {/tex} r = 3 2 {tex} \times {/tex} r 0 = 9 {tex} \times {/tex} 5.3 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -11 r = 4.77 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -10 m
solution: The radius of atom whose principal quantum number is n is given by, r = n 2 r 0 where, r 0 = radius of innermost electron orbit for hydrogen atom and r 0 = 5.3 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -11 m For second excited state, n = 3 {tex}\therefore {/tex} r = 3 2 {tex} \times {/tex} r 0 = 9 {tex} \times {/tex} 5.3 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -11 r = 4.77 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -10 m
solution_html:
The radius of atom whose principal quantum number is n is given by, r = n2r0
where, r0 = radius of innermost electron orbit for hydrogen atom and  r0 = 5.3{tex} \times {/tex}10-11 m
For second excited state, n = 3
{tex}\therefore {/tex} r = 32 {tex} \times {/tex} r0 = 9 {tex} \times {/tex} 5.3 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-11
r = 4.77 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-10 m

subject: Physics
question_html: Explain, in brief, why Rutherford's model cannot account for the stability of an atom.
question_text: Explain, in brief, why Rutherford's model cannot account for the stability of an atom.
explanation: When an electron revolves around the nucleus radiates electromagnetic energy and hence radius of orbit of electron decreases gradually. Thus, electron will finally on spiral path of decreasing radius and at the end, it will fall into nucleus, but this does not happen. Also, according to it we should obtain radiation of all possible wavelength but in actual practice atomic spectrum is a line spectrum.Thus, Rutherford atomic model cannot account for stability of atom.
solution: When an electron revolves around the nucleus radiates electromagnetic energy and hence radius of orbit of electron decreases gradually. Thus, electron will finally on spiral path of decreasing radius and at the end, it will fall into nucleus, but this does not happen. Also, according to it we should obtain radiation of all possible wavelength but in actual practice atomic spectrum is a line spectrum.Thus, Rutherford atomic model cannot account for stability of atom.
solution_html: When an electron revolves around the nucleus radiates electromagnetic energy and hence radius of orbit of electron decreases gradually. Thus, electron will finally on spiral path of decreasing radius and at the end, it will fall into nucleus, but this does not happen. Also, according to it we should obtain radiation of all possible wavelength but in actual practice atomic spectrum is a line spectrum.Thus, Rutherford atomic model cannot account for stability of atom.

subject: Physics
question_html: Using the relevant Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the radius of the electron in the nth orbit of the electron in hydrogen atom.
question_text: Using the relevant Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the radius of the electron in the nth orbit of the electron in hydrogen atom.
explanation: A hydrogen like atom consists of a tiny positively charged nucleus and an electron revolving in a stable circular orbit around the nucleus As, the centripetal force is provided by the electrostatic force of attraction, we have {tex}\frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { ( Z e ) \times e } { r ^ { 2 } } \text { or } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} ............(i) From the first postulate of Bohr's atomic model, the angular momentum of the electron is {tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex} .............(ii) where, n (= 1, 2, 3, ....) is principal quantum number . From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}r = n ^ { 2 } \frac { h ^ { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...........(iii) This is the equation for the radii of the permitted orbits. According to this equation, {tex}r _ { n } \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex} Since, n = 1, 2. 3, ... it follows that the radii of the permitted orbits increase in the ratio 1: 4: 9: 16:....
solution: A hydrogen like atom consists of a tiny positively charged nucleus and an electron revolving in a stable circular orbit around the nucleus As, the centripetal force is provided by the electrostatic force of attraction, we have {tex}\frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { ( Z e ) \times e } { r ^ { 2 } } \text { or } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} ............(i) From the first postulate of Bohr's atomic model, the angular momentum of the electron is {tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex} .............(ii) where, n (= 1, 2, 3, ....) is principal quantum number . From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}r = n ^ { 2 } \frac { h ^ { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...........(iii) This is the equation for the radii of the permitted orbits. According to this equation, {tex}r _ { n } \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex} Since, n = 1, 2. 3, ... it follows that the radii of the permitted orbits increase in the ratio 1: 4: 9: 16:....
solution_html:
A hydrogen like atom consists of a tiny positively charged nucleus and an electron revolving in a stable circular orbit around the nucleus
As, the centripetal force is provided by the electrostatic force of attraction, we have
{tex}\frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { ( Z e ) \times e } { r ^ { 2 } } \text { or } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex}............(i)
From the first postulate of Bohr's atomic model, the angular momentum of the electron is
{tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex}.............(ii)
where, n (= 1, 2, 3, ....) is principal quantum number.
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}r = n ^ { 2 } \frac { h ^ { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...........(iii)
This is the equation for the radii of the permitted orbits.
According to this equation, {tex}r _ { n } \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Since, n = 1, 2. 3, ... it follows that the radii of the permitted orbits increase in the ratio 1: 4: 9: 16:....

question_html:
Using the relevant Bohr's postulates, derive the expressions for the 

  1. speed of the electron in the nth orbit
  2. radius of the nth orbit of the electron in hydrogen atom.
question_text: Using the relevant Bohr's postulates, derive the expressions for the speed of the electron in the nth orbit radius of the nth orbit of the electron in hydrogen atom.
explanation: From Bohr's first postulate, {tex}\frac{mv^2}r=\frac{kZe^2}{r^2}{/tex} where k = {tex}\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_o}{/tex} Thus, r = {tex}\frac{k Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}{/tex} From Bohr's postulate of angular momentum, r = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{k Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} or v = {tex}\frac{2 \pi k Z e^{2}}{n h}{/tex} This is the velocity of electron in Bohr's stationary orbit. Now, mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} or v = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{mv^2}r=\frac{kZe^2}{r^2}{/tex} Putting the value of v, we get {tex}\frac{m}{r} \frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m^{2} r^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{k Z e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} or r {tex}=\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k Z e^{2}}{/tex} This is the radii of Bohr's stationary orbit.
solution: From Bohr's first postulate, {tex}\frac{mv^2}r=\frac{kZe^2}{r^2}{/tex} where k = {tex}\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_o}{/tex} Thus, r = {tex}\frac{k Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}{/tex} From Bohr's postulate of angular momentum, r = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{k Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} or v = {tex}\frac{2 \pi k Z e^{2}}{n h}{/tex} This is the velocity of electron in Bohr's stationary orbit. Now, mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} or v = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{mv^2}r=\frac{kZe^2}{r^2}{/tex} Putting the value of v, we get {tex}\frac{m}{r} \frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m^{2} r^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{k Z e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} or r {tex}=\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k Z e^{2}}{/tex} This is the radii of Bohr's stationary orbit.

    From Bohr's postulate of angular momentum,
    r = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{k Z e^{2}}{m v^{2}}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v}{/tex} or v = {tex}\frac{2 \pi k Z e^{2}}{n h}{/tex}
    This is the velocity of electron in Bohr's stationary orbit.
     
  2. Now, mvr = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} or v = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r}{/tex}

    This is the radii of Bohr's stationary orbit.

subject: Physics
question_html:
State any two postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom. What is the maximum possible number of spectral lines observed when the hydrogen atom is in its second excited state? Justify your answer.
Calculate the ratio of the maximum and minimum wavelengths of the radiations emitted in this process.
question_text: State any two postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom. What is the maximum possible number of spectral lines observed when the hydrogen atom is in its second excited state? Justify your answer. Calculate the ratio of the maximum and minimum wavelengths of the radiations emitted in this process.
explanation: Two postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom are Every atom consists of small and massive central core, known as nucleus around which electron revolve. The necessary centripetal force is provided by electrostatic force of attraction between positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons. The electrons revolved around the nucleus in only those circular orbits which satisfy the quantum condition, that the angular momentum of electrons is equal to integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2\pi}{/tex} where, h is Planck's constant. Thus, for any stationary orbit, {tex}mvr=\frac{nh}{2\pi}{/tex} where, n = 1, 2, 3,... In second excited state i.e., n = 3, two spectral lines namely Lyman series and Balmer series can be obtained corresponding to transition of electron from n = 3 to n = 1 and n = 3 to n = 2, respectively. For Lyman series, n = 3 to n = 1, for minimum wavelength {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { \operatorname { min } } } = R \left[ \frac { 1 } { 1 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 3 ^ { 2 } } \right] = \frac { 8 R } { 9 }{/tex} ...........(i) For Balmer series (maximum wavelength), {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { \max } } = R \left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 3 ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left( \frac { 9 - 4 } { 36 } \right) R = \frac { 5 R } { 36 }{/tex} ..........(ii) On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { \max } } { \lambda _ { \operatorname { min } } } = \frac { 8 R / 9 } { 5 R / 36 } = \frac { 8 R } { 9 } \times \frac { 36 } { 5 R } = \frac { 32 } { 5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda _ { \max } : \lambda _ { \operatorname { min } } = 32 : 5{/tex}
solution: Two postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom are Every atom consists of small and massive central core, known as nucleus around which electron revolve. The necessary centripetal force is provided by electrostatic force of attraction between positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons. The electrons revolved around the nucleus in only those circular orbits which satisfy the quantum condition, that the angular momentum of electrons is equal to integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2\pi}{/tex} where, h is Planck's constant. Thus, for any stationary orbit, {tex}mvr=\frac{nh}{2\pi}{/tex} where, n = 1, 2, 3,... In second excited state i.e., n = 3, two spectral lines namely Lyman series and Balmer series can be obtained corresponding to transition of electron from n = 3 to n = 1 and n = 3 to n = 2, respectively. For Lyman series, n = 3 to n = 1, for minimum wavelength {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { \operatorname { min } } } = R \left[ \frac { 1 } { 1 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 3 ^ { 2 } } \right] = \frac { 8 R } { 9 }{/tex} ...........(i) For Balmer series (maximum wavelength), {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { \max } } = R \left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 3 ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left( \frac { 9 - 4 } { 36 } \right) R = \frac { 5 R } { 36 }{/tex} ..........(ii) On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { \max } } { \lambda _ { \operatorname { min } } } = \frac { 8 R / 9 } { 5 R / 36 } = \frac { 8 R } { 9 } \times \frac { 36 } { 5 R } = \frac { 32 } { 5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda _ { \max } : \lambda _ { \operatorname { min } } = 32 : 5{/tex}
solution_html:
Two postulates of Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom are

  1. Every atom consists of small and massive central core, known as nucleus around which electron revolve. The necessary centripetal force is provided by electrostatic force of attraction between positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons.
  2. The electrons revolved around the nucleus in only those circular orbits which satisfy the quantum condition, that the angular momentum of electrons is equal to integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2\pi}{/tex} where, h is Planck's constant. Thus, for any stationary orbit,
    {tex}mvr=\frac{nh}{2\pi}{/tex}
    where, n = 1, 2, 3,...

In second excited state i.e., n = 3, two spectral lines namely Lyman series and Balmer series can be obtained corresponding to transition of electron from n = 3 to n = 1 and n = 3 to n = 2, respectively.
For Lyman series, n = 3 to n = 1, for minimum wavelength
{tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { \operatorname { min } } } = R \left[ \frac { 1 } { 1 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 3 ^ { 2 } } \right] = \frac { 8 R } { 9 }{/tex}...........(i)
For Balmer series (maximum wavelength),
{tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { \max } } = R \left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 3 ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left( \frac { 9 - 4 } { 36 } \right) R = \frac { 5 R } { 36 }{/tex}..........(ii)
On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}\frac { \lambda _ { \max } } { \lambda _ { \operatorname { min } } } = \frac { 8 R / 9 } { 5 R / 36 } = \frac { 8 R } { 9 } \times \frac { 36 } { 5 R } = \frac { 32 } { 5 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda _ { \max } : \lambda _ { \operatorname { min } } = 32 : 5{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: State the basic assumption of the Rutherford model of the atom. Explain in brief why this model cannot account for the stability of an atom?
question_text: State the basic assumption of the Rutherford model of the atom. Explain in brief why this model cannot account for the stability of an atom?
explanation: Basic assumptions of Rutherford atomic model: Atom consists of small central core, called atomic nucleus in which whole mass and positive charge is assumed to be concentrated. Electrons revolves around the nucleus in various circular orbits and necessary centripetal force is provided by electrostatic force of attraction between positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons. When an electron revolves around the nucleus, then it radiates electromagnetic energy and hence, radius of orbit of electron decreases gradually. Thus, electron revolve on spiral path of decreasing radius and finally, it should fall into nucleus, but this does not happen. Thus, Rutherford atomic model cannot account for stability of atom.
solution: Basic assumptions of Rutherford atomic model: Atom consists of small central core, called atomic nucleus in which whole mass and positive charge is assumed to be concentrated. Electrons revolves around the nucleus in various circular orbits and necessary centripetal force is provided by electrostatic force of attraction between positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons. When an electron revolves around the nucleus, then it radiates electromagnetic energy and hence, radius of orbit of electron decreases gradually. Thus, electron revolve on spiral path of decreasing radius and finally, it should fall into nucleus, but this does not happen. Thus, Rutherford atomic model cannot account for stability of atom.
solution_html:
Basic assumptions of Rutherford atomic model:

  1. Atom consists of small central core, called atomic nucleus in which whole mass and positive charge is assumed to be concentrated.
  2. Electrons revolves around the nucleus in various circular orbits and necessary centripetal force is provided by electrostatic force of attraction between positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons.

When an electron revolves around the nucleus, then it radiates electromagnetic energy and hence, radius of orbit of electron decreases gradually. Thus, electron revolve on spiral path of decreasing radius and finally, it should fall into nucleus, but this does not happen. Thus, Rutherford atomic model cannot account for stability of atom.

question_html: Draw a schematic arrangement of the Geiger Marsden experiment. How did the scattering of a-particles by a thin foil of gold provide an important way to determine an upper limit on the size of the nucleus? Explain briefly.
question_text: Draw a schematic arrangement of the Geiger Marsden experiment. How did the scattering of a-particles by a thin foil of gold provide an important way to determine an upper limit on the size of the nucleus? Explain briefly.
explanation: Observations: Only a small fraction of number of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles rebound back. This shows that the number of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles undergoing head on collision is very small. The conclusion is that the entire positive charge of atom is concentrated in a small volume called the nucleus. At the distance of head on approach, the entire kinetic energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is converted into electrostatic potential energy. This distance of head on approach gives an upper limit of the size of nucleus (denoted by r 0 ) and is given by E k = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{(Z e)(2 e)}{r_0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r 0 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 Z e^2}{E_k}{/tex} This is about 10 -14 m.

This shows that the number of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles undergoing head on collision is very small. solution_html:
Observations:

  1. Only a small fraction of number of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles rebound back. This shows that the number of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles undergoing head on collision is very small. The conclusion is that the entire positive charge of atom is concentrated in a small volume called the nucleus. At the distance of head on approach, the entire kinetic energy of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle is converted into electrostatic potential energy. This distance of head on approach gives an upper limit of the size of nucleus (denoted by r0 ) and is given by

    Ek = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{(Z e)(2 e)}{r_0}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r0 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 Z e^2}{E_k}{/tex}
    This is about 10-14 m.

question_html: In the Rutherford scattering experiment, the distance of closest approach for an a-particle is d0 . If a-particle is replaced by a proton, then how much kinetic energy in comparison to {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle will be required to have the same distance of closest approach d0?
question_text: In the Rutherford scattering experiment, the distance of closest approach for an a-particle is d 0 . If a-particle is replaced by a proton, then how much kinetic energy in comparison to {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle will be required to have the same distance of closest approach d 0 ?
explanation: Distance of closest approach, {tex}d _ { 0 } = \frac { 2 k Z e ^ { 2 } } { K.E } = \frac { 4 k Z e ^ { 2 } } { m v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Kinetic energy {tex} \propto {/tex} Z (atomic number) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { K _ { \text { proton } } } { K _ { \alpha } } = \frac { Z _ { \text { proton } } } { Z _ { \alpha } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow K _ { \text { proton } } : K _ { \alpha } = 1 : 2{/tex} This means kinetic energy required by proton is half of the kinetic energy required by an alpha particle.
solution: Distance of closest approach, {tex}d _ { 0 } = \frac { 2 k Z e ^ { 2 } } { K.E } = \frac { 4 k Z e ^ { 2 } } { m v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Kinetic energy {tex} \propto {/tex} Z (atomic number) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { K _ { \text { proton } } } { K _ { \alpha } } = \frac { Z _ { \text { proton } } } { Z _ { \alpha } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow K _ { \text { proton } } : K _ { \alpha } = 1 : 2{/tex} This means kinetic energy required by proton is half of the kinetic energy required by an alpha particle.
solution_html:
Distance of closest approach,
{tex}d _ { 0 } = \frac { 2 k Z e ^ { 2 } } { K.E } = \frac { 4 k Z e ^ { 2 } } { m v ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Kinetic energy {tex} \propto {/tex} Z (atomic number)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { K _ { \text { proton } } } { K _ { \alpha } } = \frac { Z _ { \text { proton } } } { Z _ { \alpha } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow K _ { \text { proton } } : K _ { \alpha } = 1 : 2{/tex}
This means kinetic energy required by proton is half of the kinetic energy required by an alpha particle.

question_html:
The energy of the electron in the ground state of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV.

  1. What does the negative sign signify?
  2. How much energy is required to take an electron in this atom from the ground state to the first excited state?
question_text: The energy of the electron in the ground state of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. What does the negative sign signify? How much energy is required to take an electron in this atom from the ground state to the first excited state?
explanation: According to the question, The energy of the electron in the ground state of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. The negative sign imply that electrons are bound to the nucleus by means of electrostatic force of attraction. Energy of electron in nth orbit of hydrogen atom is given by, {tex}E _ { n } = - \frac { 13.6 } { n ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV }{/tex} For first excited state, n = 2 {tex}E _ { 2 } = - \frac { 13.6 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV } = - \frac { 13.6 } { 4 } = - 3.4 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} Energy required = 3.4 eV- (-13.6 eV) = 10.2 eV

Energy of electron in nth orbit of hydrogen atom is given by, {tex}E _ { n } = - \frac { 13.6 } { n ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV }{/tex} For first excited state, n = 2 {tex}E _ { 2 } = - \frac { 13.6 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV } = - \frac { 13.6 } { 4 } = - 3.4 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} Energy required = 3.4 eV- (-13.6 eV) = 10.2 eV
solution_html:
According to the question, 
The energy of the electron in the ground state of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV.

  1. The negative sign imply that electrons are bound to the nucleus by means of electrostatic force of attraction.
  2. Energy of electron in nth orbit of hydrogen atom is given by, {tex}E _ { n } = - \frac { 13.6 } { n ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV }{/tex}
    For first excited state, n = 2
    {tex}E _ { 2 } = - \frac { 13.6 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV } = - \frac { 13.6 } { 4 } = - 3.4 \mathrm { eV }{/tex}
    Energy required = 3.4 eV- (-13.6 eV) = 10.2 eV

question_html: The energy of the electron, in the ground state of hydrogen, is - 13.6 eV. question_text: The energy of the electron, in the ground state of hydrogen, is - 13.6 eV. Calculate the energy of the photon that would be emitted, if the electron were to make a transition corresponding to the emission of the first line of the (i) Lyman series (ii) Balmer series of the hydrogen spectrum.
explanation: Given: E 1 = - 13.6 eV Now, {tex}E _ { n } = - \frac { 13.6 } { n ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV } {/tex} For n = 2, {tex}E _ { 2 } = - \frac { 13.6 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV } {/tex} = -3.4 eV For n = 3, {tex} E _ { 3 } = - \frac { 13.6 } { 3 ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV } {/tex} = - 1.5 eV For Lyman series, E = E 2 - E 1 = (- 3.4)- (- 13.6) = 10.2 eV For Balmer series, E = E 3 - E 2 = (- 1.5)- (- 3.4) = 1.9 eV
solution: Given: E 1 = - 13.6 eV Now, {tex}E _ { n } = - \frac { 13.6 } { n ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV } {/tex} For n = 2, {tex}E _ { 2 } = - \frac { 13.6 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV } {/tex} = -3.4 eV For n = 3, {tex} E _ { 3 } = - \frac { 13.6 } { 3 ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV } {/tex} = - 1.5 eV For Lyman series, E = E 2 - E 1 = (- 3.4)- (- 13.6) = 10.2 eV For Balmer series, E = E 3 - E 2 = (- 1.5)- (- 3.4) = 1.9 eV
solution_html:
Given: E1 = - 13.6 eV
Now, 
{tex}E _ { n } = - \frac { 13.6 } { n ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV } {/tex}
For n = 2,
{tex}E _ { 2 } = - \frac { 13.6 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV } {/tex} = -3.4 eV
For n = 3,
{tex} E _ { 3 } = - \frac { 13.6 } { 3 ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { eV } {/tex} = - 1.5 eV 

  1. For Lyman series, E = E2 - E1 = (- 3.4)- (- 13.6) = 10.2 eV
  2. For Balmer series, E = E3 - E2 = (- 1.5)- (- 3.4) = 1.9 eV
