solution: According to Bohr's theory, only such orbits are permissible for the motion of an electron around the nucleus in which the angular momentum of an electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{{2\pi }}{/tex} . {tex}L=m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} n = 1, 2, 3, Here n is the principal quantum number.
solution_html:
According to Bohr's theory, only such orbits are permissible for the motion of an electron around the nucleus in which the angular momentum of an electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{{2\pi }}{/tex}.
{tex}L=m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} n = 1, 2, 3,
Here n is the principal quantum number.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Photons, with a continuous range of frequencies, are made to pass through a simple of rarefied hydrogen. The transitions, shown in Fig. 11.20, indicate three of the spectral absorption lines in the continuous spectrum.

  1. Identify the spectral series, of the hydrogen emission spectrum, to which each of these three lines correspond.
  2. Which of these lines corresponds to the absorption of radiation of maximum wavelength?
question_text: Photons, with a continuous range of frequencies, are made to pass through a simple of rarefied hydrogen. The transitions, shown in Fig. 11.20, indicate three of the spectral absorption lines in the continuous spectrum. Identify the spectral series, of the hydrogen emission spectrum, to which each of these three lines correspond. Which of these lines corresponds to the absorption of radiation of maximum wavelength?
explanation: Line I {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Lyman series. Line II {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Balmer series and Line III {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Paschen series Line III corresponds to absorption of photon of minimum energy and hence of maximum wavelength.
solution: Line I {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Lyman series. Line II {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Balmer series and Line III {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Paschen series Line III corresponds to absorption of photon of minimum energy and hence of maximum wavelength.
solution_html:
1. Line I {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Lyman series. Line II {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Balmer series and Line III {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Paschen series
  2. Line III corresponds to absorption of photon of minimum energy and hence of maximum wavelength.

subject: Physics
question_html:
The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6eV

  1. What is the potential energy of an electron in the 3rd excited state?
  2. If the electron jumps to the ground state from the 3rd excited state, calculate the wavelength of the photon emitted.
question_text: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6eV What is the potential energy of an electron in the 3 rd excited state? If the electron jumps to the ground state from the 3 rd excited state, calculate the wavelength of the photon emitted.
explanation: The energy of an electron in nth orbit is given by {tex}{E_n} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}eV{/tex} For 3 rd excite state, n = 4 {tex}\therefore {E_4} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{4^2}}} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{16}} = - 0.85eV{/tex} Required energy to jump electron to the ground state from the 3 rd excited state E = E 4 - E 1 {tex} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{4^2}}} - \left( { - \frac{{13.6}}{{{1^2}}}} \right){/tex} = - 0.85 + 13.6 = 12.75 eV {tex}\therefore {/tex} Wavelength of the photon emitted as {tex}\lambda = \frac{{hc}}{E}\left( {As,\;E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{12.75 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{19.878 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{20.4}} = 0.974 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} {tex} = 974\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}
solution: The energy of an electron in nth orbit is given by {tex}{E_n} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}eV{/tex} For 3 rd excite state, n = 4 {tex}\therefore {E_4} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{4^2}}} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{16}} = - 0.85eV{/tex} Required energy to jump electron to the ground state from the 3 rd excited state E = E 4 - E 1 {tex} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{4^2}}} - \left( { - \frac{{13.6}}{{{1^2}}}} \right){/tex} = - 0.85 + 13.6 = 12.75 eV {tex}\therefore {/tex} Wavelength of the photon emitted as {tex}\lambda = \frac{{hc}}{E}\left( {As,\;E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{12.75 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{19.878 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{20.4}} = 0.974 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} {tex} = 974\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}
solution_html:
The energy of an electron in nth orbit is given by
{tex}{E_n} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}eV{/tex}

  1. For 3 rd excite state, n = 4
    {tex}\therefore {E_4} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{4^2}}} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{16}} = - 0.85eV{/tex}
  2. Required energy to jump electron to the ground state from the 3 rd excited state
    E = E4 - E1
    {tex} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{4^2}}} - \left( { - \frac{{13.6}}{{{1^2}}}} \right){/tex}
    = - 0.85 + 13.6 = 12.75 eV
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Wavelength of the photon emitted as
    {tex}\lambda = \frac{{hc}}{E}\left( {As,\;E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }} \right){/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{12.75 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{19.878 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{20.4}} = 0.974 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 974\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: In a hydrogen atom, if the electron is replaced by {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle that is 200 times heavier but has the same charge, how would its radius change?
question_text: In a hydrogen atom, if the electron is replaced by {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle that is 200 times heavier but has the same charge, how would its radius change?
explanation: Radius, {tex}r \propto \frac{1}{m}{/tex} When the electron is replaced by a 200 times heavier particle, the radius decreases to {tex}\frac{1}{200}{/tex} of the original radius.
solution: Radius, {tex}r \propto \frac{1}{m}{/tex} When the electron is replaced by a 200 times heavier particle, the radius decreases to {tex}\frac{1}{200}{/tex} of the original radius.
solution_html:
Radius, {tex}r \propto \frac{1}{m}{/tex}
When the electron is replaced by a 200 times heavier particle, the radius decreases to {tex}\frac{1}{200}{/tex}of the original radius.

subject: Physics
question_html: The total energy of the electron in the ground state of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. The kinetic energy of the electron in the first excited state will be
question_text: The total energy of the electron in the ground state of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. The kinetic energy of the electron in the first excited state will be

options.explanation: Kinetic energy of electron = {tex}\large\frac{kze^2}{2r}{/tex} Potential energy of electron = {tex}\large\frac{kze^2}{r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P.E= -2(K.E){/tex} Total energy = P.E +K.E = {tex}-2K.E+K.E=-K.E{/tex} Total energy in the first excited state {tex}=-3.4\;ev{/tex} {tex}KE= -(-3.4 ev)=3.4eV{/tex}

explanation: Kinetic energy of electron = {tex}\large\frac{kze^2}{2r}{/tex} Potential energy of electron = {tex}\large\frac{kze^2}{r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P.E= -2(K.E){/tex} Total energy = P.E +K.E = {tex}-2K.E+K.E=-K.E{/tex} Total energy in the first excited state {tex}=-3.4\;ev{/tex} {tex}KE= -(-3.4 ev)=3.4eV{/tex}
